tEn: Naval on Wealth: From Assets to Knowledge
tZh: 纳瓦尔论财富：从资产到知识
sEn: Naval Ravikant redefines wealth beyond assets that earn while you sleep, embracing David Deutsch's view of wealth as the set of physical transformations you can effect, driven by knowledge.
sZh: 纳瓦尔·拉维坎特重新定义财富，超越睡后收入资产，采纳大卫·多伊奇的观点：财富是你能影响的物理变换集合，由知识驱动。

consensus.en: Wealth is the set of physical transformations you can effect, driven by knowledge, not capital.
consensus.zh: 财富是你能够实现的物理转变的集合，由知识驱动，而非资本。

consensus.en: Good products are hard to vary and have surprising reach, like the iPhone.
consensus.zh: 好的产品难以改变且具有惊人的延伸性，例如 iPhone。

consensus.en: Judgment is the most important skill in an age of infinite leverage, built from experience and reflection.
consensus.zh: 在无限杠杆的时代，判断力是最重要的技能，通过经验和反思建立。

consensus.en: Happiness is a personal skill; pursue truth, love, and beauty after securing health and wealth.
consensus.zh: 幸福是一种个人技能；在确保健康和财富后，追求真理、爱和美好。

consensus.en: AI is a leverage tool for structured thinkers, not a replacement for human judgment.
consensus.zh: AI 是结构化思考者的杠杆工具，而非人类判断力的替代品。

contrarian.en: Marx was wrong: value lies in knowledge, not capital; removing Elon Musk from SpaceX destroys its wealth.
contrarian.zh: 马克思错了：价值在于知识而非资本；将埃隆·马斯克从 SpaceX 移除会摧毁其财富。

contrarian.en: AI alignment is a joke; we can't even align humans, and it often boils down to coercion.
contrarian.zh: AI 对齐是个笑话；我们甚至无法对齐人类，它往往归结为胁迫。

contrarian.en: Depression can be a learned choice and habit, not purely a chemical imbalance.
contrarian.zh: 抑郁可能是一种习得的选择和习惯，而非纯粹的化学失衡。

contrarian.en: The biggest impediment to progress is the size of institutions, not ethics or regulation.
contrarian.zh: 进步的最大障碍是机构的规模，而非伦理或监管。

contrarian.en: It's better to be in love than to be loved; giving love brings more fulfillment.
contrarian.zh: 去爱比被爱更好；付出爱带来更多满足感。

turns.en: Hello and welcome. It's been 5 years since The Almanac of Naval first came out. And to honor that, Naval and I wanted to do a special edition for you. We spent the day together and recorded over 4 hours of our conversation. Now, we're sharing that here with you, updating and expanding the key ideas in the book. Like everything else in the Almanac of Naval, this version is being made available for free in addition to the audio book available on your favorite platform. This book has grown as a word-of-mouth phenomenon, being gifted and recommended, ultimately reaching millions of people all over the world in 40 languages. I hope you will help these good ideas continue to spread. Thank you for joining us. Let's dive in. Over the last few years, seeing you go deeper and deeper into David Deutsch's ideas and at least from my seat, see the explanations that he puts forth and the connecting threads that you draw into your sort of existing theories about building wealth and how the definition of wealth evolves and expands and becomes universal and applies at the civilization level in the same rules all the way down to the decisions that you make as an individual.
turns.zh: 大家好，欢迎收听。距离《纳瓦尔宝典》首次出版已经过去了 5 年。为了纪念这个时刻，纳瓦尔和我决定为大家制作一个特别版。我们花了一整天时间，录制了超过 4 小时的对话。现在，我们在这里与你分享，更新并扩展了书中的核心思想。和《纳瓦尔宝典》中的其他内容一样，这个版本除了在你喜欢的平台上提供的音频书之外，也是免费提供的。这本书通过口口相传而成长，被赠送和推荐，最终以 40 种语言传播到全球数百万人。我希望你能帮助这些好想法继续传播。谢谢你的加入。让我们开始吧。过去几年，我看到你越来越深入地研究 David Deutsch 的思想，至少从我这里看，你把他提出的解释和你关于创造财富的现有理论联系起来，财富的定义如何演变、扩展并变得普遍，既适用于文明层面，也同样适用于你作为个人所做的决定。

turns.en: Deutsch had a better definition of wealth than I did. My definition of wealth was very focused by my desire to make money. And so my definition of wealth was assets that earn while you sleep. And so I wanted to break out of the 9-to-5 trap of the input tied to output trap. And I didn't want to have to work hard. I always used to say I want to be the most successful guy for doing the least work in every aspect of my life. Not just in business, but even my personal relationships, even my day-to-day happiness, even my exercise and work. I'm like all smart people, I'm lazy, right?
turns.zh: Deutsch 对财富的定义比我的更好。我对财富的定义非常聚焦于赚钱的欲望。所以我的定义是：财富是你在睡觉时也能赚钱的资产。我想摆脱朝九晚五的陷阱，摆脱投入与产出挂钩的陷阱。我不想辛苦工作。我以前常说，我想成为生活中每个方面做事最少却最成功的人。不仅仅是商业，甚至包括我的人际关系、日常幸福、锻炼和工作。我和所有聪明人一样，很懒，对吧？

turns.en: What's the highest leverage solution?
turns.zh: 最高杠杆的解决方案是什么？

turns.en: That's right. Laziness is a form of leverage, efficiency, what have you. And it's not lazy in the sense that I want to sit around and do nothing. It's just that then I have time to do other things. I can focus on my health. I can spend time with my children. I can educate myself. I don't just have to be a drone working away. And so my definition of wealth was around, okay, let's break out of the 9-to-5 trap. Let's have my assets work while I sleep. And what are those assets that can work while I sleep? Well, capital is an obvious one. You invest money, but what is that capital actually doing? When you quote unquote invest capital, what you're doing is you're giving up the right to future assets that society owed you for having done work in the past, assuming it was all properly earned. And then you're giving that up so that businesses can employ people and use capital machinery to build new things.
turns.zh: 没错。懒惰是一种杠杆、效率，等等。这不是说我想要无所事事地坐着。而是这样我才有时间做其他事情。我可以专注于健康，花时间陪孩子，自我教育。我不必只是一个不停工作的机器人。所以我对财富的定义是：好吧，让我们摆脱朝九晚五的陷阱。让我的资产在我睡觉时工作。哪些资产可以在睡觉时工作？资本是一个明显的例子。你投资钱，但资本实际上在做什么？当你“投资资本”时，你是在放弃未来资产的权利——这些资产是社会因为你过去的工作而欠你的，假设这些工作都是正当获得的。然后你放弃这些，以便企业可以雇佣人员并使用资本机器来建造新东西。

turns.en: Well, another asset that could earn while you sleep is just a piece of a business. It could be literally a machine, a 3D printer working while you're sleeping. It could be a computer doing computation. It could be a GPU cluster computing the next AI model. It could be an organization, a process of people who are operating a certain way and you kind of check in and make sure the assembly and the goals and all those pieces are correct and some of them working in different time zones in different hours. Could be intellectual property.
turns.zh: 嗯，另一个可以在你睡觉时赚钱的资产就是企业的一部分。它可以是一台机器，比如一台 3D 打印机在你睡觉时工作。它可以是一台计算机在进行计算。它可以是一个 GPU 集群在计算下一个 AI 模型。它可以是一个组织，一个按某种方式运作的人员流程，你只需检查并确保组装、目标等所有部分都正确，其中一些人在不同时区、不同时间工作。也可以是知识产权。

turns.en: It could be intellectual property. It could be a piece of media that's out there that just continues to circulate and people either pay for it or it gets you access or notoriety or something else. So, exactly. So that was my definition of wealth and I think it's a good practical definition of wealth if you're just trying to make money.
turns.zh: 可以是知识产权。可以是一段媒体内容，它一直在传播，人们要么付费，要么它为你带来访问量、知名度或其他东西。所以，没错。这就是我对财富的定义，我认为如果你只是想赚钱，这是一个很好的实用定义。

turns.en: See,
turns.zh: 你看，

turns.en: But David Deutsch's definition of wealth is deeper and more philosophical. And it extends and scales really nicely all the way from a society or civilization down to a single individual. And his definition of wealth is the set of physical transformations that you can affect. Effect as in bring into existence. So an example of that would be, okay, if I have a lot of money stored up then I have wealth because I can go buy a machine and the machine could be as simple as a backhoe that's digging trenches or building houses, or it could be I can hire people to go do something, etc. So there's definitely a huge stored capital component to it. But the bigger part of wealth is again it's a set of physical transformations that you can affect. And the vast majority of the capability to create physical transformations comes from technology and it comes from leverage technology. Like 10 cavemen or 10 paleolithic people wouldn't have the same ability to change things as 10 modern humans do. And that's because of knowledge. If you pay careful attention, you realize that knowledge is the big multiplier. It's not the capital. So this is one thing where Marx was completely wrong. One of the many reasons why Marxism completely fails. Leave aside the obvious that it has wrong incentives, the value is not in the capital. It's not in the factories. It's in the knowledge. If you removed Elon Musk from SpaceX, you can't capture his wealth. It disappears because the knowledge just disappears. SpaceX is less valuable. It is not a pie to be divided up. It is a group of very intelligent people held together by a common mission who are continually affecting change and you're funding them and betting on that.
turns.zh: 但 David Deutsch 对财富的定义更深刻、更哲学。它从社会或文明一直延伸到个人，扩展和缩放得非常漂亮。他对财富的定义是：你能够影响的物理变换集合。“影响”是指使之存在。举个例子，如果我有很多钱，那么我就有财富，因为我可以去买一台机器，这台机器可以简单到是一台挖掘机，用来挖沟或盖房子，或者我可以雇人去做某事等等。所以其中肯定有巨大的存储资本成分。但财富的更大一部分仍然是你能影响的物理变换集合。而创造物理变换的绝大部分能力来自技术，来自杠杆技术。比如 10 个穴居人或 10 个旧石器时代的人，他们的改变事物的能力不如 10 个现代人。这是因为知识。如果你仔细观察，你会发现知识是巨大的乘数，而不是资本。所以这是马克思完全错误的一点。马克思主义彻底失败的众多原因之一。撇开明显的错误激励不谈，价值不在于资本，不在于工厂，而在于知识。如果你把 Elon Musk 从 SpaceX 中移除，你无法捕捉他的财富。财富消失了，因为知识消失了。SpaceX 的价值降低了。它不是一块可以分割的馅饼。它是一群非常聪明的人，由共同使命凝聚在一起，不断影响变革，而你为他们提供资金并押注于此。

turns.en: It's not a piece of gold that you can slice off a chunk and take it and then melt it down. Even that, by the way, doesn't have much value. Gold doesn't have much value. Gold is a pointer to actual value. It's just a scarce metal you can use to trade value. But the actual value is not in the gold. The value is in the people doing things. So as a society gains new knowledge, it becomes wealthier. As an individual gains new knowledge, they become wealthier. For example, I am wealthy by conventional standards, but my earning power is also the highest it's ever been. Why? Because I have tremendous knowledge and people have knowledge that I have knowledge and so because of that, I can affect change at a very big level. What do I get famous for? For doing my startups? No, not really. What am I proudest of? Startups come and go. I'm proud of it in the sense that I got to be a man in the arena and I got to actually do something. What I seem to have gotten popular for is just for thinking through and then articulating things in a certain way and that's knowledge, right? And this brings me back to earlier we were talking about timeless knowledge and wisdom and kind of modern knowledge. Modern knowledge like knowledge about things, knowledge how to build cars and computers and robots and all that. That knowledge is transmissible. One person can discover it, they can convey it to the next person.
turns.zh: 它不是一块金子，你可以切下一块拿走然后熔化。顺便说一句，即使是金子也没有多大价值。黄金是实际价值的指针。它只是一种稀缺金属，你可以用来交易价值。但实际价值不在黄金中。价值在于做事的人。所以当一个社会获得新知识时，它变得更富有。当一个人获得新知识时，他变得更富有。例如，按传统标准我很富有，但我的赚钱能力也是前所未有的。为什么？因为我拥有巨大的知识，人们知道我有知识，因此我可以在非常大的层面上影响变革。我因为什么而出名？因为我的创业公司？不，不是。我最自豪的是什么？创业公司来来去去。我为此感到自豪，是因为我成为了竞技场中的人，真正做了一些事情。我似乎受欢迎的原因，仅仅是因为我思考并以某种方式表达事物，这就是知识，对吧？这让我回到之前我们讨论的永恒知识和智慧，以及现代知识。现代知识比如关于事物的知识，如何制造汽车、计算机、机器人等等。这些知识是可传播的。一个人可以发现它，然后传递给下一个人。

turns.en: The next person can then take it on and duplicate and copy it, making everybody wealthier. Knowledge about human nature, knowledge about what is a good life, what is the purpose of life. As Aristotle said, 'Eudaimonia' or I'm saying that wrong. I don't speak Latin or Greek.
turns.zh: 下一个人就可以接手、复制和拷贝它，让每个人都更富有。关于人性的知识，关于什么是美好生活、什么是人生目的的知识。正如亚里士多德所说，“Eudaimonia”——我可能说错了，我不懂拉丁语或希腊语。

turns.en: It's a lot of vowels.
turns.zh: 元音很多。

turns.en: Yeah, it's a lot of vowels, but it's basically like, how do you live a happy life? That's a very practical philosophy. That knowledge, that wisdom is very hard to convey. Wisdom is very simple. If wisdom could be communicated, we'd all be wise and we'd all be done. But it always has to be stitched in with personal experience and it has to be recreated inside the listener. You cannot just copy. It's not a process that you can copy. So wisdom is something that you need to hear over and over and over again. And you need to hear it in a thousand different ways, in a thousand different contexts until you're going through your own life. And then you do something and it clicks and you're like, 'Oh, this guy said that in this particular way and that resonates with me in this moment.' And then you think it through and you stitch it into your worldview. It becomes part of your value system. And the problem is if you've already heard it said a certain way either too early or too many times, it's a cliche and everyone's going to roll their eyes, right? So one of the things I think some of the greatest wisdom in the world is in nursery rhymes, like 'Row, Row, Row Your Boat.' Find me a greater piece of wisdom than that. It's very hard. 'Row, row, row your boat gently down the stream, merrily, merrily, merrily, merrily, life is but a dream.' There's a lot of wisdom in that. Life is like a river, it is kind of flowing at a certain pace. You do want to row, you don't want to just sit there. You want to be gentle about it, you don't want to splash around too hard. You want to keep a good attitude. Merrily, life is a dream because the whole thing disappears and you're gone. So there's a lot of wisdom in there. And it's packaged in a clever way that persists across time with rhyming and simple words that even children can commit. But at the same time, if I gave that to you as wisdom in a book, you would just laugh because it's too cliche, too simple. You heard it too early.
turns.zh: 是的，元音很多，但它基本上就是：如何过上幸福的生活？这是一门非常实用的哲学。那些知识、那些智慧很难传达。智慧非常简单。如果智慧可以沟通，我们都会变得智慧，一切就都完成了。但它总是必须与个人经验缝合在一起，并且必须在听者内心重新创造。你不能只是复制。它不是你可以复制的过程。所以智慧是你需要一遍又一遍地听到的东西。你需要以一千种不同的方式、在一千种不同的情境中听到它，直到你经历自己的人生。然后你做了某件事，它突然就通了，你会想：“哦，这个人用这种特别的方式说过，此刻与我产生了共鸣。”然后你仔细思考，把它缝合进你的世界观。它成为你价值体系的一部分。问题是，如果你已经以某种方式听过它，要么太早，要么太多次，它就成了陈词滥调，每个人都会翻白眼，对吧？所以我认为世界上一些最伟大的智慧就在童谣里，比如《划船歌》。你能找到比这更伟大的智慧吗？很难。“轻轻划船，顺流而下，快快乐乐，快快乐乐，人生不过一场梦。”这里面有很多智慧。人生就像一条河，它有一定的流速。你确实想划船，你不想只是坐在那里。你要温和地对待它，不要太用力地扑腾。你要保持良好的态度。快快乐乐的，人生是一场梦，因为一切都会消失，你也会离去。所以这里面有很多智慧。它以一种巧妙的方式包装，通过押韵和简单的词语跨越时间，甚至孩子们也能记住。但与此同时，如果我把这作为智慧写进书里给你，你只会嘲笑它，因为它太陈词滥调、太简单了。你听它听得太早了。

turns.en: Yeah, I feel like the Naval detractors, quote unquote, the most common criticism is like, 'Oh, it's fortune cookie or it's a cliche or everybody knows that.' And my response is like, that's Lindy. Cliche is Lindy. All I'm trying to do there is, and I'm saying it mostly for myself, so even when I edit my tweets, because I think I'm more likely to remember it in a different way than I originally phrased it, is I'm trying to say something true in an interesting way. That's literally it. And even if the truth has been said before, and any truth about human nature has been said before, it is a repetition by definition. I don't mind repeating it because it's in an interesting way, and I just figured it out in such a way that it's going to stick with me. And maybe someone else there will figure it out in a way that'll stick with them when I say it this way. And more interestingly, maybe they'll say something back which will cause me to re-evaluate and expand what I just thought. And maybe I'll meet someone interesting. That's why I tweet.
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得 Naval 的批评者，所谓的，最常见的批评就是：“哦，那是幸运饼干里的话，或者是陈词滥调，或者人人都知道。”我的回应是，那就是 Lindy 效应。陈词滥调就是 Lindy。我在那里所做的，而且我主要是对自己说的，所以即使我编辑我的推文，因为我认为我更容易以不同于最初措辞的方式记住它，就是试图以一种有趣的方式说出一些真实的东西。就是这样。即使真理以前被说过，任何关于人性的真理以前都被说过，它本质上就是重复。我不介意重复它，因为它以一种有趣的方式呈现，而且我刚刚以一种会让我记住的方式想通了它。也许别人会在我这样说的时候，以一种对他们来说会记住的方式想通它。更有趣的是，也许他们会回应一些东西，让我重新评估和扩展我刚才的想法。也许我会遇到有趣的人。这就是我发推的原因。

turns.en: And so anything having to do with any topic that is not brand new is going to be cliched. So if you want to only stick to brand new topics, what are you left with? Well, you're left with contemporary storytelling like what did Kim Kardashian have for breakfast or what policies Donald Trump is fighting with the Supreme Court justice over today. To me, that stuff is entertaining, but if that's all we're going to talk about, then please leave. I don't find that additive to my life in any way.
turns.zh: 所以任何与不是全新的话题相关的东西都会变成陈词滥调。所以如果你只想坚持全新的话题，你还剩下什么？嗯，你只剩下当代叙事，比如金·卡戴珊早餐吃了什么，或者唐纳德·特朗普今天与最高法院大法官在什么政策上争吵。对我来说，这些东西很有趣，但如果这就是我们要谈论的全部，那就请离开吧。我不觉得这对我的生活有任何增益。

turns.en: Or we can talk about things that are new. We can talk about the latest breakthrough in an AI model. We can talk about the latest way to do image generation or the latest way people are building drones or what have you, but only a very, very small number of people are qualified to talk about that. They're not necessarily putting all those breakthroughs on Twitter. Nor are you there to absorb deep technical information each time. Nor can most of us tell apart what's real from what's not and what's hyping and what's fake. So I feel like if you're going to discuss anything important and timeless, you are absolutely going to run into cliches.
turns.zh: 或者我们可以谈论新事物。我们可以谈论 AI 模型的最新突破。我们可以谈论图像生成的最新方法，或者人们建造无人机的最新方式等等，但只有非常非常少的人有资格谈论这些。他们不一定把所有那些突破都发在 Twitter 上。你也不是每次都在那里吸收深奥的技术信息。我们大多数人也无法分辨什么是真实的、什么是虚假的、什么是炒作、什么是伪造。所以我觉得如果你要讨论任何重要且永恒的东西，你绝对会遇到陈词滥调。

turns.en: Yeah. David Deutsch, the definition of wealth connected with the ethics of wealth creation. I think that was one seeing the connections between knowledge, wealth, and ethical wealth creation was a huge unlock because I think that's maybe the biggest precursor that gets people stuck before even internalizing the rest of the ideas in the thread is like if you don't believe ethical wealth creation is possible, you will reject the whole game.
turns.zh: 是的。大卫·多伊奇，财富的定义与财富创造的伦理相联系。我认为看到知识、财富和伦理财富创造之间的联系是一个巨大的解锁，因为我认为这可能是最大的前兆，让人们在甚至内化线程中其余想法之前就卡住了：如果你不相信伦理财富创造是可能的，你就会拒绝整个游戏。

turns.en: That's right. Yes. You will get bitter too early.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。你会过早地变得愤世嫉俗。

turns.en: And it kind of sucks when people do that. The common critiques of capitalism are all around cronyism or the Federal Reserve money printing or handing out favors to bankers and so on. There's no question when money is involved, when wealth is involved, not just a portion of the efforts, but the majority of efforts will go into siphoning, controlling, defending, fighting over, slicing up wealth. The majority. If you look in nature, there are six times as many species that are parasites as are standalone, right? So most things are eating each other and trying to live off of something else. And there are a few that are reaching for the sun in the sky and trying to survive. And so it's just always seems easier to people to grab something than to create something. And when society ends up with too many people focused on slicing up the pie rather than baking it, then those societies collapse. And it's easy to be cynical. I mean, you watch the bank bailouts. But I would argue none of that is free market capitalism. That is government intervention through and through. Picking winners, picking losers, which is not to say that capitalism means everybody plays fair. Capitalism is you come up with a minimum structured set of rules, the minimum viable that allows people to play the fair game where a lot of the energy that would get directed into stealing property or fighting each other gets channeled into creating property. So that's why the basis of capitalism is respect for private property. If I improve a piece of land or if I create something, I can keep that improvement.
turns.zh: 人们这样做的时候挺糟糕的。对资本主义的常见批评都围绕着裙带关系、美联储印钞、向银行家输送利益等等。毫无疑问，当涉及金钱、涉及财富时，不仅是一部分努力，而是大部分努力都会用于抽取、控制、保卫、争夺、分割财富。大部分。如果你看看自然界，寄生物种的数量是独立物种的六倍，对吧？所以大多数东西都在互相吞噬，试图靠别的东西生存。只有少数在向着天空中的太阳伸展，努力生存。所以对人们来说，抢夺东西似乎总是比创造东西更容易。当社会最终有太多人专注于分蛋糕而不是烤蛋糕时，那些社会就会崩溃。而且很容易变得愤世嫉俗。我的意思是，你看到银行救助。但我会争辩说，那些都不是自由市场资本主义。那是彻头彻尾的政府干预。挑选赢家，挑选输家，这并不是说资本主义意味着每个人都公平竞争。资本主义是你提出一套最低限度的结构化规则，最低可行规则，让人们能够进行公平的游戏，其中很多原本会用于偷窃财产或互相争斗的能量被引导到创造财产上。所以这就是为什么资本主义的基础是尊重私有财产。如果我改良了一块土地，或者我创造了某样东西，我可以保留那个改良成果。

turns.en: And yes, you have to figure out how to deal with the externalities of pollution and with public goods and so on and so forth. But basic free market capitalism figured that out to a pretty large extent and has been responsible for the greatest increase in wealth and human flourishing in history. And people forget that. They don't spend enough time looking at places where people are living on a dollar a day or don't have the ability to protect private property. Those societies just melt down into full-on warfare.
turns.zh: 是的，你必须想办法处理污染的外部性和公共物品等问题。但基本的自由市场资本主义在很大程度上已经解决了这些问题，并且带来了历史上最伟大的财富增长和人类繁荣。人们忘记了这一点。他们没有花足够的时间去看那些每天靠一美元生活或无法保护私有财产的地方。那些社会只会彻底崩溃，陷入全面战争。

turns.en: We don't think about a capitalism quality score.
turns.zh: 我们不会考虑资本主义质量评分。

turns.en: That's right. That's what it should be. The US is about one-third or 40% socialist now because that's how much GDP is controlled by the government, including state, federal, and local governments. Then you've got another 20 or 30% that is drawn by regulation or in sectors like education and healthcare, which are basically quasi-government run. So even here, the private sector is quite small and shrinking. It's just like how much of a load can that carry before people give up or flee? The failure case for the US is the best and brightest no longer immigrate here. So I do think you can focus on all that unfairness and melt down. But at the same time, this is the greatest period for wealth creation in human history. There's more knowledge, more capital, more leverage being created than any other time. So if you're moderately intelligent, not afraid of working hard, and flexible, you can do extremely well. You have to get it outside of your mind that the whole game is rigged.
turns.zh: 没错，就应该这样。美国现在大约有三分之一到 40% 是社会主义，因为这么多 GDP 由政府控制，包括州、联邦和地方政府。另外还有 20% 到 30% 受监管影响，或者处于教育和医疗等基本准政府运营的行业。所以即使在这里，私营部门也相当小，而且在萎缩。这就像在人们放弃或逃离之前，它能承受多大的负担？美国的失败案例是最优秀和最聪明的人不再移民到这里。所以我确实认为你可以关注所有这些不公平，然后崩溃。但与此同时，这是人类历史上财富创造最伟大的时期。知识、资本和杠杆的创造比以往任何时候都多。所以如果你中等聪明，不怕努力工作，而且灵活，你可以做得非常好。你必须从脑海中消除整个游戏被操纵的想法。

turns.en: Yeah. It's just like, look, in life, there is a lot of luck. The fact that you even exist is luck. There are a lot of factors out of your control, but life is long. People are very consistent. Compounding does work. And you can rise past the luck just through sheer force of will and hard work. If you don't believe in that, then you're a cynic. And cynics' beliefs are self-fulfilling. You will be stuck in the mud. However, people who are optimistic, willing to work hard, and willing to look past the unethical or luck-based nature of it all, they're the only ones who have a chance. At least my experience has been that those people do well on long time scales. So it might be 10 to 30 years. It's not going to be two years. There are no get-rich-quick schemes. That's just someone selling you something. On a long enough time scale, you can rise out of the muck. But it starts with believing that it's possible. If you don't think it's possible, then it's impossible for you. I think this is a common theme you will notice across the great entrepreneurs. Steve Jobs and Elon Musk take on what they consider to be impossible tasks because they look around and say, 'If other people can do it, why can't I?' So I think that mindset is super important.
turns.zh: 是的。就像，在生活中，运气成分很大。你存在本身就是运气。有很多因素你无法控制，但人生很长。人们非常一致。复利确实有效。你可以通过纯粹的意志力和努力工作超越运气。如果你不相信这一点，那你就是个愤世嫉俗者。而愤世嫉俗者的信念是自我实现的。你会陷入泥潭。然而，那些乐观、愿意努力工作、愿意忽略其中不道德或基于运气的本质的人，他们才是唯一有机会的人。至少我的经验是，那些人在长期尺度上做得很好。所以可能是 10 到 30 年。不会是两年。没有快速致富的计划。那只是有人在向你推销东西。在足够长的时间尺度上，你可以摆脱困境。但首先要相信这是可能的。如果你认为不可能，那对你来说就是不可能的。我认为这是你会在伟大企业家身上注意到的共同主题。史蒂夫·乔布斯和埃隆·马斯克承担了他们认为不可能的任务，因为他们环顾四周说：“如果别人能做到，为什么我不能？”所以我认为这种心态非常重要。

turns.en: What I like about Deutsch also is that he has a very optimistic view on this. It's not just a belief. He really does structure it in good explanations as to why human progress happens. He says we should be optimistic because we discovered this model in the British Enlightenment, which was their philosophical movement where we basically figured out that we can advance science and society through the quest for good explanations. That means we are truth-seeking. We make conjectures. Those conjectures are open to criticism. Every conjecture can be criticized, and then we hold them up to experiment. We test theories against each other. We find out what works, and whatever works we circulate and promulgate to other people, and then we advance society forward. He's shown that this is how we've been operating for the last few hundred years. Time periods like this have existed in the past, maybe during the Italian Renaissance, maybe during the Greek sort of enlightenment age, maybe during the Industrial Revolution onwards. We would be foolish to lose that or extinguish it by being anti-rational, saying there's no such thing as truth, giving up the quest for good explanations, or banning criticism. That's what censorship does. Or by believing that certain people, like big science, always have the answers. So there is reason to be optimistic as long as we don't let go of this method of advancing. This method of conjecture and criticism leading to good explanations is the real scientific method. We get taught the scientific method when we're kids. That's not really how it works. That's a very idealized fiction test tube laboratory version. But the reality is that under the right circumstances, given the right freedoms, we're constantly scientific about everything in our lives. Every person is trying to improve their life. So everyone who cares about being healthy and fit is a little scientist running all the different experiments: which kind of diet works for me, which kind of exercise works for me. They're constantly making new hypotheses, hearing new hypotheses, trying new things, seeing what worked and what didn't. So they're doing the same thing in their own way. All truth-seeking systems work this way. In science, you can call it conjecture and criticism. In technology and business, you can call it innovation and companies going out of business, right? Companies create things, and the ones that fail go out of business. In evolution, you can call it mutation. The genes mutate, and then selection removes the ones that didn't cut it from the gene pool. But all truth-seeking systems work this way: we make conjectures, either random like in mutation or deliberate like in science or through technology innovation or business innovation, and then there is a filtering process. Even Taleb talks about this in a completely different way. He has this whole skin in the game thing. Skin in the game isn't just merely 'I have my own money at stake.' Skin in the game is 'I get cut out over time if I don't succeed.' If I take risk, I should bear the risk. That's what he considers bad capitalism: when other people bear the risk. That's what banksters do. Good capitalism is when I take a risk and then I bear the consequences or the rewards of that risk. Which is why, to Taleb, the most ethical thing you can do is be a risk taker. You be an entrepreneur, you go and you bear risk. Don't pass it off to other people or society.
turns.zh: 我喜欢 Deutsch 的一点是，他对此持非常乐观的态度。这不仅仅是一种信念。他确实用很好的解释来构建人类进步的原因。他说我们应该乐观，因为我们在英国启蒙运动中发现了这个模式，那是他们的哲学运动，我们基本上明白了可以通过追求好的解释来推进科学和社会。这意味着我们在追求真理。我们提出猜想。这些猜想接受批评。每个猜想都可以被批评，然后我们通过实验来检验。我们相互测试理论。我们找出什么有效，然后把有效的东西传播给其他人，从而推动社会前进。他表明，这就是我们过去几百年运作的方式。这样的时期在过去也存在过，也许是在意大利文艺复兴时期，也许是在希腊启蒙时代，也许是从工业革命开始。如果我们变得反理性、说没有真理、放弃追求好的解释或禁止批评，从而失去或熄灭它，那将是愚蠢的。这就是审查制度所做的。或者相信某些人，比如大科学，总是有答案。所以只要我们不放弃这种进步的方法，就有理由乐观。这种猜想和批评导致好解释的方法才是真正的科学方法。我们小时候被教科学方法。但那并不是真正的工作原理。那是一个非常理想化的虚构试管实验室版本。但现实是，在正确的环境下，给予正确的自由，我们一直在对生活中的一切进行科学探索。每个人都在努力改善自己的生活。所以每个关心健康和健身的人都是一个小科学家，进行各种不同的实验：哪种饮食适合我，哪种锻炼适合我。他们不断提出新假设，听到新假设，尝试新事物，看看什么有效，什么无效。所以他们以自己的方式做着同样的事情。所有追求真理的系统都是这样运作的。在科学中，你可以称之为猜想和批评。在技术和商业中，你可以称之为创新和公司倒闭，对吧？公司创造东西，失败的公司就倒闭了。在进化中，你可以称之为突变。基因发生突变，然后选择将那些不合格的从基因库中移除。但所有追求真理的系统都是这样运作的：我们提出猜想，要么是随机的，比如突变，要么是刻意的，比如科学或技术创新或商业创新，然后有一个过滤过程。就连 Taleb 也以完全不同的方式谈论这一点。他有整个“风险共担”的概念。风险共担不仅仅是“我自己的钱处于风险中”。风险共担是“如果我不成功，随着时间的推移我会被淘汰”。如果我承担风险，我就应该承担风险。他认为坏资本主义就是别人承担风险。这就是银行家所做的。好资本主义是我承担风险，然后我承担该风险的后果或回报。这就是为什么对 Taleb 来说，你能做的最道德的事情就是成为一个风险承担者。你成为一名企业家，你去承担风险。不要把它转嫁给别人或社会。

transcript.sec: Skin in the Game and Accountability

turns.en: And then the skin in the game arises not from the fact that you had some skin in the game, but that over time the people who were bad risk takers got weeded out and they either lost capital, they lost their lives, or they lost their reputations. And the people who were good risk takers ended up benefiting and then spreading the benefits that they gained to the rest of society. So even his skin in the game phrasing is somewhat isomorphic to Deutsche's conjecture and criticism.
turns.zh: 而“利益攸关”的出现，并非因为你一开始就投入了利益，而是随着时间的推移，那些糟糕的风险承担者被淘汰了，他们要么损失了资本，要么失去了生命，要么毁掉了声誉。而那些优秀的风险承担者最终受益，并将他们获得的利益传播给社会其他成员。所以，即使是他的“利益攸关”这个说法，在某种程度上也与多伊奇的猜想和批评同构。

turns.en: Yeah, I think that's why accountability was such an important reframe from entrepreneurs take risk because accountability bakes in this you personally bear the upside and the downside. You don't want risk for risk's sake. You want to conspicuously, directly, and publicly take a risk.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这就是为什么“问责制”是对“企业家承担风险”这样一个重要的重新定义，因为问责制内在地要求你个人承担收益和损失。你不想要为了冒险而冒险。你想要的是显著地、直接地、公开地承担风险。

turns.en: Yeah. This is why I think Buffett and Munger were a little opposed to investment banks going public because investment banks used to be partnerships, LLCs where the individual partners would take on risk. LLCs only protect you so much. If the partners do something bad, you can have risk flow through to them. On the flip side, once you go public as a big faceless corporation, you can hide behind the CEO. You can be on the board and they can take risks that can bankrupt society. Society has to bail them out because they levered up and they put the whole financial system at risk. But somehow they themselves still get paid and walk scot-free and get to keep their personal assets. They're fine. So it's this pushing risk off into society through a corporate liability shield. I think Buffett and Munger pointed out that that was not a good thing.
turns.zh: 是的。这就是为什么我认为巴菲特和芒格有点反对投资银行上市，因为投资银行过去是合伙制或有限责任公司，个人合伙人要承担风险。有限责任公司的保护是有限的。如果合伙人做了坏事，风险可以传导到他们身上。反过来，一旦你作为一家大型匿名公司上市，你就可以躲在 CEO 后面。你可以进入董事会，他们可以承担可能让社会破产的风险。社会不得不救助他们，因为他们加了杠杆，把整个金融体系置于风险之中。但不知何故，他们自己仍然拿到报酬，逍遥法外，还能保住个人资产。他们安然无恙。所以，这是通过公司责任盾牌将风险推给社会。我认为巴菲特和芒格指出这不是一件好事。

turns.en: Yeah, Munger is a great example. The people who make unethical behavior impossible are some of the effective saints of the civilization, right? So, he talks about double entry bookkeeping and a cash register and so on.
turns.zh: 是的，芒格就是一个很好的例子。那些让不道德行为变得不可能的人，是文明中一些有效的圣人，对吧？所以，他谈到了复式记账法和收银机等等。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, the problem is unethical is a fuzzy line, right? Different people have different morality. It's easier to be moral when you're not in the ring yourself doing anything or fighting anything or building anything because then you can just get on your moral high horse. So, academics and journalists can pretend to be the most moral people when they don't actually do anything, right? So on the flip side you have like for example in the AI race, people are swallowing up all the copyrighted content then later saying oops, sorry I ate your homework and spit it back out. So this is a difficult conversation, it's not like a one-size-fits-all glib answer.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，问题在于不道德是一条模糊的界限，对吧？不同的人有不同的道德观。当你自己不在圈内做任何事、斗争或建设时，更容易显得道德，因为你可以站在道德高地上。所以，学者和记者可以在实际上什么都不做的时候假装是最道德的人，对吧？所以反过来，例如在 AI 竞赛中，人们吞噬了所有受版权保护的内容，然后说“哎呀，对不起，我吃了你的作业又吐了出来”。所以这是一个艰难的对话，不是一个放之四海而皆准的油滑答案。

transcript.sec: Ethics as Bottleneck and the Problem of Size

turns.en: Is there a sense where ethics and morals around how we conduct capitalism is like the bottleneck of progress? You're saying are ethics or morals a bottleneck to progress?
turns.zh: 从某种意义上说，我们进行资本主义的方式中的伦理和道德是否是进步的瓶颈？你是说伦理或道德是进步的瓶颈吗？

turns.en: Yeah. As we think about how effective capitalism is in relation to how many people are makers versus takers.
turns.zh: 是的。当我们思考资本主义的有效性与创造者和索取者的人数之间的关系时。

turns.en: Yeah. And there's culture and there's regulation and there's so many inputs to that.
turns.zh: 是的。还有文化、监管以及许多其他输入因素。

turns.en: Yeah, there are. I think actually the biggest impediment to progress, believe it or not, is size. Size of institutions, size of countries, size of groups. Because the larger the group you have, the more groupthink you have. Groups don't admit mistakes. Groups don't search for truth. Groups search for consensus. Only individuals or very small teams of people can search for truth. Large groups have to have consensus or they fall apart. They fight with each other and they degenerate. So groups don't change their minds. Groups don't apologize. It's mobs. And so I think the best situation comes from when you have small groups of people who are each responsible for both the benefits and the losses. They have skin in the game and are able to take risks. So for example in religion, you want to see back in Europe when it was hundreds of religions and they were each trying their own thing and they came to the new world and then you kind of got to see which worked and which didn't. In technology and in business, you want to see lots of city states with different policies. You want to see lots of companies competing with each other. So, humans do best when we're small groups competing.
turns.zh: 是的，有。我认为实际上进步的最大障碍，信不信由你，是规模。机构的规模、国家的规模、群体的规模。因为群体越大，群体思维就越严重。群体不承认错误。群体不寻求真理。群体寻求共识。只有个人或非常小的团队才能寻求真理。大群体必须有共识，否则就会分崩离析。他们互相争斗，然后退化。所以群体不会改变想法。群体不会道歉。那是乌合之众。所以我认为最好的情况是，当你有小群体的人，每个人都对收益和损失负责。他们有利益攸关，能够承担风险。例如在宗教方面，你想看到在欧洲有数百种宗教时，它们各自尝试自己的东西，然后来到新世界，你就能看到哪些有效，哪些无效。在技术和商业领域，你想看到许多城邦有不同的政策。你想看到许多公司相互竞争。所以，人类在作为小群体竞争时表现最好。

turns.en: But the problem with this is that competition breaks into warfare. So, religions are responsible for a lot of bloodshed when they're next to each other. These little city states always end up fighting and being consolidated. These little businesses end up being swallowed up by larger ones or there's monopoly effects when one of them ends up in charge. So an ideal society really understands how to federalize, how to create lots and lots of small competing but not in a physical kinetic competition but in a virtual competition, figures out how to harness all of that competitive energy to create great things. And so the US benefited from being federalist, from having 50 states with 50 different regulatory systems and 50 different experiments being run on the governance level, and it benefits from capitalism which is lots of small companies competing. But where it breaks down is when you have a small number of big monopolies or you have big banks that have taken over the government or the government running education and healthcare or when you end up with all 50 states only being allowed to compete in small things but you have three-letter agencies and giant federal taxes running everything from DC. So that's when the system starts breaking down. So I think that the most flourishing systems are the ones where you have small countries, you have small tribes and you have small companies competing against each other in a nonviolent way. And the whole trick is how do you keep them each sovereign and from making it kinetic and overrunning each other physically. To me, that would be the most moral system because you can't get us all to agree on what's moral and what's ethical, right? That's an impossible thing. That's why the whole AI alignment thing is a joke. We can't even align humans, how are we going to align AIs? All AI alignment really boils down to in practice is the owner or inventor or creator of that AI wants to put on a leash and tell it what to do.
turns.zh: 但问题在于，竞争会演变成战争。所以，当宗教相邻时，它们造成了大量流血事件。这些小城邦总是最终互相争斗并被吞并。这些小企业最终被更大的企业吞并，或者当其中一个掌权时产生垄断效应。所以一个理想的社会真正懂得如何联邦化，如何创造大量小规模的竞争，但不是物理上的动能竞争，而是虚拟竞争，懂得如何利用所有这些竞争能量来创造伟大的事物。因此，美国受益于联邦制，拥有 50 个州和 50 种不同的监管体系，以及在治理层面进行的 50 种不同实验，并且受益于资本主义，即许多小公司相互竞争。但系统崩溃的地方在于，当你有少数大型垄断企业，或者大银行接管了政府，或者政府运行教育和医疗，或者当所有 50 个州只被允许在小事上竞争，而三个字母的机构和巨额联邦税从华盛顿控制一切时。那时系统就开始崩溃了。所以我认为最繁荣的系统是那些拥有小国家、小部落和小公司以非暴力方式相互竞争的系统。而整个诀窍在于如何保持它们各自的主权，并防止竞争变成动能冲突和物理上的相互征服。对我来说，这将是最道德的体系，因为你无法让我们所有人都同意什么是道德和伦理，对吧？那是不可能的。这就是为什么整个 AI 对齐是个笑话。我们甚至无法对齐人类，我们怎么能对齐 AI？所有 AI 对齐在实践中归结为，该 AI 的所有者、发明者或创造者想要给它套上缰绳，告诉它该做什么。

turns.en: Yeah, it's very easy to be aligned with people when we have a focus only on what we ourselves and what we control and decisions that we make for ourselves. But as soon as I start to think it's morally and ethically necessary to control what you do, now all of a sudden we have a real disagreement about morals and ethics.
turns.zh: 是的，当我们只关注我们自己、我们控制的事物以及我们为自己做出的决定时，很容易与人保持一致。但一旦我开始认为在道德和伦理上有必要控制你做什么，突然间我们就在道德和伦理上产生了真正的分歧。

turns.en: That's right. There's incentive alignment which is I can give you equity in the same business and then we're incentive aligned.
turns.zh: 没错。存在激励对齐，比如我可以给你同一家企业的股权，然后我们就激励对齐了。

turns.en: Although you may still choose to ignore that incentive and do something different. But really all other forms of alignment tend to boil down to coercion. Right? Maybe we're aligned because we're part of the same religion. We're part of the same tribe. We live in the same household. We're doing the same business together. But the moment you're talking about aligning strangers who don't live next to each other that don't have kinship, I think the only way you can align them is by clubbing one of them until they pay. You know, they listen, that's just authoritarianism. That's just force. That's violence. That's warfare, which undergirds everything. Like society runs on this thin veneer of civilization, but ultimately underneath the people with the guns are always in charge. And sometimes they forget it for a decade or two decades at a time, but either they take back over or someone from the outside comes in and takes over. Which is why I think as a society we have too many frivolous laws being passed where people pretend like the law is not being enforced by people with guns. Every law down to like your parking ticket is enforced by somebody with a gun because if you don't pay that parking ticket, then they're going to summon you to court. And then if you don't go to court, then they're going to send somebody to your house. And if you don't answer that, then they'll put out a warrant for your arrest. And if you don't turn yourself in, then people with guns will come after you. So, at the end of the day, if you just follow that down, it's amazing how few people seem to realize this. But everything is enforced by people with guns. And then those people with guns, you're giving them guns and telling them to enforce laws. And of course, it's human nature, especially as that organization becomes larger, they will start enforcing it preferentially. You know, they'll enforce it a little bit harder against their enemies and a little bit softer against their friends. It's like that South American dictator who famously said, you know, for my friends, everything; for my enemies, the law, right? Because the law is just whatever the biggest gang agrees upon. And as a society, we become very disconnected from that. Especially in modern democracies where people who don't have any physical power, any stake in the system are voting to control people who do have physical power. It creates an unstable situation. And you know we might end up in a situation where well I don't know this is actually a very interesting question. Do we end up with more city states in the future? Do we end up with more small dictatorships or small kinships of organized people with weapons carving out little fiefs? Or do we ossify into a small number of very large states? In my mind there's nothing worse than a global government like a single government running everything because it's a one-size-fits-all. It's a panopticon and it's much more likely to end up like you know China or Russia kind of control them democratic the truly democratic republic. And even because democracies have a habit of electing dictators and that's a one-way street.
turns.zh: 尽管你仍然可以选择忽略那个激励，去做别的事情。但所有其他形式的对齐，实际上都归结为胁迫。对吧？也许我们因为同属一个宗教、同一个部落、住在同一个屋檐下、一起做生意而对齐。但当你谈论对齐那些不住在一起、没有亲缘关系的陌生人时，我认为唯一的方法就是用棍棒打到他们服从为止。你懂的，他们听话，那不过是威权主义，是暴力，是战争，而这一切支撑着所有事物。社会运行在薄薄的文明表层上，但归根结底，持枪的人永远掌权。有时他们会忘记这一点十年或二十年，但要么他们重新夺回权力，要么外部势力介入并接管。这就是为什么我认为社会通过了太多轻浮的法律，人们假装法律不是由持枪者执行的。每一条法律，小到停车罚单，都是由持枪者执行的——因为如果你不交罚单，他们就会传唤你上法庭；如果你不去法庭，他们就会派人去你家；如果你不回应，他们就会发出逮捕令；如果你不自首，持枪的人就会来抓你。所以，归根结底，如果你顺着这条链往下看，很少有人意识到这一点：一切都是由持枪者执行的。然后你给那些持枪者枪，告诉他们执行法律。当然，人性使然，尤其是当组织变得更大时，他们会开始有偏袒地执行法律。他们会更严厉地对待敌人，更宽松地对待朋友。就像那位南美独裁者名言说的：对我的朋友，一切；对我的敌人，法律。因为法律不过是最大帮派达成的共识。作为社会，我们与这一点脱节了。尤其是在现代民主国家，那些没有实际权力、没有系统利益的人投票来控制拥有实际权力的人，这造成了不稳定的局面。我们可能会陷入一种情况——这其实是个很有趣的问题：未来我们会看到更多城邦吗？更多小型独裁政权或由有组织的武装人员组成的亲缘团体割据小领地？还是我们会僵化成少数几个非常大的国家？在我看来，没有什么比全球政府更糟糕的了——一个统治一切的单一政府，因为它是一刀切的，是全景监狱，更可能最终像中国或俄罗斯那样控制，而不是真正的民主共和国。而且，民主国家有选举独裁者的习惯，那是一条单行道。

turns.en: I want to see if we can play with some of the David Deutsch ideas in a business and judgment context. Right. If good explanations are hard to vary or good products hard to vary.
turns.zh: 我想看看我们能否在商业和判断的语境中运用一些大卫·多伊奇的思想。对吧。如果好的解释难以变动，或者好的产品难以变动。

turns.en: Absolutely. Good products are hard to vary. So hard to vary means that you can't change the details without the thing breaking or falling apart or no longer being a good explanation. So one of the ways you recognize them is you look back at the explanation and you say, 'Well, how could it have been otherwise? There's no other thing which would fit all the facts.' It's like when you put a puzzle piece in the right spot in a puzzle like, 'Oh, of course it had to be that one because no other piece will fit if it doesn't have this piece and this part and this part jutting out and this hole over here.' So the same way good products are hard to vary. So if you look at an iPhone for example, it's hard to change the characteristics of the iPhone without breaking what makes it great. And in fact, their original form factor when they launched, I think it was in 2007 is still quite similar to the form factor today. It hasn't really changed, right? They nailed the basic form factor same way with like the laptop form factor, right? It still hasn't changed back from the original days. So good products are very hard to vary. They do have a very unique set of interlocking parts that then allows them to be greater than the whole. You know, there's this concept in complexity theory called emergence. And emergence is when you take a whole bunch of different parts and you put them together and something new emerges that you did not expect. A new capability emerges. But one of the interesting parts is that the parts that you put together below, you didn't give them more freedom. You gave them constraints. You actually locked them together in certain ways. And by constraining the way in which they could operate, some new capability emerged above. So it was the constraints that created the capability above. And so that's a little counterintuitive, right? Because you would think more freedom leads to more freedom. No, in this case it was constraints. Same way like if you look at a corporation or a contract, a contract is a voluntary constraint that we enter into to go create something in the future. Like a marriage contract is to go create children in the household. Or like a company is a contract to go create an equity bearing entity where we agree in advance how we're going to split up the proceeds and who's going to do the work and how you're going to get the capital. So to be able to create things, you actually have to be able to constrain things. Once the things are locked together, they're hard to vary. You can't remove a component, nor can you change a component without the thing falling apart. And that's why a good product is simple. It actually, by simple I don't mean inordinately simple. It doesn't mean like ELI5 simple necessarily but it means that you remove the parts that are unnecessary.
turns.zh: 完全正确。好的产品难以变动。难以变动意味着你不能改变细节而不让东西崩溃、散架或不再是一个好的解释。识别它们的方法之一是回顾解释并问自己：“它怎么可能不是这样？没有其他东西能符合所有事实。”就像拼图时把一块拼图放在正确位置：“哦，当然必须是这一块，因为如果没有这块凸起和这个凹槽，其他任何一块都放不进去。”同样，好的产品难以变动。以 iPhone 为例，很难改变它的特性而不破坏它的卓越之处。事实上，2007 年发布时的原始外形与今天的外形仍然非常相似，没有真正改变，对吧？他们抓住了基本外形，就像笔记本电脑的外形一样，至今没有变回最初的样子。所以好的产品非常难以变动。它们确实有一套独特的互锁部件，使整体大于部分之和。复杂性理论中有一个概念叫涌现。涌现是指你把大量不同的部件放在一起，产生了一些你未曾预料的新东西，一种新能力出现了。但有趣的是，你放在下面的部件并没有获得更多自由，你给了它们约束。你实际上以某种方式将它们锁定在一起。通过限制它们运作的方式，上方涌现出了新能力。所以是约束创造了上方的能力。这有点反直觉，对吧？因为你会认为更多自由会带来更多自由。不，在这种情况下是约束。同样，看看公司或合同：合同是我们自愿进入的约束，以便在未来创造某物。比如婚姻合同是为了在家庭中生育孩子；公司合同是为了创建一个股权实体，我们事先约定如何分配收益、谁做什么工作、如何获得资本。所以为了创造事物，你必须能够约束事物。一旦事物锁定在一起，它们就难以变动。你不能移除一个组件，也不能改变一个组件而不让整个东西崩溃。这就是为什么好的产品是简单的。简单并不意味着过分简单，也不意味着像“解释给五岁小孩”那样简单，而是意味着你移除了不必要的部分。

turns.en: Elegant.
turns.zh: 优雅。

turns.en: Elegant. Yes. So there was an Antoine de Saint-Exupéry quote where he says you know the airplane wing is perfectly designed not because there's nothing left to add but because there's nothing left to take away. Right. You can't take it away. It is platonic. It is the platonic ideal of an airplane wing. It is hard to vary. So a good product is similarly hard to vary. You know, one of the conundrums that people come up with these days is why are all things converging on the same design? Why do all cars look the same? Right? It's because we're doing wind tunnel testing now and so we know that that's like the most efficient model and we want efficient. So all the cars now look windswept.
turns.zh: 优雅。是的。安托万·德·圣-埃克苏佩里有句名言：飞机机翼设计完美，不是因为没有什么可添加的，而是因为没有什么可去掉的。你不能去掉它。它是柏拉图式的，是机翼的柏拉图理想形态，难以变动。所以好的产品同样难以变动。如今人们遇到的一个难题是：为什么所有东西都趋同于同一设计？为什么所有汽车看起来都一样？因为我们现在做风洞测试，知道那是最有效的模型，而我们想要高效。所以所有汽车现在都看起来像被风吹过一样。

transcript.sec: Good products are hard to vary

turns.en: It's hard to vary out of that design. Unless you're doing it to be ironic or to stand out, like maybe you know Elon with the Cybertruck: 'I got so much power, I don't care.' But I'll bet you that thing is wind tunnel tested too. It's angular and wind tunnel tested, as opposed to streamlined and wind tunnel tested. But good products are hard to vary because they also encapsulate knowledge, and as you gain knowledge, you put that into the product. What technology does is it's the automation of knowledge. You figure something out, then you figure out how to automate it, then you figure out how to scale that automation, and you figure out how to distribute that scaled automation. That embedded knowledge is hard to vary because underneath it is a good explanation. Once you figure out how to make electric cars, once you have the knowledge for batteries, it's kind of inevitable that most cars will go electric. You may need some off-road vehicles or military vehicles around gasoline for survivability reasons and disaster relief reasons, but for the average everyday consumer, they don't want to drive to the gas station and get smelly oil that can blow up on them and put it into this Rube Goldberg machine of parts and then have controlled explosions driving pistons while they go down the seat. It's going to look in 50 years like when you see those images of guys riding steam engines—you know, like the early cars are literally steam engines with boilers and coal. It's going to look like that, right? And once you figure out electric cars, then you realize actually we want electric cars. Not only so you don't have to go to the gas station, they have fewer moving parts, it's easier to repair, it's less messy, it's less polluting. But also because if you want self-driving cars, electric cars do better because it's much easier for a car to recharge itself than it is for it to refuel itself. And if you have swappable batteries, maybe you can operate 24/7 with very little downtime. And it can capitalize better, bring the cost down. So it becomes what it is. Once you see electric cars working, you realize that that is a hard to vary product for the next generation. So I think all good products have this characteristic. In a sense, if you're selling multiple versions of your product, you better have a really good reason for that.
turns.zh: 很难脱离那种设计。除非你是为了讽刺或引人注目，比如马斯克的特斯拉 Cybertruck：‘我太强了，我不在乎。’但我敢打赌那玩意儿也经过风洞测试。它是有棱角的风洞测试，而不是流线型的风洞测试。但好的产品很难被改变，因为它们也封装了知识。随着你获得知识，你把它融入产品中。技术所做的是知识的自动化。你弄明白一件事，然后想办法自动化它，然后想办法规模化这种自动化，再想办法分发这种规模化的自动化。那些嵌入的知识很难改变，因为其背后是一个好的解释。一旦你弄明白如何制造电动汽车，一旦你掌握了电池的知识，大多数汽车转向电动几乎是必然的。你可能需要一些越野车或军用车辆使用汽油，出于生存能力和救灾原因，但对于普通日常消费者来说，他们不想开车去加油站，加那种可能爆炸的臭油，然后把它塞进这个鲁布·戈德堡机械装置里，再让受控爆炸驱动活塞，同时他们坐在座位上。50 年后，这看起来就像你看到的那些骑着蒸汽机的人——你知道，早期的汽车简直就是带锅炉和煤的蒸汽机。它会变成那样，对吧？一旦你弄明白了电动汽车，你就会意识到我们其实想要电动汽车。不仅是不用去加油站，它们活动部件更少，更容易修理，更干净，污染更少。而且，如果你想要自动驾驶汽车，电动汽车表现更好，因为汽车给自己充电比加油容易得多。如果你有可更换电池，也许可以 24/7 运行，停机时间很少。它能更好地利用资本，降低成本。所以它就成了这样。一旦你看到电动汽车运行良好，你就会意识到那是下一代难以改变的产品。所以我认为所有好产品都有这个特性。从某种意义上说，如果你销售多个版本的产品，你最好有非常好的理由。

turns.en: Well, that gets to the other one, which is good products have a surprising reach.
turns.zh: 嗯，这就引出了另一个点，好的产品有惊人的影响力。

turns.en: Absolutely. Great products, just like good explanations, can reach to lots of applications that you never thought of. You know, Steve Jobs and crew never sat around with the iPhone or the iPad saying, 'Oh, this will be great for Exxon and companies doing oil work because there's a cool app for them for figuring out their logistics and workflow and tracking.' No, they don't go to that level. Or a more relevant example might be when the iPhone came along, you know, the Blackberry was entrenched in enterprise and Steve Ballmer, God bless him, made fun of the iPhone for not having a keyboard. Steve Ballmer did lots of things right, so I hate to pick him out. I mean, he did very well for Microsoft and for himself, so maybe I won't call him out as much, but there were people who basically said, 'Well, the BlackBerry has a keyboard. The enterprise will never tolerate a phone without a keyboard.' And what they didn't account for is that there are so many things solved by not having a keyboard, by having the multi-touch, and by having a great screen and software keyboard. And nowadays voice—like nowadays, the last thing you want to do is add a keyboard to a phone. It's all going voice based thanks to AI. Once you've built that product, it has reach, and once it reaches into every consumer's pocket, well, those consumers bring those products to work and they're like, 'Why am I using a Blackberry at work and I'm using an iPhone at home and the iPhone is so much better?' And the knowledge is the Deutsch definition. Knowledge is the thing that stays persistent in the environment. It replicates itself in the environment. Genes that encode proper knowledge on how to adapt to the environment, they replicate. Those genes get passed down. Genes that are incorrect, that are false, they do not replicate in the environment. They get weeded out of the gene pool. The same is true of ideas. If I give you an idea for how to make money, or if I give you even a general principle, or I give you a specific thing to go out and do and it fails, you're not going to keep that knowledge. You're not going to spread that knowledge. So knowledge is persistent in the environment. And once you've created knowledge, and once the knowledge of multi-touch screens was created, then the knowledge of keyboards was obsolete, at least in that context. And the Blackberries got replaced by iPhones when people who brought their iPhones in from home would use them at work instead of using Blackberries. And the IT guys got outvoted because ultimately they're there to provide a service to the rest of the company and not to run the company. And then also technology has deeply winner-take-all network effects. All technology does. It just is more obvious in some cases than others. Like something like a Facebook or a WhatsApp has an extremely visible network effect. Apple does too because they're developer platforms. But even things like Amazon, which you would have thought would not have a network effect. Back in the day, we thought there'd be zillions of e-commerce stores in a death struggle to the bottom. Turns out it's not the case. Turns out there's one huge winner, and just like the internet does, which is it has one huge winner for everything and then has a long tail of very small players, but it destroys the middle. It destroys the mediocre middle, which was relying on geography or regulations to survive. They get blown away by the mega aggregator and then by a long tail. But all of technology has network effects and has scale economies. So the best product gets to amortize its development over the largest user base and so it has the most things thought through. It has the most support. It has the most latest bleeding-edge technology. Doesn't matter how rich you are, you can't buy a better phone. There's no amount of money you can spend to buy a better phone than the latest iPhone or the latest Android phone. Like that's it, right? We all have access to the same phone because there is an R&D budget of hundreds of billions of dollars that has gone into the smartphone supply chain and into the current smartphone designs.
turns.zh: 完全正确。伟大的产品，就像好的解释一样，可以触及你从未想过的许多应用。你知道，史蒂夫·乔布斯和团队从来没有拿着 iPhone 或 iPad 说：‘哦，这对埃克森美孚和做石油工作的公司来说太棒了，因为他们有一个很酷的应用程序来规划物流、工作流程和追踪。’不，他们不会想到那个层面。或者一个更相关的例子可能是，当 iPhone 问世时，黑莓在企业领域根深蒂固，史蒂夫·鲍尔默，上帝保佑他，嘲笑 iPhone 没有键盘。史蒂夫·鲍尔默做了很多正确的事，所以我不愿意挑他的毛病。我的意思是，他为微软和他自己都做得很好，所以也许我不该过多指责他，但确实有人说：‘黑莓有键盘。企业永远不会容忍没有键盘的手机。’他们没有考虑到的是，没有键盘、有多点触控、有出色的屏幕和软件键盘，能解决很多问题。而现在有了语音——如今，你最不想做的就是给手机加键盘。多亏了 AI，一切都转向语音。一旦你构建了那个产品，它就有了影响力，一旦它进入每个消费者的口袋，那些消费者就会把产品带到工作中，他们会想：‘为什么我在工作用黑莓，在家用 iPhone，而 iPhone 好得多？’知识就是多伊奇的定义。知识是在环境中持续存在的东西。它在环境中自我复制。编码了如何适应环境的正确知识的基因会复制。那些基因被传递下去。错误的、不正确的基因不会在环境中复制。它们会被从基因库中淘汰。思想也是如此。如果我给你一个赚钱的想法，或者我给你一个一般原则，或者我给你一个具体的事情去做，但它失败了，你就不会保留那个知识。你不会传播那个知识。所以知识在环境中是持久的。一旦你创造了知识，一旦多点触控屏幕的知识被创造出来，键盘的知识就过时了，至少在那个背景下。黑莓被 iPhone 取代，是因为人们把家里的 iPhone 带到工作中使用，而不是用黑莓。IT 人员被否决了，因为归根结底，他们是为公司其他部门提供服务的，而不是管理公司。而且技术具有深刻的赢家通吃网络效应。所有技术都是如此。只是有些情况更明显。比如 Facebook 或 WhatsApp 有非常明显的网络效应。苹果也有，因为它们是开发者平台。但即使是像亚马逊这样的公司，你本以为不会有网络效应。过去，我们认为会有无数电子商务商店陷入一场你死我活的竞争。结果并非如此。结果是一个巨大的赢家，就像互联网一样，每件事都有一个巨大的赢家，然后是一个长尾的小玩家，但它摧毁了中间层。它摧毁了平庸的中间层，那些依赖地理或法规生存的中间层。它们被超级聚合器和长尾击垮了。但所有技术都有网络效应和规模经济。所以最好的产品可以在最大的用户基础上摊销其开发成本，因此它考虑得最周全。它拥有最多的支持。它拥有最新的尖端技术。不管你多有钱，你都买不到更好的手机。花多少钱也买不到比最新款 iPhone 或最新款安卓手机更好的手机。就是这样，对吧？我们都能用上同样的手机，因为智能手机供应链和当前智能手机设计已经投入了数千亿美元的研发预算。

transcript.sec: Status signaling and the pursuit of timeless knowledge

turns.en: So if you're rich, all you can do is you can like encrust it with diamonds in a kind of a sad attempt to look special, but you really just look like an idiot. Or it's like cars, like really expensive cars are not better. They're just weirder. I don't think there's a better car on the planet today than probably the Tesla Model Y. That's, you know, 2025. Like objectively speaking, if you look at all the things that it does that other cars don't do, it's kind of the all-around best car. You can't spend a million dollars to buy a better car. You can just spend a million dollars to buy a goofier car or a weirder car. More extreme variables, more extreme. And then you're just showing yourself as someone who's goofy and weird and trying to signal for status. And there's no worse way to signal status than to be shown as trying to buy status.
turns.zh: 所以如果你很有钱，你能做的就是把东西镶上钻石，用一种可悲的方式试图显得特别，但实际上你看起来就像个白痴。或者像汽车，真正昂贵的车并不更好，只是更奇怪。我认为今天地球上没有比特斯拉 Model Y 更好的车了，那是 2025 年的。客观来说，如果你看它做的其他车做不到的事情，它基本上是全方位最好的车。你花一百万美元买不到更好的车，只能买到更滑稽或更奇怪的车。更极端的变量，更极端。然后你只是表现出自己是个滑稽古怪、试图炫耀地位的人。没有比被看作试图用钱买地位更糟糕的炫耀方式了。

turns.en: I had this theory that as humans we're always searching for something perfect and permanent because that's the opposite of the life that we have which is an imperfect and impermanent life. And we can search for it in spirituality, the quest for God; we can search for it in science, which is the quest for the grand unified perfect theory of everything. There are analogies in art and beauty, in the Sistine Chapel, trying to encapsulate something perfect for a long, long period of time. And so I feel like I personally want to focus on that, and there's a struggle because you also want to look at new things. The world is always moving and advancing, especially in the domain of things and objects and learning and technology and science. You want to look at the most recent things. You don't want to learn the science from 30 years ago. You want to learn the science best we know today, and the same about technology. And that has practical value: knowing about AI or self-driving cars or robotics or what have you. So it's always good to know about what's moving quickly and is on the forefront of knowledge, but at the same time you also want to study the timeless. And that's human nature; human nature doesn't change. So there you want to rely upon the olden people. You know, you want to read Schopenhauer and you want to read the Dao De Jing and so on, or the Bible, because people don't change. And so with my tweets, I kind of struggle with, or even with writing or podcasting, thinking time that I spend, what I struggle with is I want to spend a lot of time on the timeless because that knowledge carries through the rest of my life, but I also want to spend time on the timely, which is the most modern stuff. Now there I have to look for the modern stuff that's actually useful. It's actually the modern stuff about things like I want to know about AI. I want to know about self-driving cars. I want to know about robotics. I want to know about space travel and space exploration. I want to know about drones. But I don't want to know what Kim Kardashian is doing today. Like that's useless knowledge. In fact, I don't even need to know any lessons in human nature from her and her interactions with other people because those lessons have been written down better by Schopenhauer or Kant or someone like that in the past, right? Plato. So when it comes to philosophy and human nature, that's timeless material. It's worth learning. It's somewhat clichéd in the sense that we can get into what cliché is, but it's somewhat clichéd. But at the same time it's very important to absorb because these are the deepest guiding principles. You know, philosophy is kind of the study of how to live a good life. I think Aristotle called that eudaimonia or something like that, probably saying the word wrong, but that's worth studying. But at the same time, it's worth studying the ultra-modern most recent stuff when it comes to the world of science and technology and things. At least for our book, our work, our writing, I don't like putting timely things in there. And the trap is timely things that have nothing to do with knowledge of how things work but rather knowledge of people or just gossip or news. I think a lot of people have said this, Taleb most recently, that you know, to see how worthless news is, just read yesterday's newspaper, right? What's the half-life of the information that you're consuming? How long will it be relevant in the future? It is predominantly based on how long has it been relevant in the past.
turns.zh: 我有一个理论：作为人类，我们总是在寻找完美和永恒的东西，因为这与我们拥有的不完美和短暂的生命相反。我们可以在灵性中寻找它——对上帝的追寻；我们可以在科学中寻找它——对万物统一完美理论的追寻。在艺术和美中也有类比，比如西斯廷教堂，试图在很长一段时间内封装某种完美的东西。所以我个人想专注于这一点，但这里有一个挣扎，因为你也想看新事物。世界总是在移动和进步，尤其是在事物、物体、学习、技术和科学领域。你想看最新的东西。你不想学 30 年前的科学，你想学我们今天知道的最好的科学，技术也一样。这有实用价值：了解 AI、自动驾驶汽车、机器人技术等等。所以了解快速变化和知识前沿的东西总是好的，但同时你也想研究永恒的东西。那是人性；人性不会改变。所以在这方面你要依赖古人。你知道，你想读叔本华，你想读《道德经》等等，或者《圣经》，因为人不会改变。所以对于我的推文，我有点挣扎，甚至对于写作或播客，我花时间思考，我挣扎的是我想花大量时间在永恒的东西上，因为那些知识会伴随我的余生，但我也想花时间在及时的东西上，即最现代的内容。现在我必须寻找那些真正有用的现代东西。实际上，现代的东西是关于像我想了解 AI、自动驾驶汽车、机器人技术、太空旅行和探索、无人机。但我不想了解金·卡戴珊今天在做什么。那是无用的知识。事实上，我甚至不需要从她和她与他人的互动中学习任何关于人性的教训，因为这些教训过去已经被叔本华、康德或类似的人更好地写下来了，对吧？柏拉图。所以当谈到哲学和人性时，那是永恒的材料，值得学习。在某种意义上它有点陈词滥调——我们可以讨论什么是陈词滥调——但它有点陈词滥调。但与此同时，吸收它非常重要，因为这是最深刻的指导原则。你知道，哲学是研究如何过好生活的学问。我想亚里士多德称之为 eudaimonia 或类似的东西，可能发音不对，但那值得研究。但与此同时，在科学和技术世界，研究最现代的最新东西也值得。至少对于我们的书、我们的工作、我们的写作，我不喜欢把及时的东西放进去。陷阱是那些与事物运作知识无关、而是关于人物或八卦或新闻的及时东西。我想很多人说过这个，最近塔勒布说过：要知道新闻有多没用，只需读昨天的报纸，对吧？你消费的信息的半衰期是多少？它在未来多久会相关？这主要取决于它在过去多久相关。

turns.en: Yeah, this ties into a little bit with Deutsch's work, which is he'll say a good explanation, one of the ways you know it's a good explanation, is that it has reach, and reach across space and time. So it applies to lots of things that you didn't expect. It explains things which you would think were out of its domain, or it was originally that theory was postulated to explain a local thing, but it almost always ends up explaining a global thing. So for example, like in the axial tilt theory of the earth, you know, you might have come up with that to explain why there are seasons. But then the seasons flip when you go to the southern hemisphere and you can't change that. You can't take the part of the theory that explains seasons in the northern hemisphere and then throw it out when you get to the southern hemisphere. That theory has deep reach or wide reach. So the best theories are deep and wide.
turns.zh: 是的，这跟多伊奇的工作有点关系，他会说一个好的解释，判断它是好解释的一个方法，就是它有跨度，跨越空间和时间的跨度。所以它适用于很多你没想到的东西。它解释了你认为超出其领域的东西，或者最初那个理论是为了解释一个局部事物而提出的，但它几乎总是最终解释了一个全局事物。例如，像地球的轴倾角理论，你可能提出它来解释为什么有季节。但当你到南半球时，季节翻转了，你不能改变这一点。你不能把解释北半球季节的那部分理论拿走，然后到了南半球就扔掉它。那个理论有深度跨度或广度跨度。所以最好的理论既深又广。

turns.en: And another completely different angle on the same thing is Jed McKenna. He has this, I don't know if you ever read Jed McKenna, but he's kind of this crazy anonymous enlightened dude, right? And he writes these really funny books starting with Spiritual Enlightenment: The Damnedest Thing. And I highly recommend them for people who are not mystical, not spiritual at all, but yet they know something is off, something is missing, something is wrong. It's like, I think it was Morpheus the way he describes the Matrix. He goes, "A splinter in your mind," right? "Do you feel that, Neo?" It's like a splinter in your mind. Yeah. So if you feel like there's a splinter in your mind, Jed McKenna is a good starting point. And he has this proof, quote unquote, of the existence of truth or God or what have you. And it's actually a rework of a very old proof by a monk named Anselm. I don't know if he realized that, but it's a rework of an existing proof. Anyway, it's not really a proof. It's not a mathematical proof, but it does rely on this idea that hey, do you believe that truth exists? And if you believe that truth exists, then you know that's a different universe than one where you believe that there is no truth, right? And if you believe that if truth exists, then do you believe that truth can be non-existent in a certain place or a certain time or it can be temporal? And the answer is no. And so if you kind of follow that chain of reasoning, you realize that truth would have to have the widest and deepest reach.
turns.zh: 另一个完全不同的角度是杰德·麦肯纳。我不知道你有没有读过杰德·麦肯纳，但他是个疯狂的匿名开悟者，对吧？他写了一些非常有趣的书，从《灵性开悟：最该死的事》开始。我强烈推荐给那些不神秘、完全不灵性，但知道有些不对劲、缺失、错误的人。就像莫菲斯描述《黑客帝国》时说的：“你脑子里有根刺”，对吧？“你感觉到了吗，尼奥？”就像你脑子里有根刺。是的。所以如果你觉得脑子里有根刺，杰德·麦肯纳是一个好的起点。他有一个所谓的真理或上帝存在的证明。实际上这是对一位叫安瑟伦的僧侣的古老证明的改写。我不知道他是否意识到这一点，但这是对现有证明的改写。无论如何，这不是真正的证明，不是数学证明，但它确实依赖于这个想法：嘿，你相信真理存在吗？如果你相信真理存在，那么你知道那是一个不同于你相信没有真理的宇宙，对吧？如果你相信真理存在，那么你相信真理可以在某个地方或某个时间不存在，或者可以是暂时的吗？答案是否定的。所以如果你沿着那条推理链，你会意识到真理必须具有最广和最深的跨度。

transcript.sec: The Universal Quest for a Theory of Everything

turns.en: Again, it would have to be this all-encompassing theory. So whether in science, whether in spirituality, whether in technology, I think we're all just looking for something perfect and permanent. And as humans, we're always going to be dissatisfied. The human mind will always be dissatisfied until it finds that, and that's kind of the drive for achievement. And the more accomplished somebody becomes, the more they get to level up and go for something even broader, even deeper, even further. So you know, Deutsch, for example, isn't just content with being a mere physicist; he's also studying what he calls the four deepest theories: computation, theory of evolution by natural selection, and epistemology, because he's trying to explain everything in kind of a grand unified theory. And you know, every philosophy at its core has to have some appeal to universality, right? To like explaining everything. It's just kind of human nature. Even the whole current AGI craze, it's about like, oh, we're going to invent God. It's going to explain everything. This is the last technology. I don't know what it is about humans, but it keeps driving us towards that. Everybody wants a theory of everything.
turns.zh: 再说一次，它必须是一个包罗万象的理论。无论是在科学、灵性还是技术领域，我认为我们都在寻找某种完美和永恒的东西。作为人类，我们永远会感到不满足。人类的心灵在找到那个东西之前永远不会满足，而这正是成就的驱动力。一个人越有成就，就越能升级，去追求更广泛、更深刻、更遥远的东西。比如，多伊奇不满足于仅仅做一个物理学家；他还在研究他所谓的四个最深刻的理论：计算、自然选择进化论和认识论，因为他试图用一种大统一理论来解释一切。你知道，每一种哲学的核心都必须具有某种普遍性的吸引力，对吧？就像解释一切一样。这几乎是人类的本性。甚至当前整个 AGI 热潮，也是关于“哦，我们要发明上帝了。它将解释一切。这是最后一项技术。”我不知道人类是怎么回事，但它一直驱使我们走向那个方向。每个人都想要一个万有理论。

turns.en: We're absolutists.
turns.zh: 我们是绝对主义者。

turns.en: Yeah. Marxism is kind of that, you know. It's kind of like we're all equal, we're all the same, you know, we're all consciousness, we're all just one. Obviously Marxism has lots of problems. I'm not going to sit here and critique Marxism all day long, although I would be happy to. But it is also that there's that universalist intent in there. So, in a deep way, this quest for the answer to everything keeps seeping back into every endeavor of our lives. And whether you realize it or not, it's always lurking underneath.
turns.zh: 是的。马克思主义就有点像那样，你知道。它有点像我们都是平等的，我们都一样，我们都是意识，我们都只是一体。显然马克思主义有很多问题。我不会坐在这里整天批判马克思主义，尽管我很乐意。但它也有那种普遍主义的意图。所以，从深层来看，这种对一切答案的追求不断渗透到我们生活的每一次努力中。无论你是否意识到，它总是潜伏在下面。

turns.en: You said that your earning power is higher than it's ever been and has been on a steady increase over time. I'm curious to sort of break down the components of that. Like how has that changed? What are the inflection points? Even as you've sort of gotten more retired, I guess you would say, less deliberate.
turns.zh: 你说你的赚钱能力比以往任何时候都高，并且随着时间的推移稳步增长。我很好奇想分解一下其中的组成部分。比如它是如何变化的？转折点是什么？即使你有点变得更退休了，我想你会说，不那么刻意了。

turns.en: Yeah. To be fair, right now I'm like minimally retired. I'm working hard. I got a new company, right? In a way, I'm probably putting in as much work as I ever have. But you know, the vast majority of it goes into my new company, which I really care about. Still in stealth mode. But you know, some of it goes into making a few investments here or there, helping out some people that I know in the ecosystem, plus my old companies. But in terms of earning power, some of that comes from stored capital, so I can fund my own projects. Some of that comes from reputation, so people will kind of trust me and fund me and so on. But a lot of it just comes through knowledge of knowing what to go after, how to structure a new company, how to get it going, how to recruit good people, recognizing early on like, oh, don't bother recruiting that person even though they're good because they're too high ego, they won't fit in, or don't sell too hard because it'll just come unstuck later. Or go after these kinds of people, you know, you need more technical builders at the beginning and less of the marketers and so on, because otherwise you're just going to create too many requirements too early, etc., etc. So it's just a lot of practical know-how which has been folded into principles.
turns.zh: 是的。公平地说，我现在算是半退休。我工作很努力。我有一家新公司，对吧？在某种程度上，我可能投入了和以前一样多的工作。但你知道，其中绝大部分都投入到了我非常在意的新公司，它还在隐身模式。但你知道，其中一部分用于做一些零散的投资，帮助生态系统中我认识的一些人，还有我的老公司。但在赚钱能力方面，一部分来自积累的资本，所以我可以资助自己的项目。一部分来自声誉，所以人们会信任我，资助我等等。但很多来自于知道该追求什么、如何构建新公司、如何启动、如何招募优秀人才的知识，早期就意识到，哦，别费心招募那个人，即使他很优秀，因为他自我太强，不会融入；或者不要推销得太用力，因为以后会出问题。或者去追求这类人，你知道，一开始需要更多的技术构建者，更少的营销人员等等，否则你只会过早地制造太多需求，等等。所以这只是一堆被提炼成原则的实用知识。

turns.en: You know, there's kind of a learning curve to everything. I think it was either Schopenhauer or Seneca, one of the two. I think this was Seneca where he basically said, it's one of his letters to Lucilius. He said something along the lines that the right way to learn is from the specific to the general and not from the general to the specific. And what that means is you do things in reality, you encounter reality, you test it, you learn from it, and then you generalize. And then that lets you know when that maxim or that aphorism or that principle or that value system, when it applies and when it does not. Because one of the problems with picking up other people's values and aphorisms, you don't know when they apply, when they don't. So when people say on Twitter, well, you contradicted yourself. It's like, well, using that word a different way, it doesn't apply in this case. It's not, you know, even though I don't use a lot of adjectives, what I mean is in most cases, in the way that I'm thinking about it, this applies. And in other cases, the way I'm thinking about it, this other thing applies. And I'm just giving myself a heuristic, which is not to be blindly followed. And these people are looking for math proofs.
turns.zh: 你知道，每件事都有学习曲线。我想是叔本华或塞内卡，两者之一。我认为是塞内卡，他在一封给卢西利乌斯的信中大致说过，正确的学习方式是从具体到一般，而不是从一般到具体。这意味着你在现实中做事，你遇到现实，你测试它，你从中学习，然后你进行概括。然后这让你知道那个格言、箴言、原则或价值体系何时适用，何时不适用。因为接受别人的价值观和箴言的一个问题是，你不知道它们何时适用，何时不适用。所以当人们在推特上说，你自相矛盾了。就好像，嗯，用不同的方式使用那个词，它在这种情况下不适用。不是的，你知道，即使我不使用很多形容词，我的意思是，在大多数情况下，按照我的思考方式，这个适用。而在其他情况下，按照我的思考方式，另一个东西适用。我只是给自己一个启发式，不是要盲目遵循。而这些人是在寻找数学证明。

turns.en: Yeah. You came in with the frame of the specific when you created the general.
turns.zh: 是的。你在创造一般时是从具体的框架出发的。

turns.en: That's right. I created the general to help me navigate future specifics, but each situation is specific, you know, so it may have multiple general things applied to it that compete, and one of them overrides or multiple of them override. So that's how you go from the specific to the general. But the opposite is when you go to academia. You know, you study too many things in school, you learn all these grand theories, and then out in the field you're spouting theories but you don't know which one applies where and when, or you've just learned the wrong theory for the current thing. And that's when you kind of end up as what Nassim Taleb calls an IYI, an Intellectual Yet Idiot. And it's basically someone who's overeducated and underpracticed.
turns.zh: 没错。我创造了一般来帮助我应对未来的具体情境，但每个情境都是具体的，所以它可能同时适用多个一般原则，它们相互竞争，其中一个或多个会覆盖其他。这就是如何从具体到一般。但相反的情况是当你进入学术界。你知道，你在学校学了很多东西，你学到了所有这些宏大的理论，然后到了实际领域，你滔滔不绝地讲理论，却不知道哪个理论在何时何地适用，或者你只是为当前的事情学到了错误的理论。这时你最终就会成为纳西姆·塔勒布所说的 IYI，即“有知识的白痴”。基本上就是过度教育而实践不足的人。

turns.en: So is the recipe for building judgment then the combination of these heuristics, these maxims, these ideas, and the experience to plug in to sort of license them up?
turns.zh: 那么，建立判断力的秘诀就是这些启发式、格言、想法，以及将它们付诸实践的经验的结合吗？

turns.en: Experience. First you got to do. First, you know, another aphorism is if you want to be a philosopher king, first be a king, right? First become a king, that's the harder one, and that's the one that'll make you. The process of becoming a king will turn you into a philosopher automatically. The process of becoming a philosopher will take you further from being a king, and you won't be a very useful philosopher either. No hard and fast rules. There are people who didn't do a lot practically speaking that were incredible philosophers, but they're very, very, very rare. But yeah, I mean, you want to be in the arena. Like, for example, Elon. I'm working on a book about him, and he has general principles, but those principles come from doing things.
turns.zh: 经验。首先你必须去做。首先，你知道，另一句格言是：如果你想成为哲人王，先成为王，对吧？先成为王，那更难，而那会让你成长。成为王的过程会自动把你变成哲学家。成为哲学家的过程会让你离王更远，你也不会成为一个非常有用的哲学家。没有硬性规定。有些人在实践上做得不多，却是了不起的哲学家，但他们非常非常罕见。但没错，我的意思是，你想在竞技场上。比如，埃隆。我正在写一本关于他的书，他有一般原则，但这些原则来自于做事。

turns.en: He knows the value, for example, of speed. Elon is famous for how fast he does things and he gets cycle time down. That is a core principle for him, and he has a lot of detailed implementations of what that means practically: engineers get to set their own requirements, you always unblock everything, you don't hide behind email. He has many heuristics in his head that derive from that big principle.
turns.zh: 他知道速度的价值，比如埃隆就以做事快著称，他能缩短周期时间。这是他的核心原则，他有很多具体的实现方式：工程师可以自己定需求，你总是要扫清一切障碍，不躲在邮件后面。他脑子里有很多源自这个大原则的启发式方法。

turns.en: But if I just sat around academia and somebody told me that speed is everything, go with speed, I'd just be kind of idiotic about it. I'd be running around hyperventilating all the time. I wouldn't know how to apply it. So, again, life is lived in the arena. You have to do the things to learn the things. It's a mistake to just learn and not do. When I was a kid, I was a big fan of reading and I used to read a lot. I read hundreds, maybe thousands of books. I've lost count. Most books I pick up and read, and I'll be like, 'Oh, I read this book 10 years ago, I just forgot about it.' And I'm two-thirds of the way through the book because I have a terrible memory for things that don't stick with me or aren't incredibly useful. I used to be proud of that fact. Now I realize most of my reading was worthless. It was all academic book knowledge. A lot of it was just fiction. These days I read less, but very deliberately. I read because I'm really interested in something and I'm trying to learn or figure something out. I'll read small amounts and then think a lot. I use reading as a way to spark my own thinking, rather than just taking in things from other people and regurgitating them later. A lot of Taleb doesn't stick with me. For example, his anti-fragile thing doesn't really stick with me because I can't find many actual examples in my life where I can apply true anti-fragility. True anti-fragility is not just resilience; it's that you actually get stronger through adversity. Yes, I can see some cases like hormetic effects in weightlifting, but there are many cases where I don't find a practical application. I get why Taleb does it—his entire investing strategy is anti-fragile; if the system collapses, he makes more, or just volatility makes him more—but I don't find as many examples. So the concept of anti-fragility doesn't stick with me, even though I know that's the book most people think had the biggest impact on them and he values it the most. It doesn't work for me. I don't read that book, but I'm always rereading Skin in the Game because that one has many nuggets I can apply. I can say, 'Oh, that's the generalization of something I noticed in my specific life.' The minority rule is a good example. I keep seeing the minority rule show up everywhere. So you do need the practical applications in your life. You have to do. Even to the extent today that I want to become smarter and wiser, that means I have to work.
turns.zh: 但如果我只是坐在学术界，有人告诉我速度就是一切，要追求速度，那我就会很愚蠢地去做。我会一直跑来跑去，喘不过气来，不知道怎么应用。所以，生活是在竞技场中度过的。你必须通过做事来学习。只学不做是错误的。我小时候很喜欢读书，读了很多，几百本甚至几千本，数不清了。大多数书我拿起来读，然后发现‘哦，这本书我十年前读过，但忘了。’读到三分之二时才发现，因为我对不深刻或不太有用的东西记性很差。我以前还为此自豪，现在意识到大部分阅读都是无用的。那都是学术性的书本知识，很多只是小说。现在我读得少了，但更有目的性。我读书是因为真的感兴趣，想学点东西或弄明白什么。我读少量内容，然后大量思考。我把阅读当作激发自己思考的方式，而不是单纯吸收别人的东西然后复述。塔勒布很多东西我记不住。比如他的反脆弱概念，我记不住，因为我在生活中找不到太多实际例子来应用真正的反脆弱。真正的反脆弱不只是韧性，而是通过逆境变得更强。是的，我能看到一些例子，比如举重中的毒物兴奋效应，但很多情况下我找不到实际应用。我理解塔勒布为什么这么做——他的整个投资策略就是反脆弱的；系统崩溃他赚更多，或者仅仅是波动性就让他赚钱——但我找不到那么多例子。所以反脆弱这个概念我记不住，尽管我知道那是大多数人认为对他影响最大的书，也是他最看重的。但对我没用。我不读那本书，但我一直在重读《非对称风险》，因为那本书有很多可以应用的精华。我可以对自己说，‘哦，那是我在具体生活中注意到的东西的概括。’少数派规则就是一个好例子。我不断看到少数派规则到处出现。所以你的生活中确实需要实际应用。你必须去做。即使到今天，我想变得更聪明、更有智慧，也意味着我必须工作。

turns.en: Yeah, I was going to say you're continuously injecting experience and principle.
turns.zh: 是的，我想说你在不断地注入经验和原则。

turns.en: Correct. You start with experience, you start with specific knowledge. Then the specific knowledge turns into more generalized knowledge. Then you can take that and turn it into values, which tie into your existing values. It improves your judgment. I think ultimately in this age of infinite leverage, judgment is the most important thing. If you knew where to navigate, people will pay you for that. The captain of a ship is chosen based on his ability to get the team together and tell them where we're going, inspire them to go there. But where to go is the most important part. If you have two candidates for CEO of Apple, the most valuable company in the world currently, maybe it's Nvidia, but whatever. You have two candidates for the CEO of the company. One is right 80% of the time and one is right 85% of the time. The company's worth trillions of dollars. Who are you going to put in charge? You'll pay the guy who is right 85% of the time a lot more. You'll pay him billions of dollars more per year because he's steering a multi-trillion dollar ship. The direction matters more than any other single thing. And finally, I would say that judgment is really important. Judgment comes through experience and reflection. So you experience things, you have honest reflection about those things, and you build your judgment. Then at some point, your judgment becomes so good that you cannot even explain it or articulate it anymore. There's a point early on where you don't know what to do and you have to rationally think through what to do and take feedback. Eventually you get so good at making decisions that you don't go to other people. Yes, you can get a little bit of feedback to inform your judgment, but you know you're in the best position to make up your mind. So you exercise the judgment and you can articulate to other people why you did that. But there comes a point where your judgment is so good that you can't even articulate it. That's when it's called taste. When you're just like, 'It doesn't feel right to me. This is just the way we should do it. This is the way I want to do it.' But at that point it's taste. People who have gotten to that point, like the Rick Rubins of the world or the Steve Jobs of the world, have really good taste. I think those are the people who are the most creative and create the greatest works of art and business because they have really good taste. I would guess that Musk, for example, on SpaceX he has taste, and there are a few key engineers around him who have taste. If you look at the leading AI labs and you see the tweet threads from some of the researchers there when they care to talk about how they're choosing which experiments to run, which ones not to run, it boils down to taste. They have taste about, 'I'm going to throw thousands of GPUs at this for hundreds of hours and spend lots of money and probably wind up with nothing. But my intuition, my taste tells me, my developed intuition, which is my taste, tells me whether to try it out or not.'
turns.zh: 没错。你从经验开始，从具体知识开始。然后具体知识变成更通用的知识。然后你可以把它转化为价值观，与你现有的价值观结合。这能提升你的判断力。我认为在这个无限杠杆的时代，判断力是最重要的。如果你知道往哪里走，人们会为此付钱给你。船长被选中是因为他能把团队凝聚起来，告诉大家我们要去哪里，并激励大家前往。但去哪里是最重要的部分。如果有两个苹果公司 CEO 候选人，苹果是目前全球最有价值的公司，也许现在是英伟达，但无所谓。两个候选人，一个 80%的时候正确，一个 85%的时候正确。公司价值数万亿美元。你会让谁掌舵？你会付给那个 85%正确的人多得多的钱。每年多付几十亿美元，因为他掌舵的是数万亿美元的船。方向比任何其他单一因素都重要。最后，我想说判断力非常重要。判断力来自经验和反思。你经历事情，诚实地反思，然后建立判断力。到了某个点，你的判断力会变得非常好，甚至无法解释或表达。早期你不知道该做什么，必须理性思考并接受反馈。最终你变得非常擅长做决定，不再需要请教别人。是的，你可以获得一些反馈来辅助判断，但你知道自己是最有资格做决定的人。所以你运用判断力，并能向别人解释为什么这么做。但到了某个点，你的判断力好到无法表达。这时它被称为品味。你只是觉得‘这不对，我们就该这么做，我就想这么做。’但那就是品味。达到那个境界的人，比如里克·鲁宾或史蒂夫·乔布斯，都有很好的品味。我认为他们是最有创造力的人，能创造出最伟大的艺术和商业作品，因为他们有很好的品味。我猜马斯克在 SpaceX 就有品味，他身边也有几个有品味的核心工程师。如果你看看领先的 AI 实验室，当研究人员愿意谈论他们如何选择做哪些实验、不做哪些实验时，归根结底是品味。他们有品味，比如‘我要投入几千块 GPU 跑几百小时，花很多钱，很可能一无所获。但我的直觉、我的品味，也就是我发展出来的直觉，告诉我是否值得一试。’

turns.en: Yeah, you had a tweet. It takes time to develop your gut, but once it's developed, don't listen to anything else.
turns.zh: 是的，你发过一条推文。培养直觉需要时间，但一旦培养好了，就不要听别的了。

turns.en: Exactly. And this is just another way of saying taste. It's your gut feel. And it can be around people.
turns.zh: 完全正确。这只是品味的另一种说法。就是你的直觉。它可以体现在对人上。

turns.en: Older people have very good judgment about other people because the one thing we are always gaining experience in is human interaction. No matter what we're doing, we're interacting with other people. So we build up experience about who to work with, who to trust, who not to trust, and who not to work with. That ends up as your gut feel. As you get older, you have to trust your gut feel.
turns.zh: 年长的人对他人的判断力非常强，因为我们始终在积累人际互动的经验。无论做什么，我们都在与他人互动。因此，我们积累了关于与谁合作、信任谁、不信任谁、不与谁合作的经验。这最终成为你的直觉。随着年龄增长，你必须相信自己的直觉。

turns.en: Is your investing now primarily feel like taste?
turns.zh: 你现在投资主要是凭感觉、凭品味吗？

turns.en: Yes, almost entirely. I hate articulating it. A lot of times I pass on things now. It's not just taste about the company; it's an understanding of my own tastes in the sense of what I like and what I don't.
