He does Siddha Maha Yoga. He's kind of more the classic guru type. There are personal friends I have. Steven Belchure, who was on Air Chat with me. He's on Twitter too. James Pierce, you know, he's tweeted a lot. Effortless Stoicism, Kapal Gupta, who I worked with in the past. There's Ascendor who's on YouTube. I tweeted him out recently. There's another guy who works as a waiter at some restaurant in the Midwest. I forget his name. I tweeted him out. There's Saja. There's so many of them. I mean, my YouTube is filled with them and I've watched enough of their videos enough that I know that these are the real deal. These are not fakers.
turns.zh: 哦，我的意思是，有一些比较知名的，比如 YouTube 上的 Rupert Spira 和 Mooji。他们并不出名。有一个人叫 Nardinan，我和他相处了很多时间。他现在半出名了。他修习 Siddha Maha Yoga。他更像是经典的上师类型。还有一些我的私人朋友。Steven Belchure，他曾和我一起在 Air Chat 上。他也在 Twitter 上。James Pierce，你知道，他发了很多推文。Effortless Stoicism，Kapal Gupta，我以前和他合作过。还有 Ascendor，他在 YouTube 上。我最近在推文中提到了他。还有另一个人，在中西部某家餐馆当服务员。我忘了他的名字。我在推文中提到了他。还有 Saja。还有很多。我的意思是，我的 YouTube 上全是他们，我看了足够多的他们的视频，我知道这些人是货真价实的。他们不是骗子。

turns.en: Is it a palpable difference when you're in their physical presence?
turns.zh: 当你和他们实际在一起时，能感觉到明显的不同吗？

turns.en: No, there's no like fancy aura where you're going to be floating on cloud nine. I mean, a lot of that is hyperbole. But I think what you find is that they have the persistent experience of no self. They see through the fact that the self is just a thought. It's a constructed identity and there's no one there to actually really get happy or unhappy. So because of that, they now live basically as pure awareness or consciousness. And so they are essentially melded with their environment. They feel a sense of peace because your awareness is the one thing that's never changed about your entire life. It is the one thing that is perfect and permanent in your life from birth to death. And they are no longer confused or think of themselves as a separate body-mind construct. So nothing bothers them. That's kind of the common thing. And the way I usually figure them out is I just spend enough time around them that I see something genuinely bad happen to them. You have to hang around long enough for something genuinely bad to happen to them, and then you see their reaction, and there's not even a perturbation. And that's how you know.
turns.zh: 不，没有什么花哨的光环让你飘飘欲仙。我的意思是，很多都是夸张的说法。但我认为你会发现，他们持续体验着无我。他们看穿了自我只是一个念头的事实。它是一个构建的身份，实际上没有一个“人”在那里真正感到快乐或不快乐。因此，他们现在基本上作为纯粹的觉知或意识而活。所以他们本质上与环境融为一体。他们感到一种平静，因为你的觉知是你一生中从未改变过的一件事。它是你从出生到死亡中完美而永恒的一件事。他们不再困惑，也不认为自己是一个独立的身心构造。所以没有什么能打扰他们。这大概是共同点。我通常识别他们的方法是，我在他们身边待足够长的时间，看到真正糟糕的事情发生在他们身上。你必须和他们待足够久，直到真正糟糕的事情发生，然后你看到他们的反应，甚至没有一丝波动。这就是你如何知道的。

turns.en: Interesting. And 'enlightened' makes it seem binary. Is that how you see it?
turns.zh: 有趣。而“开悟”这个词让它看起来是二元的。你是这么看的吗？

turns.en: It is binary. It is binary because, and this is a little counterintuitive, it's not a path. It's not like a... Oh, Sadhguru is another one, by the way. I met a bunch of them. I should keep a list. I have a seekers list on Twitter which is open, which tracks not necessarily enlightened people, but that has a couple of those on there, but also people who are working towards it. They care about it. They're genuine seekers. That said, I'm not sure there's any such thing as progress because it's either you believe that you are a separate self or not. It's that simple. Not even believe. Believe is the wrong word. Belief is not strong enough.
turns.zh: 它是二元的。它是二元的，因为——这有点反直觉——它不是一条路径。它不像……哦，顺便说一句，Sadhguru 是另一个。我见过他们很多。我应该列个清单。我在 Twitter 上有一个公开的“求道者”列表，里面不一定都是开悟的人，但有几个是，还有正在努力的人。他们关心这个。他们是真正的求道者。话虽如此，我不确定是否有进步这回事，因为要么你相信你是一个独立的自我，要么你不相信。就这么简单。甚至不是相信。“相信”这个词不对。信念不够强。

turns.en: So, either you know that you're a separate self or you know that you are not. The reality is everyone's agnostic.
turns.zh: 所以，要么你知道自己是一个独立的自我，要么你知道自己不是。现实是每个人都是不可知论者。

turns.en: You know, there are very few actual atheists and very few actual religious people. If you were actually religious, you'd just be like, 'Well, everything's in God's hands, everything's fine.' And if you were actually an atheist, you'd be like, 'Well, none of this matters, so everything's fine.' Right? But we're all agnostic.
turns.zh: 你知道，真正的无神论者和真正的宗教人士都很少。如果你真的是宗教人士，你会说，“好吧，一切都在上帝手中，一切都很好。”如果你真的是无神论者，你会说，“好吧，这一切都不重要，所以一切都很好。”对吧？但我们都是不可知论者。

turns.en: Do these people have like relationships and kids and...
turns.zh: 这些人有关系和孩子吗……

turns.en: Yeah, it's actually a great question, right? Because you think of first of all, the word enlightenment is horrible. It implies that there's some state you end up in where everything is perfect and you're always happy. It's not the case at all. It's just that you disappear and all that's left is... There's a great old philosophical puzzle called the philosophical zombie problem. I saw it referred to not necessarily, but it starts off with what is consciousness and all that. What is consciousness for? It's exactly that question. The philosophical zombie problem basically says: let's say you have to go through life, solve problems, react to the environment, think through things. What do you need consciousness for? Why can't there just be a robot doing all of that? Why do you need conscious awareness? A robot could be reacting to stimuli, solving problems, doing all those things. Consciousness is completely unnecessary. So the question is: why consciousness? And this is a very western concept. Why consciousness? And all the enlightened people would tell you: actually consciousness is the only thing there is. Everything appears in consciousness, is made of consciousness, disappears in consciousness, and is seen by consciousness. There's nothing other than consciousness. You don't experience anything in the outside world. You just experience consciousness as consciousness. So that's all there actually is. The actual consciousness observes everything, including your body and your mind. So it's not your body, not your mind. It's its own thing, and everything is made of it. So that's all that exists, and that's all you are. So they would reverse the answer and say: why zombie? You're asking why consciousness? No. The answer is: why zombie? There's no zombie. The zombie doesn't exist. There is no separate self. There's nothing else out there. So this is a completely different frame. In that sense, there is no enlightenment. There's no separate being to be enlightened. There's just life. There's just stuff happening. There are thoughts appearing and disappearing. They connect to each other, talk to each other. They're valid. They exist. There are bodies and minds walking around. And there is an awareness of those things. You are basically a localized awareness. What you think you are is localized awareness. This goes back to the oldest question that Robin and Marshy used to talk about: just who am I? The way the enlightened people—again, bad word—get there is they have a persistent experience of no self, and that becomes their new default. It is binary. It's not a pathway.
turns.zh: 是的，这其实是个好问题，对吧？因为首先，“开悟”这个词很糟糕。它暗示着某种最终状态，一切都完美，你永远快乐。根本不是这样。只是你消失了，剩下的只有……有一个古老的哲学谜题叫“哲学僵尸问题”。它不一定被直接引用，但它的起点是：什么是意识，等等。意识有什么用？正是这个问题。哲学僵尸问题基本上说：假设你必须经历生活，解决问题，对环境做出反应，思考事情。你需要意识做什么？为什么不能只是一个机器人在做这一切？为什么你需要有意识的觉知？机器人可以反应刺激、解决问题、做所有那些事情。意识完全是不必要的。所以问题是：为什么要有意识？这是一个非常西方的概念。为什么要有意识？而所有开悟的人会告诉你：实际上，意识是唯一存在的东西。一切都在意识中出现，由意识构成，在意识中消失，并被意识所看见。除了意识，别无他物。你并没有体验外部世界的任何东西。你只是体验意识作为意识。所以那就是实际存在的一切。真正的意识观察一切，包括你的身体和你的思想。所以它不是你的身体，也不是你的思想。它是自己的东西，一切由它构成。所以那就是存在的一切，那就是你的一切。所以他们会反过来回答：为什么要有僵尸？你在问为什么要有意识？不。答案是：为什么要有僵尸？没有僵尸。僵尸不存在。没有独立的自我。外面没有别的东西。所以这是一个完全不同的框架。在这个意义上，没有开悟。没有独立的个体需要被开悟。只有生命。只有事情在发生。有思想出现和消失。它们相互连接，相互交谈。它们是有效的。它们存在。有身体和思想在走动。并且有对这些事物的觉知。你基本上是一个局部的觉知。你认为自己是什么，就是局部的觉知。这回到了最古老的问题，罗宾和马什过去常说的：我到底是谁？开悟的人——再次强调，这个词不好——达到那种状态的方式是，他们持续体验到无我，这成为他们新的默认状态。它是二元的。不是一条路径。

turns.en: And one of the ways that some of them get there—some of them are just naturals, almost born that way. Some of them have a mystical experience. Some of them have a psychotic break. That's literally what Krishna already had, right? He had this thing called the process which kept coming back to him, and it just seems like a psychotic break plus his brother died and he went through a bad time. Some people get there through meditation, decades of meditation. Some people get there through just kind of devoting their life service to others. Their self disappears, they see through it. But the one that I found the most interesting was they just do what's called self-examination. Self-examination is just looking for the self, looking for this separate self. You're convinced you're Eric. I'm convinced I'm the wall. What do we mean by that? What is that? It's a very fuzzy definition. What is that exactly? Find me this Eric that's constantly talking about himself, referring to himself. Find me this Eric who suffers. Find me this Eric who feels pride. Find me this Eric who thrives. Find me this Eric who feels good. Find me this Eric who feels bad. Where is this guy hiding? Who is this? Look for that. And what you find is if you look, look, look, you can never pin it down. It's always on the edge of your vision. It's what Alan Watts described as: you take a burning stick and whirl it around, and it looks like you have a flaming wheel. You don't have a flaming wheel. What you have is a lot of thoughts going around that convince you there's this character in there called Eric. But there's no actual Eric. The self is just a thought. These are just thoughts that are happening that are convincing you there's someone there. So even when I'm speaking to you, there are words coming out and thoughts coming out. That's not me. They're just flying out. I'm not controlling them. There's no separate me sitting there controlling them. And then for a moment, I felt sort of proud of how I said this and articulated it to you. What is that feeling of pride? It's just a feeling. Who is that feeling referring to? It's not referring to anyone. There's no one it's referring to. It's just another thought. That pride was just another thought without a target. If I keep looking for that target long enough, then maybe I'll finally realize that target doesn't exist, or that target has never existed. And if that target has never existed, then eventually those thoughts that were going towards a fake target will start dying down because they're false, they're useless. That knowledge will no longer be replicated in my mind in the Deutschian sense. And then I will be at peace because I will no longer have these egotistical emotions and ups and downs, and I'll realize there was never anybody here and it was all just consciousness all along. And some people call that God.
turns.zh: 其中一些人达到那种状态的方式——有些人是天生的，几乎生来如此。有些人有神秘体验。有些人有精神崩溃。克里希那穆提就是这样，对吧？他有一种叫“过程”的东西，不断回到他身上，看起来就像精神崩溃，加上他哥哥去世，他经历了一段艰难时期。有些人通过冥想达到，几十年的冥想。有些人通过奉献一生服务他人达到。他们的自我消失了，他们看透了。但我发现最有趣的是，他们只是做所谓的“自我审视”。自我审视就是寻找自我，寻找这个独立的自我。你确信你是埃里克。我确信我是墙。我们是什么意思？那是什么？这是一个非常模糊的定义。那到底是什么？找到这个不断谈论自己、指代自己的埃里克。找到这个受苦的埃里克。找到这个感到骄傲的埃里克。找到这个茁壮成长的埃里克。找到这个感觉良好的埃里克。找到这个感觉糟糕的埃里克。这家伙藏在哪里？这是谁？去寻找。你会发现，如果你一直找，永远无法确定它。它总是在你视野的边缘。就像艾伦·瓦茨描述的：你拿着一根燃烧的棍子旋转，看起来像有一个火轮。你没有火轮。你有的是一大堆思绪在旋转，让你相信里面有一个叫埃里克的角色。但并没有真正的埃里克。自我只是一个念头。这些只是正在发生的念头，让你相信那里有一个人。所以即使我在对你说话，有词语和思绪出来。那不是我。它们只是飞出来。我没有控制它们。没有一个独立的我坐在那里控制它们。然后有一刻，我为自己这样说并清晰地表达给你而感到有点骄傲。那种骄傲的感觉是什么？只是一种感觉。那种感觉指向谁？它不指向任何人。它没有指向任何人。它只是另一个念头。那个骄傲只是另一个没有目标的念头。如果我足够长时间地寻找那个目标，也许我最终会意识到那个目标不存在，或者那个目标从未存在过。如果那个目标从未存在过，那么那些指向虚假目标的念头最终会开始消失，因为它们是假的，是无用的。那种知识将不再在我的头脑中复制，用戴维·多伊奇的话说。然后我会平静下来，因为我不再会有这些自我情绪和起伏，我会意识到这里从来没有人，一直只有意识。有些人称之为上帝。

turns.en: It's going to take me a while to grok all that. But that's the point.
turns.zh: 我需要一段时间才能完全理解这一切。但这就是重点。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, maybe some people are very lucky, it happens for them right away, and most people never want it, so it's fine.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，也许有些人非常幸运，它立刻发生在他们身上，而大多数人从不想要它，所以没关系。

turns.en: Yeah. Do you... the truly enlightened people... if you ask them like... I've gone through this with all of them in different ways, but I've tested them.
turns.zh: 是的。你……那些真正开悟的人……如果你问他们……我和他们所有人都以不同方式经历过这个，但我测试过他们。

turns.en: I'm kind of a smartass about this, but I've tested them to see if they feel emotional pain in any way, right? And my favorite way to test them is subtle, where they don't know it's obvious. The one I like to ask is: what if you weren't enlightened? What if you lost your enlightenment? Or what if you had never been enlightened? They're completely okay with that, because the truth doesn't change if you know it or not. And if you are not around to know the truth, if you don't exist in any separate sense, it doesn't matter if you know it or not.
turns.zh: 我在这件事上有点自作聪明，但我测试过他们，看他们是否会在任何方面感到情感痛苦。我最喜欢的测试方式是微妙的，他们不会察觉。我喜欢问的一个问题是：如果你没有开悟呢？如果你失去了开悟呢？或者如果你从未开悟呢？他们完全能接受，因为真理不会因为你知道与否而改变。如果你不在场去知道真理，如果你在任何独立意义上都不存在，那么你知道与否都无关紧要。

turns.en: Yeah. And if it breaks under that question, then it was false. It was part of your identity to the enlightened.
turns.zh: 是的。如果它在那个问题下崩溃了，那它就是假的。那是你身份中属于开悟的部分。

turns.en: That's right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Interesting.
turns.zh: 有意思。

turns.en: But going back to your question about whether they can still go about their lives, have girlfriends, businesses, and jobs. Absolutely they can. They do actually. Some of the people I mentioned have jobs, one is running a company, one is really good at dating. They have full clarity of mind, fully functional bodies. They even have desires, wants, and needs, but they're not egoic. They're not wrapped up in a small sense of self that gets easily threatened, angry, or depressed. They'll take the actions you and I would take, but much more calmly and they won't take anything personally. They still get feedback from the environment and react. If anything, they're more capable than ever, and their desires are more genuine, less mimetic, less status-oriented. Are they less motivated than someone like me? Yeah, I haven't met an enlightened person as motivated as me. But I haven't met many people as motivated as me. Maybe I've met five people in my life who are more motivated. So it's hard to intersect that set.
turns.zh: 但回到你的问题，他们是否还能继续生活，有女朋友、生意和工作？绝对可以。他们确实如此。我提到的一些人有工作，一个在经营公司，一个很擅长约会。他们头脑完全清晰，身体功能健全。他们甚至有欲望、想要和需要，但没有自我。他们没有被一个容易受威胁、生气或抑郁的小我包裹。他们会采取你我会采取的行动，但更冷静，不会把任何事情个人化。他们仍然从环境中获得反馈并做出反应。如果说有什么不同，他们比以往更有能力，他们的欲望更真实，更少模仿，更少关注地位。他们是否比我这样动力更强的人动力更弱？是的，我还没见过像我一样有动力的开悟者。但我也没见过多少像我一样有动力的人。也许我一生中只遇到过五个比我更有动力的人。所以很难有交集。

turns.en: And would I take that trade, being slightly less motivated for being... Sure. I was going to ask if you, having been so close, are seeking that or if you feel like it's impossible not to seek it even though I know that's a trap. It's like the shiniest of shiny objects. Why would you want to start the next great company when you could be the Buddha, when you could be God himself? The greatest people in human history that everyone knows are Jesus, Muhammad, Buddha. It's not even the Caesars of the world, maybe the Einsteins. But essentially truth seekers. Truth seekers are the most revered, and artists. History remembers artists and truth seekers. And the truth-seeking artists are the highest of them all, like Da Vinci. That said, it's another chase for something permanent, another shiny object, a chase for things being different than they are. The key thing about enlightened people is that they are fine with everything the way it is, which includes not being fine with the way things are. They self-actualize.
turns.zh: 我会接受这个交换吗，动力稍弱一点来换取……当然。我本来想问，你离得这么近，是否在追求那个，或者你是否觉得不可能不去追求，尽管我知道那是个陷阱。它就像最闪亮的物体。你为什么想创办下一家伟大的公司，当你可以成为佛陀，成为上帝本身？人类历史上最伟大、人人皆知的人物是耶稣、穆罕默德、佛陀。甚至不是凯撒之流，也许是爱因斯坦。但本质上是真理追寻者。真理追寻者最受尊敬，还有艺术家。历史记住的是艺术家和真理追寻者。而追寻真理的艺术家是其中最崇高的，比如达芬奇。尽管如此，这是另一种追逐，追逐某种永恒的东西，另一个闪亮的物体，追逐事物变得不同。开悟者的关键在于他们对一切如其所是感到满意，这包括对事物现状的不满意。他们自我实现。

turns.en: They may self-actualize in more authentic ways that are different from how they would have self-actualized pre-enlightenment, but they still self-actualize.
turns.zh: 他们可能以更真实的方式自我实现，不同于开悟前的自我实现方式，但他们仍然自我实现。

transcript.sec: Breaking false dichotomies: happiness and effectiveness

turns.en: I'm really interested in the ways you've broken these false dichotomies. You mentioned one just there: the enlightened and unmotivated are potentially more effective. You've gotten more effective as you've become happier, even though everyone has this fear that if I become happy or accept what is, I'll be less productive.
turns.zh: 我真的很感兴趣你打破这些错误二分法的方式。你刚才提到一个：开悟且缺乏动力的人可能更有效率。你随着变得更快乐而变得更有效率，尽管每个人都有这种恐惧：如果我变得快乐或接受现状，我会变得不那么高效。

turns.en: Yeah. What would you rather battle? A terminator that is enlightened or a terminator that is wrapped in an ego? A terminator wrapped in ego is much easier to manipulate. Might have guilt, self-doubt, anger, will be less efficient. A terminator that is enlightened is a true terminator. You need to get away from that right away. It's interesting. There is a place for emotions. Modern society is very safe, so a lot of the anger we feel is derived from times when anger was there for physical violence or physical reasons. But we now take it on as mental. A lot of modern suffering comes from feeling things chronically that were meant to be felt acutely for brief periods. So I don't think you are less effective because you're happier. You will choose to do different things. So it may look like you're changing for the worse because you may look less motivated or motivated for a different thing, but you're motivated for something that is going to make you more effective while you're being happy. By the way, no one chooses to go back to being less happy.
turns.zh: 是的。你更愿意与什么战斗？一个开悟的终结者还是一个被自我包裹的终结者？被自我包裹的终结者更容易被操纵。可能有内疚、自我怀疑、愤怒，效率更低。一个开悟的终结者是真正的终结者。你需要立刻远离它。这很有趣。情绪有其位置。现代社会非常安全，所以我们感受到的很多愤怒源于愤怒曾用于身体暴力或身体原因的时代。但现在我们把它当作心理上的。很多现代痛苦来自于长期感受那些本应短暂强烈感受的事情。所以我不认为你因为更快乐而效率更低。你会选择做不同的事情。所以可能看起来你在变糟，因为你看起来动力不足或为不同的事情而动力十足，但你为某件事而动力十足，这件事会让你在快乐的同时更有效率。顺便说一句，没有人选择回到不那么快乐的状态。

turns.en: Right. Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。是的。

turns.en: No, it's just like money. No one chooses to go back to being poor.
turns.zh: 不，就像钱一样。没有人选择回到贫穷。

turns.en: Strictly dominant strategy. Correct.
turns.zh: 严格占优策略。正确。

turns.en: To become happy.
turns.zh: 变得快乐。

turns.en: That's right. These are strictly dominant strategies. These are unalloyed goods. So when people say, 'I don't value happiness,' that's just not true. Because every time you've been happy, you don't want to give that up. And every time you do something that makes you a little bit happier, you're not going to give that up. As you go through life, there's a point where you have roommates and you get to not having roommates, you don't want to go back. But then you have kids, the worst and best roommates. It kind of sucks when the kids grow up and leave too. You don't want to go back to commutes. If you've had long commutes and then you get to walk to work or have a short commute, it sucks to go back. Retirement or working for yourself. These are one-way doors. It's very hard to come back through those doors.
turns.zh: 没错。这些是严格占优策略。这些是纯粹的善。所以当人们说“我不重视幸福”时，那是不对的。因为每次你快乐时，你都不想放弃它。每次你做让你更快乐一点的事情，你都不会放弃它。随着你经历人生，有一个阶段你有室友，然后你不再有室友，你不想回去。但然后你有了孩子，最差和最好的室友。孩子长大离开时也有点糟糕。你不想回到通勤。如果你有过长时间通勤，然后你开始步行上班或通勤很短，回去很糟糕。退休或为自己工作。这些都是单向门。很难再通过那些门回来。

turns.en: I asked about the enlightened people in their relationships because a lot of people ask me what's something I disagree with you on. I think as a test to see if I'm just a complete involved sycophant. One of the things I wrestle with the most is this idea that only the individual ascends.
turns.zh: 我问了关于开悟者在关系中的情况，因为很多人问我有什么不同意你的地方。我认为这是测试我是否只是一个完全迎合的谄媚者。我最纠结的事情之一是只有个体才能提升这个想法。

turns.en: All of your experiences are individual and like objectively I understand the correctness of your perception as individual but I also know that so much of happiness in my lived experience and research comes from our relationships with the people around us. I have to imagine there's a lot of nuance there. You can't control them but you are a participant in these relationships and they do affect your day-to-day happiness.
turns.zh: 你所有的经历都是个人的，客观上我理解你作为个体的感知是正确的，但我也知道，在我的生活经验和研究中，幸福很大程度上来自我们与周围人的关系。我想这其中一定有很多细微之处。你无法控制他们，但你是这些关系的参与者，它们确实会影响你日常的幸福。

turns.en: Yeah, we will disagree because I think that it's very difficult to be happy if you're bound up in needy relationships and just needing the relationship is needy. Now, obviously, I have my family and I love them very much and they're very important to me, but I'm not going to stay in any relationship that makes me unhappy. I will do my duty. I'll fulfill my obligations. I'll take care of them even if they don't like me. But I'm not going to create or sustain a false relationship. And this is truth. Every relationship you're in, you're in because that person provides some value for you or because your identity is tied up and you have some obligation towards them. And that's fine. But if your wife changed tomorrow, if she was inhabited by a demon who just did everything horrible to you, you would leave, right? So every relationship other than pure blood relationships which are different—they run on a genetic level. You know, you see your kids and you just go nuts in a good way. Every relationship is transactional. It does have a value exchange component. It does have an expectation of give and take. And it's kind of, you know, it sounds good but not true, but false. And this is where truth is hard. If truth was easy everyone would do it, right? But this is where these false relationships and these false obligations are tough. So like I don't go to my friends' birthdays unless I think the party is going to be fun. I don't go to weddings, for example, because I think they're largely just enormous amounts of time taken out of your day, you know, of your week—destination weddings. Oh my god, right. I don't go to obligatory things like, 'Oh, so and so is having an anniversary, so and so is having this thing, you have to go,' or 'These people need you there for this.' F no. That's where I draw the hard line. No obligations. And relationships drive you into obligations, and then the obligations drive you into pain. Now you're not having the pleasant part of the relationships. Now I'm left with in my life people who are similarly free, similarly low ego. We hang out all the time, but it's voluntarily. We all want to see each other and we're all being at our best to each other because we want to attract the other person. We don't take each other for granted, which is the worst thing that can happen in a relationship. You have to invest in all your relationships because you want to keep them alive. If you value them, you invest in them. But there's no false attachment. You know, it's the neediness that destroys a relationship or creates a fake relationship. Obviously, to have family, you have obligations and you fulfill them. And I actually don't have a single estranged family member. Everyone is in my life. Everyone in family. So, you know, it's not one of those 'your family's broken, you fix the world' situations. The family's functioning really well, but at the same time, you have to be happy on your own. You know, the secret to a happy relationship is two happy people. Okay? Because you can't be happy with your spouse. They'll drag you down or you'll drag them down. So it's important that each person find happiness. And happiness is personal. They each have to work on it. The worst thing you can do in a relationship is go to somebody else and say, 'Cheer up. Be happy. What's wrong with you? Why are you depressed?' Right? It doesn't work, right? So it has to be tackled at an individual level and then once your own house is in order then you can form genuine relationships with other people. But putting relationships ahead of your own work and your own happiness I think is a mistake. You will get neither.
turns.zh: 是的，我们会意见不同，因为我认为如果你被有需求的关系束缚，仅仅需要关系本身就是有需求的，那很难幸福。显然，我有家人，我非常爱他们，他们对我很重要，但我不会留在任何让我不幸福的关系里。我会尽我的责任，履行我的义务，即使他们不喜欢我，我也会照顾他们。但我不会创造或维持一段虚假的关系。这是事实。你身处每一段关系，都是因为那个人为你提供了某种价值，或者因为你的身份与之绑定，你对他们有某种义务。这没问题。但如果你的妻子明天变了，她被一个恶魔附身，对你做尽坏事，你会离开，对吧？所以除了纯粹的血缘关系——那不同，它基于基因层面——每一段关系都是交易性的。它确实有价值交换的成分，有给予和接受的期望。这听起来不错，但并不真实，是虚假的。而这就是真理艰难的地方。如果真理容易，每个人都会去做，对吧？但正是这些虚假的关系和虚假的义务让人难受。所以，我不会去参加朋友的生日派对，除非我觉得派对会很有趣。比如我不去婚礼，因为我认为它们基本上就是浪费你一天、甚至一周的大量时间——尤其是目的地婚礼。天哪，对吧。我不去那些义务性的场合，比如‘某某在庆祝纪念日，某某有这事，你必须去’，或者‘这些人需要你在场’。绝不。这就是我划清界限的地方。没有义务。关系会把你推向义务，而义务又会把你推向痛苦。这样你就无法享受关系中愉快的部分。现在，我生活中留下的是那些同样自由、同样低自我的人。我们经常聚在一起，但都是自愿的。我们都想见到彼此，并且都以最好的状态对待对方，因为我们想吸引对方。我们不把对方视为理所当然，这是关系中最糟糕的事情。你必须投资于你所有的关系，因为你希望它们保持活力。如果你珍视它们，你就投资它们。但没有虚假的依恋。是需求感摧毁了关系，或者创造了虚假的关系。显然，有家庭就有义务，你履行它们。实际上，我没有一个疏远的家庭成员。每个人都在我的生活中。所有家人都在。所以，这不是那种‘你的家庭破碎了，你去修复世界’的情况。家庭运作得很好，但同时，你必须自己幸福。你知道，幸福关系的秘诀是两个幸福的人。好吗？因为你无法通过配偶获得幸福。他们会拖累你，或者你会拖累他们。所以每个人找到自己的幸福很重要。幸福是个人的。他们每个人都必须为此努力。在关系中你能做的最糟糕的事情就是去找别人说：‘振作起来。开心点。你怎么了？为什么抑郁？’对吧？这没用，对吧？所以必须从个人层面解决，一旦你自己的事情理顺了，你才能与他人建立真正的关系。但把关系置于你自己的工作和幸福之前，我认为是个错误。你会两者皆失。

turns.en: I think that's a good formulation of like they are valuable and important. You want to pursue them in that order. And if I'm describing it correctly your approach, you want them to be maximally truthful. Like you don't want to go into a relationship trying to make someone else happy.
turns.zh: 我认为这是一个很好的表述：它们是有价值和重要的，你想按那个顺序去追求。如果我描述得正确，你的方法是希望它们尽可能真实。就像你不想进入一段关系试图让别人幸福。

turns.en: And I don't want them to do things to make me happy that are dishonest. I don't want them to feel like I'm a burden or an obligation. You know, like one of the issues I have is, as a parent, I do things for my kids because I love them. And people around me are always trying to train them to say thank you. They'll say, 'Oh, your dad gave you something. Did you say thank you?' I'm like, 'Don't do that. Don't train them. They're not an animal.' If they genuinely feel gratitude, it'll come out in some way. I don't need it. I'm not doing it for the gratitude, right? And I don't want to train them like an animal. If they're genuinely thankful, it'll come out, you know? And if they're not, it won't. But don't force it. I don't want any forced obligations relationship in either direction.
turns.zh: 我也不希望他们为了让我幸福而做不诚实的事。我不希望他们觉得我是个负担或义务。你知道，我遇到的一个问题是，作为父母，我为孩子做事是因为我爱他们。我周围的人总是试图训练他们说谢谢。他们会说：‘哦，你爸爸给了你东西。你说谢谢了吗？’我说：‘别那样做。别训练他们。他们不是动物。’如果他们真心感激，它会以某种方式表达出来。我不需要它。我不是为了感激才做的，对吧？我不想像训练动物一样训练他们。如果他们真心感激，它会表现出来，你知道吗？如果不感激，就不会。但不要强迫。我不想要任何方向上的强迫义务关系。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: The only relationships I believe in are peer relationships. Obviously people work for you but even then you always want to treat them as much like a peer as you can given the circumstances, because otherwise it ends up formalized, it ends up ritualistic, it ends up false, it ends up unhappy. You know, it's funny, if you go to an event like a wedding or a big party, a lot of times you'll find a lot of show about happiness—people cheering, yeah—but the genuine article is rarely there. The genuine happiness comes in smaller interactions that are more genuine and less forced. If you're forced to wear a tux, forced to show up at a certain time, forced to listen to a speech, forced to do a toast, forced to go around—it's just not what actually makes you happy. If it genuinely made you happy, you would be doing that every day. You'd be going to every party and every wedding and every bar mitzvah and everything you get invited to. But at some level, those things are tedious. You're doing them for other people and they're doing them for you.
turns.zh: 我唯一相信的关系是平等关系。显然，有人为你工作，但即便如此，你总是希望尽可能把他们当作平等的人来对待，因为否则关系会变得形式化、仪式化、虚假、不幸福。你知道吗，有趣的是，如果你去参加像婚礼或大型派对这样的活动，很多时候你会发现很多关于幸福的表演——人们欢呼，是的——但真正的东西很少在那里。真正的幸福来自更小、更真实、更少强迫的互动。如果你被迫穿燕尾服，被迫在特定时间出现，被迫听演讲，被迫祝酒，被迫四处走动——那并不是真正让你幸福的东西。如果它真的让你幸福，你会每天都这样做。你会去每一个派对、每一场婚礼、每一个成人礼以及你被邀请的每一件事。但在某种程度上，那些事情是乏味的。你为别人做，他们也为你做。

turns.en: Like who's doing it for themselves, right? So I think true celebration is a beautiful thing but it has been ritualized so much like Valentine's Day, having to send flowers, Hallmark cards, all this stuff is ritualistic. Real happiness is when you're genuinely grateful with somebody else and you know you do something kind for them because you just want to and you have to create space for that in your life and you have to create situations where you feel it authentically for people to genuinely inspire that in you. I don't want anything ritualistic. I never want a card. I never want a ritualistic gift. I don't want a medal. I don't want an award. All these things are useless.
turns.zh: 就像谁是为了自己而做呢？所以我认为真正的庆祝是美好的，但它已经被过度仪式化了，比如情人节、必须送花、贺曼卡片，所有这些都只是形式。真正的幸福是当你真心感激某人，你为他们做善事只是因为你想做，你必须在生活中创造空间，创造能让你真实感受到这种情感的情境。我不想要任何形式化的东西。我从不想要卡片，不想要形式化的礼物，不想要奖章，不想要奖项。所有这些都没用。

turns.en: I was gonna ask you about helping people find the courage to be that truthful in their lives. But I think it just goes back to the long-term view like that the truth, even if it causes short-term pain of like, hey, I'm not going to do this to make you happy. I don't want you to do this just to make me happy, is the best way to create this long term.
turns.zh: 我本想问你关于帮助人们找到在生活中如此诚实的勇气。但我认为这又回到了长期视角：真相，即使带来短期痛苦，比如“我不会为了让你开心而做这个，我也不希望你只是为了让我开心而做这个”，是创造长期幸福的最佳方式。

turns.en: That's absolutely true. But I think it's even deeper than that. It's that truth is non-negotiable. Find me a single person who says I don't want the truth. That's a rare person, right?
turns.zh: 完全正确。但我认为这甚至更深层：真相是不可妥协的。你能找到一个说“我不想要真相”的人吗？那是很少见的，对吧？

turns.en: Well, the express preference I think there's probably plenty of people living choosing to live lies.
turns.zh: 嗯，就明确偏好而言，我认为可能有很多人选择活在谎言中。

turns.en: I think they don't want to disappoint other people and I think so we're all caught in this collective trap.
turns.zh: 我认为他们不想让别人失望，所以我们都被困在这个集体陷阱里。

turns.en: Right? Like one of the things COVID did that was good was it broke the collective trap of commuting, right? It legitimized work from home. Now some people might take it too far and they just checked out completely. But overall it broke society of its addiction to commuting to unnecessary commuting to unnecessary meetings and that was good. So I think the same way like it would be good if we can break society of its addiction to pomp and circumstance.
turns.zh: 对吧？就像新冠疫情做的一件好事是打破了通勤的集体陷阱，它让居家办公合法化。现在有些人可能做得太过，完全躺平了。但总的来说，它打破了社会对通勤、不必要的通勤和不必要会议的沉迷，这是好事。所以同样，如果我们能打破社会对排场和形式的沉迷，那也会很好。

turns.en: Right? Like Valentine's Day, that should be happening all the time with the person you love spontaneously and naturally instead of February 14th, everybody tries to get in the same overpriced restaurant and buys the same overpriced flowers and gives the same overpriced checks the same box, right? And if you don't feel like doing it naturally, then that means there's something wrong with your relationship that you should fix or you should move on to another relationship. But what's not the correct answer is to do this falsehood of checking the box every year and kicking the can down the road just wasting everybody's time.
turns.zh: 对吧？就像情人节，应该随时与你爱的人自发自然地发生，而不是在 2 月 14 日，每个人都挤进同一家昂贵的餐厅，买同样的高价花，送同样的高价支票，打同样的勾。如果你没有自然的感觉，那就意味着你们的关系有问题，你应该修复它，或者转向另一段关系。但正确的答案不是每年都做这种虚假的“打勾”行为，把问题踢到以后，浪费所有人的时间。

turns.en: Oh, I had this whole thread on how a lot of the great virtues are selfish. I can reinterpret almost every virtue as something you do for long-term selfishness. That's the trick, right? The modern devil is cheap dopamine.
turns.zh: 哦，我有一条完整的推文，讲很多伟大的美德都是自私的。我几乎可以把每一种美德重新解释为你为了长期自私而做的事情。这就是诀窍，对吧？现代的魔鬼是廉价的多巴胺。

turns.en: So I tweeted that and this was actually one of the reasons why I tweet because sometimes I get a great response. So, I tweeted, "The modern devil is cheap dopamine." And someone responded, "Always has been."
turns.zh: 所以我发了那条推文，这实际上是我发推的原因之一，因为有时我会得到很好的回应。我发了：“现代的魔鬼是廉价的多巴胺。”有人回复：“一直都是。”

turns.en: It's true. The devil has always been cheap dope. You go back the seven deadly sins, it's all cheap dopamine. It's basically saying, "Don't do something that makes you feel good right now because it's going to screw you over later." So on the flip side, one of the tweets that I sent out which I think Elon liked he commented on it was that virtues are the set of values that if we were to all take them on individually would lead to a win-win outcome for society. So the virtues are things that are long-term good for you as an individual and which is selfish long-term selfish and they're long-term good for the collective because they allow us to play iterated prisoners dilemma or to play stag iterated stag hunt kind of games in a positive equilibrium from a game theory perspective that lead to good game theoretic outcomes. If everybody in society followed even the classic biblical virtues, all society would level up and each individual would be better off. So that's true. But I also think that all the virtues if you were to follow them as an individual on a long enough time frame, they would make you individually better off. So it's the ultimate marshmallow test. By the way, that experiment doesn't replicate. So, sorry to bring it up, but it's one of those behavioral psych experiments everybody knows. But it is in some sense a lot of a good life is the marshmallow test.
turns.zh: 没错。魔鬼一直是廉价毒品。回顾七宗罪，全都是廉价的多巴胺。它基本上是在说：“不要做让你现在感觉良好但以后会搞砸你的事。”所以反过来，我发的一条推文，我想埃隆·马斯克喜欢并评论了，那就是：美德是一套价值观，如果我们每个人都采纳它们，就会为社会带来双赢的结果。所以美德是对你个人长期有利的东西，是长期自私的，而且对集体长期有利，因为它们让我们能够玩迭代囚徒困境或迭代猎鹿游戏，从博弈论角度看，达到正均衡，产生好的博弈论结果。如果社会中的每个人都遵循甚至经典的圣经美德，整个社会都会提升，每个个体也会更好。这是真的。但我也认为，所有美德，如果你作为个体在足够长的时间框架内遵循它们，它们会让你个人过得更好。所以这是终极的棉花糖测试。顺便说一句，那个实验无法重复。所以抱歉提出来，但这是每个人都知道的行为心理学实验之一。但从某种意义上说，美好生活很大程度上就是棉花糖测试。

turns.en: And there are a lot of things that people think that you have to do it the standard way. Like philanthropy is a good example, right? You have to go volunteer for charity and help out people who are far away. Well, how about helping out people who are right next to you, right? People who live down the street, people who work for you, people who work with you. Generally helping out those people is selfishly very rewarding. You can get some benefit back from it. So, it's a good place to start with charity. It's very local, very actionable. So, your money is not being wasted. You're not being tricked. It's not being redirected. And it's just a way to do something practical.
turns.zh: 而且有很多事情人们认为必须按标准方式做。比如慈善就是一个好例子，对吧？你必须去志愿做慈善，帮助远方的人。那么，帮助身边人呢？比如住在街对面的人、为你工作的人、与你共事的人。通常帮助这些人从自私的角度看非常有益。你可以从中得到一些回报。所以，这是慈善的一个好起点。它非常本地化、非常可行。这样你的钱不会浪费，你不会被骗，不会被挪用。这只是做点实事的一种方式。

turns.en: Another tweet I had was your family is broken, but you're going to fix the world.
turns.zh: 我的另一条推文是：你的家庭一团糟，但你却要去拯救世界。

turns.en: Right? This is the whole modern activism disease where you're agitating about causes that are thousands of miles away that you've never laid eyes on based on what you hear because they're socially popular. But the reality is you could just go walk down the street and help the homeless person or the vet or the poor person looking for a job or what have you or literally go make amends with your uncle or your parents. What right do you have to say I know what's right to fix the world when you can't even get your local family going? Right? It's okay to help people on the other side of the world, but don't agitate for saying, "I know what's correct." When you can't even solve your own local problems. You get the right to solve global problems after you solve local problems. That's true in environmentalism, too. Everybody's worried about CO2. Well, what about rivers and forests and so on?
turns.zh: 对吧？这就是现代激进主义的通病：你为千里之外、从未亲眼见过的事情而激动，只因为听说它们在社会上流行。但现实是，你可以走到街对面帮助流浪汉、退伍军人、找工作的穷人等等，或者干脆去和你的叔叔或父母和解。当你连自己的家庭都搞不定时，你有什么资格说“我知道如何拯救世界”？对吧？帮助世界另一端的人没问题，但不要激动地说“我知道什么是对的”。当你连自己的本地问题都解决不了时。在解决本地问题之后，你才有资格解决全球问题。环保主义也是如此。每个人都担心二氧化碳。那么河流、森林等等呢？

turns.en: This goes back to your tweet, the greater your ability, the greater your tribe. And the sort of iterative loop of resource allocation where society gives more trust and more leverage and more influence to people who are doing a good job of taking care of the people around them.
turns.zh: 这又回到了你的推文：能力越大，部落越大。以及资源分配的迭代循环：社会将更多的信任、杠杆和影响力给予那些善于照顾身边人的人。

turns.en: Yeah. Somebody asked me about Elon long time ago on Twitter. This is years and years ago before he was the richest man in the world and all that.
turns.zh: 是的。很久以前有人在推特上问过我关于埃隆·马斯克的事。那是很多很多年前，在他成为世界首富之前。

transcript.sec: On the Power of a Mission Larger Than Self

turns.en: Uh and somebody said, "What do you think of Elon?" And I said, well, you know, when someone devotes their entire life to giving society what it needs, then society has no choice but to give them everything they want, right? But you can't rely on that. You can't do it that selfishly or count that closely. But ultimately because his desire was pure regardless of what people say about Elon today, forgetting with Elon, but when you have aspirations that are larger than yourself, then other people will align with you because there are other people who feel that your motives are pure. I want that thing too. So let's line up and you become a Schelling point. In game theory sense, you become a rallying cry for other people who want to see that thing to mess around. You know in that sense if you say I want to go to Mars I want to make humans a spacefaring species. Well there is a large percentage of our technical builder population that shares that same vision that are brilliant that are felt stymied that now see a vehicle to do that. So they will literally go and build a vehicle under SpaceX to go do that. So by having that mission be larger than about yourself. You assemble people. Similar like Martin Luther King when he says I have a dream speech it's very inclusive. It's very high level. It's very unifying. It's universal. And so it pulls people together. It's also the reason why Marxism has such a seductive appeal because, you know, Marx says, "No, it's a blank slate. We're all equal. We're all the same. Holy Spirit, it's all consciousness. Everybody get together." It's a religious pull towards universality. So the more universal the mission, the more people it's going to pull together regardless of whether it's effective or not. That's sort of the irony here.
turns.zh: 有人问我：“你怎么看埃隆？”我说，当一个人把毕生精力都用来给社会提供它需要的东西时，社会就别无选择，只能给他想要的一切，对吧？但你不能依赖这个，不能那么自私地去做，也不能算得那么精确。但归根结底，因为他的愿望是纯粹的——不管今天人们怎么说埃隆，先不提他——当你拥有超越自身的抱负时，其他人就会与你对齐，因为会有其他人觉得：你的动机是纯粹的，我也想要那个东西。所以让我们站在一起，你就成了一个谢林点。从博弈论的角度说，你成了其他想做成那件事的人的集结号。比如你说“我要去火星，我要让人类成为星际物种”，那么有很大一部分技术建造者群体——他们才华横溢、感到受挫——现在看到了实现这一目标的载体，就会真的去 SpaceX 下面造一个飞行器来实现它。所以，通过让使命超越自身，你就能聚集人才。类似马丁·路德·金发表“我有一个梦想”演讲时，它非常包容、非常高层、非常统一、非常普世，因此把人们凝聚在一起。这也是马克思主义具有如此诱人吸引力的原因，因为马克思说：“不，它是一块白板，我们都是平等的，我们都一样，圣灵，全是意识，大家团结起来。”这是一种对普世性的宗教式吸引。所以使命越普世，它聚集的人就越多，不管它是否有效。这有点讽刺。

turns.en: Yeah. Well, this gets to the memetics of an idea: it will spread relative to its spreadability, not relative to its correctness.
turns.zh: 是的。这涉及到思想的模因学：它会根据其可传播性来传播，而不是根据其正确性。

turns.en: It will spread according to its spreadability, but then it'll survive according to its correctness. So, a civilization that adopts a bad idea because of its good spread characteristics wipes itself off the map, kind of like the Soviet Union did. And so, you're left with Russia now, but it's not the Soviet Union. It's completely different structure. For example, the Chinese Communist Party, you know when it was under Mao and they made the Great Leap Forward and there was all that suffering and starvation, more Chinese people died during that event than all the casualties in World War II from all people put together — not the Chinese just Chinese deaths — Great Leap Forward versus all casualties in all causes in World War II, right? So that just shows you what a bad idea can do. But then how did they survive? Well, they switched to capitalism, they started having local farms that could — it's a government-controlled capitalism, but essentially they created a capitalist section to their economy with private farming and eventually with private enterprises that are still backed by state money, but the more privatized they became, the faster they came up. So eventually you have to adopt good ideas or good explanations because you'll just get wiped out. The skin in the game will eliminate you. So you're right, bad ideas can spread and unfortunately the way those bad ideas get taken out is not that we necessarily learn our lessons so much, but it's that the entities, the people, the institutions that had those bad ideas don't survive. They get eliminated.
turns.zh: 它会根据可传播性传播，但会根据正确性存活。所以，一个文明如果因为一个坏思想有好的传播特性而采纳它，就会把自己从地图上抹掉，有点像苏联那样。结果现在剩下俄罗斯，但它不是苏联了，结构完全不同。例如，中国共产党在毛泽东领导下搞大跃进，造成了那么多苦难和饥荒，在那次事件中死去的中国人比二战中所有国家所有原因造成的伤亡总数还要多——不是中国人，只是中国人在大跃进中的死亡，对比二战中所有原因的所有伤亡，对吧？这正好说明一个坏思想能造成什么后果。但他们怎么存活下来的呢？他们转向了资本主义，开始搞地方农场——这是一种政府控制的资本主义，但本质上他们在经济中创造了一个资本主义部分，有私人农业，最终还有私人企业，虽然仍由国家资金支持，但他们越私有化，发展得越快。所以最终你必须采纳好思想或好解释，否则就会被淘汰。置身事外会消灭你。所以你说得对，坏思想可以传播，但不幸的是，这些坏思想被清除的方式，不一定是我们吸取了教训，而是那些拥有坏思想的实体、个人、机构无法存活，它们被淘汰了。

turns.en: So you really want to be an entity with a bunch of correct ideas so you don't get eliminated.
turns.zh: 所以你确实想成为一个拥有大量正确思想的实体，这样才不会被淘汰。

turns.en: Yeah. My pseudospiritual take on Deutsch's definition of knowledge, which he always laughs about when he mentions because he knows I'm into it and it has a pseudospiritual context to it which he's not into, is that truth is a crystal in the multiverse. So if you go with the multiverse theory of quantum physics, which is the most direct interpretation — if you basically believe there's nothing special about an observer that causes the collapse and that observers unobserved and the observed are all the same, then the most straightforward interpretation is that the multiverse theory naturally emerges from that. Then it says: just because I look at something, just because I look at the cat, doesn't mean that the cat is alive or dead in Schrödinger's cat. It's that the cat was dead in some parts of the multiverse and it was alive in other parts of the multiverse. And I just didn't know which universe I was in until I opened the box. And if you take that interpretation, then there is a multiverse of outcomes. There's an infinite number of universes that are always differentiating into subsections based on: did the particle go left or did it go right? Did this happen or did that happen? And there are various things that can cause those universes to diverge. But if you were to look at the content of those universes, knowledge is a thing that tends to spread because it's correct. Truth survives because it matches up. You can do things with truth. You avoid falsehoods. So there are more universes in which the true thing is true than the falsehood, because falsehoods are infinitely variable. For every truth I have, there's unlimited false explanations. There is a finite number of truth explanations. Relatively speaking, you can have mostly true explanations. You never get the absolute truth. But the set of true explanations is much smaller than the set of falsehoods. However, once the truth is found, because it is hard to vary and because it spreads, the falsehood gets eliminated. So if there's a multiverse and there are infinite versions of you — and again I think there's a pretty straightforward interpretation of quantum physics — if there's a multiverse and there's infinite versions of you, then the ones that are flourishing the most and the ones that are most alike are actually the most true versions of you. The ones that have found the most truth. The false ones are differentiated in all kinds of ways. But the true ones are more similar to each other. And the truth that each of them have realized that cuts through is more similar than all the variations of falseness. So what you want to be is you want to be the most truth-oriented version of yourself and that is the most true version of itself. And now we can segue into Rick and Morty because in Rick and Morty there's infinite Ricks, right? There's infinite universes, there's infinite Ricks and Rick is a hero scientist. And there's this council of Ricks where the infinite Ricks because they can cross across the multiverse.
turns.zh: 是的。我对多伊奇的知识定义有一个伪灵性的理解——他每次提到这个都会笑，因为他知道我喜欢这个，而且它有他不太喜欢的伪灵性背景——那就是：真理是多元宇宙中的一颗水晶。如果你接受量子物理的多元宇宙理论，这是最直接的诠释——如果你基本上相信观察者没有什么特别之处会导致坍缩，而且未被观察的观察者和被观察者都是一样的，那么最直接的诠释就是多元宇宙理论自然从中产生。它说：仅仅因为我看了某样东西，仅仅因为我看了那只猫，并不意味着猫在薛定谔的猫中是死是活。而是猫在多元宇宙的某些部分是死的，在其他部分是活的。我只是不知道自己在哪个宇宙，直到我打开盒子。如果你接受这个诠释，那么就有多元宇宙的结果。有无限个宇宙，它们总是根据“粒子向左还是向右？这件事发生了还是那件事发生了？”而分化成子部分。有各种事情可以导致这些宇宙分叉。但如果你看这些宇宙的内容，知识是一种倾向于传播的东西，因为它是正确的。真理存活下来，因为它匹配得上。你可以用真理做事。你避开谬误。所以真理为真的宇宙比谬误为真的宇宙更多，因为谬误是无限可变的。对于每一个真理，我有无限个错误解释。真理解释的数量是有限的。相对而言，你可以有大部分正确的解释。你永远得不到绝对真理。但正确解释的集合比谬误的集合小得多。然而，一旦真理被发现，因为它难以改变且会传播，谬误就会被消除。所以如果存在多元宇宙，并且有无限个版本的你——我再次认为量子物理有一个相当直接的诠释——如果存在多元宇宙，并且有无限个版本的你，那么那些最繁荣、最相似的版本实际上是你最真实的版本。那些找到了最多真理的版本。虚假的版本以各种方式分化。但真实的版本彼此更相似。而每个版本所领悟到的、穿透一切的真理，比所有虚假的变体更相似。所以你想成为的，是那个最以真理为导向的自我版本，那也就是最真实的自我版本。现在我们可以转到《瑞克和莫蒂》了，因为在《瑞克和莫蒂》里有无限个瑞克，对吧？有无限个宇宙，有无限个瑞克，瑞克是一个英雄科学家。还有一个瑞克委员会，因为无限个瑞克可以穿越多元宇宙。

turns.en: They meet up and one of the funny things you notice is that Rick C137, which is the hero of the story, he's like the most... he's Rick, he's weird, but he's the most normal of the Ricks. The rest of them are all weird in some way. They're all false in some way. He and they even call him, they say he's the Rickest of the Ricks, right? So, I don't know if they thought this through or if they stumbled upon it or if it's just another principle that gets in here, but I view that as like a funny example of the version of yourself that you want to be. You want to be the Rickest of the Ricks. I want to be the most naive of all. You want to be the most Eric of the Erics. And that's the most truth-oriented version of yourself, and to find truth it means the least motivated reasoning, which means the lowest ego, which means the least self. Which means the one who's operating at the highest level for the greatest, most universal principles. It's the one who is the most knowledgeable about science and about philosophy and is probably the one who's most likely to be creating works of beauty and is just probably the happiest.
turns.zh: 他们聚在一起时，你会发现一件有趣的事：故事主角 Rick C137 是最 Rick 的，他很怪，但他是所有 Rick 中最正常的。其他 Rick 都在某些方面很古怪，都在某些方面很虚假。他们甚至称他为“Rick 中的 Rick”，对吧？我不知道这是他们深思熟虑的结果，还是偶然发现的，或者只是另一个原则，但我认为这是一个有趣的例子，说明你想成为什么样的自己。你想成为 Rick 中的 Rick。我想成为最天真的那个。你想成为 Eric 中的 Eric。那是最真实导向的自我，而要找到真理，就意味着最少的动机性推理，最低的自我，最少的自我。也就是那个在最高层面为最伟大、最普遍的原则运作的人。那个最懂科学、最懂哲学的人，很可能也是最能创造美的人，而且很可能最快乐。

turns.en: How do you think about the interaction between lowering of ego and finding authenticity? Like I think some people think of their identity as like the things that make them unique, and authenticity also as like leaning into the things that make you unique.
turns.zh: 你怎么看待降低自我与找到真实性之间的相互作用？我觉得有些人把身份认同视为让自己独特的东西，而真实性也是拥抱那些让你独特的东西。

turns.en: I think of authenticity as just not putting on a fake persona, not having a mask. And why do we put on fake personas and masks? It's because we want to be seen a certain way. We care what other people think. We want to fit in. We have an image and an identity that we're trying to project. So when you're trying to project a self-image that doesn't match up to who you actually are, that's when you are inauthentic. So you need to take that mask off. And if you take that mask off, then you face the risk of embarrassment, which is people say, "Oh, you know, you're not supposed to do that." Like when I was first tweeting, I got messages from VCs saying, "Oh, that's weird. My partner says you're not backable. You're a weirdo. You've gone off the rails." I view authenticity as an output of not pretending to be someone or something other than you are.
turns.zh: 我认为真实性就是不装假，不戴面具。我们为什么要装假、戴面具？因为我们想以某种方式被看待。我们在乎别人怎么想。我们想融入。我们有一个想要投射的形象和身份。所以当你试图投射一个与真实自我不符的自我形象时，你就是不真实的。所以你需要摘掉面具。如果你摘掉面具，就会面临被尴尬的风险，人们会说：“哦，你不该那样做。”就像我第一次发推文时，收到风投的消息说：“哦，那很奇怪。我的合伙人说你不可投资。你是个怪人。你走偏了。”我认为真实性是不假装成别人或别的东西的结果。

turns.en: So, that's wearing masks. That's trying too hard. That's signaling status. That's using jargon to sound smart. That's claiming to know something that you don't know or claiming not to know something you do know. Like humility is also a trap, like false humility, you know people call it out, humble bragging, but humility is also an ego trip. If you're actually low ego, there's no such thing as humility any more than there is such a thing as pride; they kind of go together. So authenticity leads to a better life again in the long term, like all the virtues. So it removes the people who are around that are only there because they think you're someone that you're not, so you can drop the pretense. You have to walk on eggshells around them. It removes you from situations where you're having to pretend or force or struggle or work hard to stay in. And so it helps you navigate to the right answers in relationships and in situations. But there's pain involved because there's change.
turns.zh: 所以，那就是戴面具。那就是太刻意。那就是炫耀地位。那就是用行话装聪明。那就是声称知道你不知道的东西，或者声称不知道你知道的东西。比如谦逊也是一个陷阱，像假谦逊，人们会指出来，叫“谦虚的炫耀”，但谦逊也是一种自我之旅。如果你真的低自我，就没有谦逊这回事，就像没有骄傲这回事一样；它们是一体的。所以，从长远来看，真实性会带来更好的生活，就像所有美德一样。它会赶走那些只因为以为你是别人而围在你身边的人，这样你就可以放下伪装。你不需要在他们面前小心翼翼。它会让你脱离那些需要假装、强迫、挣扎或努力维持的处境。所以它帮助你在关系和情境中找到正确的答案。但其中也有痛苦，因为涉及改变。

turns.en: You know, part of the thing is like yes, I'm lazy, but I will not accept bad outcomes. You know, I have an ability to walk away from suboptimal relationships or situations. Even if it's painful, I just won't stay in them.
turns.zh: 你知道，部分情况是，是的，我很懒，但我不会接受糟糕的结果。我有能力离开次优的关系或处境。即使很痛苦，我也不会留在里面。

turns.en: Is that only for a very specific subset of things that you choose to desire?
turns.zh: 那只是针对你选择渴望的非常特定的一小部分事情吗？

turns.en: It's for everything.
turns.zh: 适用于一切。

turns.en: How do you decide like where to focus that energy, those standards?
turns.zh: 你怎么决定把精力和标准放在哪里？

turns.en: That's a hard one. I mean, we're always falling into traps. Modern society has a lot of traps, dopamine traps, right? You can get sucked into politics. You can get sucked into drug use. You can get sucked into screen addiction, scrolling, porn, alcohol. There's just so many traps in modern society. They've legalized weed, which the libertarian me wants to say, okay, consenting adult, do whatever you want. But in reality, I think it just sucks the energy out of young men. Like, you can't be ambitious if you're stoned all the time. It'll literally destroy your ability and desire to go and do anything. And it's a band-aid. It's a band-aid that's covering things up. Which may be fine for society. It's the modern bread and circuses, but not so good for the individual. And I don't care about society. I care about the individual. Yes, society has to be functional. But I feel like modern society's given up on that. We're taking the wrong part of libertarianism, which is we basically said not only you're free to do anything you want, but as long as it doesn't immediately hurt somebody else, it should be celebrated. But then society as a whole kind of falls apart because everyone's a hedonist. No one has a long-term stake in the system. There's no shared ethos. There's no shared rules. There's no understanding of the basics. And then you end up with high crime. You end up with dirt. You end up with wasteful government. And the state becomes a substitute for religion. The religious instinct is so strong in people. Religion is a cooperating system for people that if you don't have a religious system, then the state will take over and create one. And I don't think the state's sense of virtue and ethics is much better than the older religions. The older religions had a better grasp of virtue and ethics. Now, they didn't modernize fast enough. So they were poorly adapted towards things like contraception and women entering the workforce and lesbian and gay rights and so on. But I think we threw the baby out with the bathwater and I don't think the religious instinct is gone. People are going to search for it no matter what. You had a great tweet that made me laugh: the problem with modern society is that atheism has reached the masses.
turns.zh: 这很难。我是说，我们总是掉进陷阱。现代社会有很多陷阱，多巴胺陷阱，对吧？你会被政治吸进去。你会被毒品吸进去。你会被屏幕成瘾、刷屏、色情、酒精吸进去。现代社会有太多陷阱了。他们让大麻合法化了，我内心的自由意志主义者想说，好吧，自愿的成年人，你想做什么就做什么。但实际上，我认为它吸走了年轻男性的能量。如果你一直嗨着，你就不可能雄心勃勃。它会彻底摧毁你去做任何事的能力和欲望。而且它是个创可贴。它是个掩盖问题的创可贴。这对社会可能没问题。这是现代的“面包与马戏”，但对个人不太好。我不在乎社会。我在乎个人。是的，社会必须运转。但我觉得现代社会已经放弃了这一点。我们走错了自由意志主义的方向，我们基本上说，不仅你可以自由做任何事，而且只要不立即伤害别人，就应该被庆祝。但然后整个社会就分崩离析了，因为每个人都是享乐主义者。没有人对系统有长期利益。没有共同的道德观。没有共同的规则。没有对基本问题的理解。然后你就得到了高犯罪率。你得到了肮脏。你得到了浪费的政府。国家成了宗教的替代品。人们的宗教本能非常强烈。宗教是人们的合作系统，如果你没有宗教系统，国家就会接管并创造一个。我不认为国家的美德和伦理感比旧宗教好多少。旧宗教对美德和伦理有更好的把握。当然，它们现代化不够快。所以它们对避孕、女性进入职场、同性恋权利等适应不良。但我认为我们把孩子和洗澡水一起倒掉了，我不认为宗教本能消失了。人们无论如何都会去寻找它。你有一条很棒的推文让我笑了：现代社会的问题是，无神论已经普及到了大众。

turns.en: Yeah, that's right. Yeah. I mean like atheism in the masses is really bad because people need an operating system and most people are not going to figure it out for themselves and it has to be a relatively common operating system. So if you don't have one you either end up with anarchy or end up with the state dictating it, which either means you're in an oligarchy or you're in a communist authoritarian hole. And related to that is like I think a fool believes religious beliefs completely and another fool just believes it has no value. Right. Religion does have tremendous value. It's just that religion was meant as an organizing principle for people to cooperate and trust each other and to work towards common causes.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。我的意思是，大众的无神论真的很糟糕，因为人们需要一个操作系统，而大多数人不会自己搞明白，它必须是一个相对通用的操作系统。所以如果你没有，要么陷入无政府状态，要么由国家来规定，这意味着要么是寡头政治，要么是共产主义威权深渊。与此相关的是，我认为一个傻瓜完全相信宗教信仰，另一个傻瓜则认为它毫无价值。没错。宗教确实有巨大的价值。只是宗教本意是作为一种组织原则，让人们合作、互信，并朝着共同目标努力。

turns.en: But it was also meant to have a strong personal exploration and virtue component, and absolutely did not modernize enough, and it was used for control. So again, like my problem is always big stuff masses, right? So I think big religion causes a lot of problems, but I think little religion is good.
turns.zh: 但它本意也应有强烈的个人探索和美德成分，而且绝对没有足够现代化，还被用于控制。所以再说一次，我的问题总是大东西、大众，对吧？所以我认为大宗教造成很多问题，但小宗教是好的。

turns.en: Yeah. The village churches.
turns.zh: 是的。乡村教堂。

turns.en: Village churches, maybe some higher level broad unifying principles, and you know, finally, like the deeper the principle, the more personally it has to be explored and held. And I think if people were to explore religious teachings — pick your favorite religion — if you explore religious teachings, everybody would find components of it that really resonate and will make their lives better, and then you can sort of absorb. But I'm not going to have the argument about which religion is better. And there was a great Far Side cartoon where there's like, you know, St. Peter at the pearly gates. You know this one? And there's like hundreds of people standing there, and like he yells out, "Mormon." The correct answer is Mormon. It's like rest of you are screwed.
turns.zh: 乡村教堂，也许还有一些更高层次的广泛统一原则，而且你知道，最终，原则越深，就越需要个人去探索和持有。我认为如果人们去探索宗教教义——选你最喜欢的宗教——如果探索宗教教义，每个人都会找到真正共鸣的部分，让他们的生活更好，然后你可以吸收。但我不打算争论哪个宗教更好。有一个很棒的法尔赛漫画，圣彼得在天堂门口。你知道这个吗？有几百人站在那里，他喊道：“摩门教。”正确答案是摩门教。其余的人就完蛋了。

turns.en: I think, by the way, that's one of the many flaws in Pascal's wager. You know, that it's not a coin flip. It's a —
turns.zh: 顺便说一句，我认为那是帕斯卡赌注的众多缺陷之一。你知道，那不是抛硬币。那是——

turns.en: Which god?
turns.zh: 哪个神？

turns.en: Right. That's the next question.
turns.zh: 对。那是下一个问题。

transcript.sec: Finding Your Fit: Exploration and Investment

turns.en: I'm one of your most iconic — is your goal in life is to find the people, business, project or art that needs you the most.
turns.zh: 你最具标志性的一句话是——你的人生目标是找到最需要你的人、事业、项目或艺术。

turns.en: Yeah, I really think that this is one place where we diverge from our evolutionary past in a big way. And so it's important to update your priors. In the past, you were in a tribe of a small number of people in a small society, a small civilization, and it was just people you could meet on foot. So the consequences of making a bad choice, sort of getting rejected, were really high. So you got married early; if you were rejected by your suitor or the woman, then it was terrible for you. Your reputation was all entirely local. You had to fit in. And now it's the exact opposite. It's a search function. You can fly anywhere in the world. You can enter any career if you're young enough and motivated enough. You can hang out with almost any set of people. There's infinite opportunity. And that's daunting. It's scary. It's actually really scary, because then if you don't make it, you get depressed and you feel like it was your fault. But the good news is you don't have to stay in any bad situation. Now, that argument where you want to find the thing that fits you the best, there's two parts of it. The first part is you have infinite choice; that's not necessarily good. It's nerve-wracking. And it's kind of bad for society if there's no loyalty. If everyone's always job hopping, if everyone's always location hopping, then you have no loyalty to your kinship or your tribe or your neighborhood. Or if you're always relationship hopping, right? You don't build any permanence. You have no children. But I think what it is is the exploration range is wide. So if you get rejected by someone, don't cry. It's like a bad Google search result. Hit back and just search again or click on the next link. There's infinite variety. I mean, you're going to meet 10, 20,000 people in your life. And there's 8 billion, and you know, you can click click click and find new ones to date all the time. Like there's so much optionality. So, you should explore widely until you find a fit. But then all the benefits in life come from compound interest. So you have to invest in something. You have to invest in a place. You have to invest in people. You have to invest in business. You have to invest in knowledge. You have to invest in a career. So you have to invest in your physical health and your body. So you know, you find the work that feels like play to you and looks like work to others. You find the person who uniquely — you know they make you happy by being who they are, and you make them happy by being who you are. You find the location where you can compound. You can build a house. You can build a household. You have people around you that you like and trust. The weather matters a lot, believe it or not. I mean, it's pretty prosaic but it matters. That's why people talk about it so much. You have to find the thing into which you can just invest, and then you get compound interest. So there's an exploration phase and there's the exploitation phase. Exploitation in the military sense, not in the slavery sense.
turns.zh: 是的，我真的认为这是我们在很大程度上偏离进化过去的一个地方。所以更新你的先验很重要。过去，你在一个小社会、小文明的小部落里，只有你能步行遇到的人。所以做出错误选择、被拒绝的后果非常严重。所以你早婚；如果你被追求者或女人拒绝，那对你来说很糟糕。你的声誉完全是局部的。你必须融入。而现在恰恰相反。这是一个搜索功能。你可以飞到世界任何地方。如果你足够年轻、足够有动力，你可以进入任何职业。你可以和几乎任何人交往。有无穷的机会。这令人畏惧。这很可怕。实际上真的很可怕，因为如果你没有成功，你会抑郁，觉得是自己的错。但好消息是你不必留在任何糟糕的处境中。现在，关于找到最适合你的东西的论点有两个部分。第一部分是你有无穷的选择；这不一定是好事。这让人紧张。而且如果缺乏忠诚，对社会也不好。如果每个人总是跳槽，总是换地方，那么你对亲属、部落或邻里就没有忠诚。或者如果你总是换关系，对吧？你没有任何持久性。你没有孩子。但我认为探索范围很广。所以如果你被某人拒绝，不要哭。就像一次糟糕的谷歌搜索结果。按返回键，再搜一次，或者点击下一个链接。有无穷的多样性。我的意思是，你一生会遇到 1 万到 2 万人。而世界上有 80 亿人，你可以一直点击、点击、点击，找到新的约会对象。选择太多了。所以你应该广泛探索，直到找到合适的。但生活中所有好处都来自复利。所以你必须投资于某样东西。你必须投资于一个地方。你必须投资于人。你必须投资于事业。你必须投资于知识。你必须投资于职业。所以你必须投资于你的身体健康和身体。所以你知道，找到那份对你来说像玩、对别人像工作的工作。找到那个人——他们做自己就能让你快乐，你做自己也能让他们快乐。找到你可以复利的地方。你可以建房子。你可以建立家庭。你身边有你喜欢和信任的人。天气很重要，信不信由你。我的意思是，这很平淡，但很重要。这就是为什么人们经常谈论它。你必须找到你可以投资的东西，然后你得到复利。所以有探索阶段和利用阶段。利用是军事意义上的，不是奴隶制意义上的。

turns.en: Execution phase or —
turns.zh: 执行阶段或——

turns.en: Exploration phase and execution phase, or investment phase. Exploration and then investment. So you explore, explore, explore until you find out what is the right fit for you, and then it's almost effortless to invest in it, and then once you start investing, then you get the compound interest returns. You can't skip either phase.
turns.zh: 探索阶段和执行阶段，或投资阶段。探索然后投资。所以你探索、探索、探索，直到找到适合你的，然后投资几乎毫不费力，一旦开始投资，你就得到复利回报。你不能跳过任何一个阶段。

turns.en: So in some situations we explore too little. Usually in job hunting we explore too little. You know, we'll spend like — and I get it, sometimes you're constrained by finances — but people will spend 3 weeks looking for a job and they'll be in the job for 5 years. That's too little exploration and too much investment. But then on the other side, like people will stay single till they're 50 and then be like, "Oh, now I need to settle down." Well, too much exploration and not enough investment, right? Or decide like it's too late to have kids for whatever reason, too set in their ways or past the biological age. So there's a balance to these, but I think modern society has made exploration a lot easier. And so, you should just realize that the benefits are still all in investment. If you never invest in anything, it's an empty life. It's a soulless life. You'll exit badly.
turns.zh: 所以在某些情况下我们探索得太少。通常在找工作时我们探索得太少。你知道，我们会花——我理解，有时受财务限制——但人们会花 3 周找工作，然后在那份工作上干 5 年。那是探索太少、投资太多。但另一方面，比如人们会单身到 50 岁，然后说：“哦，现在我需要安定下来。”好吧，探索太多、投资不足，对吧？或者因为某种原因决定太晚要孩子，太固执或过了生育年龄。所以这些需要平衡，但我认为现代社会让探索变得容易得多。所以你应该意识到好处仍然全在投资中。如果你从不投资任何东西，那是空虚的生活。那是没有灵魂的生活。你会糟糕地退出。

turns.en: The transition between the two feels like where the rubber really meets the road. You know, it's avoiding kind of a local maxima or understanding whether you're actually in the right place to really root down and invest.
turns.zh: 两者之间的过渡感觉像是关键所在。你知道，这是避免局部最优，或者理解你是否真的在正确的地方扎根和投资。

turns.en: Yeah. I think this is where the experience comes in handy, right? So how do we learn? We don't learn through time. That's where I think Malcolm Gladwell was wrong. It's not 10,000 hours. Okay? He's completely wrong about that. I mean, I get where he's coming from. I'm not criticizing Gladwell, but it's become a meme, right? This 10,000 hours. What it really is is 10,000 iterations. And what are iterations? It's not 10,000 obviously, but it's repeated iterations is how we learn. And iterations are when you — it's not repetitions. It's very deliberate. Do not use the word repetitions. Iteration means you do something, then you honestly reflect upon the outcome, you make a change, and you try again.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为这就是经验派上用场的地方，对吧？那么我们如何学习？我们不是通过时间学习。这就是我认为马尔科姆·格拉德威尔错的地方。不是 1 万小时。好吗？他完全错了。我的意思是，我理解他的出发点。我不是在批评格拉德威尔，但这已经成了一个梗，对吧？这 1 万小时。实际上它是 1 万次迭代。什么是迭代？显然不是 1 万次，但重复迭代是我们学习的方式。迭代是当你——不是重复。它非常刻意。不要用“重复”这个词。迭代意味着你做某事，然后诚实地反思结果，做出改变，再试一次。

turns.en: Then you honestly reflect upon the outcome, you make a change, and you try again. You do this enough times in any endeavor, it will develop your judgment.
turns.zh: 然后你诚实地反思结果，做出改变，再试一次。在任何事情上重复足够多次，就能培养你的判断力。

turns.en: And then once your judgment is well-developed enough, you will start not trying a whole bunch of things. You will reject a whole bunch of things until finally you say, 'Well, actually, I just want to be with this kind of person or I just want to live in this kind of place or I just want to do this kind of thing.' And that's it. Now you're done. Now you can start investing.
turns.zh: 一旦你的判断力足够成熟，你就会开始不去尝试很多事情。你会拒绝很多事情，直到最后你说：“其实，我只想和这样的人在一起，或者我只想住在这样的地方，或者我只想做这样的事情。”就是这样。现在你完成了。现在你可以开始投入了。

turns.en: Love, by the way, all this stuff is ex post facto reasoning. I never really thought this through while I was doing it. It's like really easy to look back and see the pattern. It's very hard to see it when you're moving forward.
turns.zh: 顺便说一句，所有这些都只是事后推理。我在做的时候并没有真正想清楚。回头看模式很容易，但在前进时很难看清。

turns.en: Yeah. Anything that we talk about, my guess is it will only resonate with somebody if they went through the experience themselves and they're like, 'Ah, now I have a word for it.' And then they can attach a word for it and that helps them remember it more later. But you can't teach someone purely in the abstract because they don't know when it applies to their situation. A lot of times people ask me advice on Twitter or on Reddit and it's like way too broad. It's like I'm not in your situation. I can't give you the answer. I just don't know.
turns.zh: 是的。我们谈到的任何东西，我猜只有亲身经历过的人才会产生共鸣，他们会说：“啊，现在我有个词来形容它了。”然后他们就能给它贴上标签，这有助于以后记住。但你不能纯粹抽象地教别人，因为他们不知道什么时候适用于自己的情况。很多时候人们在 Twitter 或 Reddit 上问我建议，问题太宽泛了。我又不在你的处境里，我无法给你答案。我就是不知道。

turns.en: Yeah, all I can give you is a general principle which may or may not apply, right? So, this is pretty unsatisfying, but it's the truth. But this is where I like platforms like Air Chat and Clubhouse back in the day where someone could ask you a question, you could get into it with them. You could ask them the next level question, the next level question, the next level question until you find like, okay, well, if I was in a situation given what you've told me, here's how I would handle it.
turns.zh: 是的，我能给你的只是一个通用原则，可能适用也可能不适用，对吧？所以这很不令人满意，但这是事实。但这就是我喜欢像 Air Chat 和过去的 Clubhouse 这样的平台的原因，在那里有人可以问你一个问题，你可以和他们深入探讨。你可以问他们更深一层的问题，再深一层，再深一层，直到你发现，好吧，根据你告诉我的情况，如果我在那个处境，我会这样处理。

turns.en: Yeah, there's kind of two levels you can engage on it, which is the general or the specific, but anything in the middle is really difficult to...
turns.zh: 是的，你可以从两个层面参与，要么是通用的，要么是具体的，但中间的任何东西都很难……

turns.en: Yeah. The general requires people to apply it themselves. So these are just mental hooks and you know may resonate may not.
turns.zh: 是的。通用的需要人们自己去应用。所以这些只是思维钩子，你知道，可能会引起共鸣，也可能不会。

turns.en: One thing we talked about is the environment being sort of upstream of your thoughts and environment determining so much of what you think how you think but that the enlightened mind sort of can choose its own environment and so taking agency over that.
turns.zh: 我们谈到的一件事是，环境有点像你思想的上游，环境决定了你思考什么、如何思考，但开悟的头脑可以选择自己的环境，从而对此拥有主动权。

turns.en: Yeah. It's a feedback loop, right? We are meant to adapt as living creatures. We adapt to our environment and we locally reverse entropy to make our environment more controllable. But the first step of that is choosing your environment consciously. And so that's why on relationships I was kind of saying don't get attached to a specific relationship even if it's not working for you. You don't have some obligation to struggle through unless you have kids or something then it's different, right?
turns.zh: 是的。这是一个反馈循环，对吧？作为生物，我们注定要适应。我们适应环境，并在局部逆转熵，使环境更可控。但第一步是有意识地选择你的环境。这就是为什么在人际关系上，我有点说不要执着于某段特定的关系，即使它不适合你。你没有义务硬撑下去，除非你有孩子之类的，那就不一样了，对吧？

turns.en: You can change your relationship. You can curate your relationship and then once you're in the right relationship, it's a lot easier because you're a better fit for each other. So, I think this also goes into my tweet a little bit like the only true test of intelligence is if you get what you want out of life, right? And that was one of my favorites for myself because it's a two-part test. One is choosing what to want and then getting it. It's not just like, oh, I want to be a 6'8" tall basketball player. It's not going to happen. So, you have to want the right things, things that are even achievable, but kind of at the edge of your range of achievability and where the process of achieving them won't make you miserable. And so you choose what to want and then you go and get what you want and then if you chose the right things and you got it then that's the only external valid signal that can determine are you intelligent or not, right?
turns.zh: 你可以改变你的关系。你可以精心挑选你的关系，一旦你处于正确的关系中，事情就容易多了，因为你们更合拍。所以，我认为这也有点符合我的一条推文：唯一真正的智力测试是你是否得到了你生活中想要的东西，对吧？那是我自己最喜欢的一条，因为它是一个两部分测试。一是选择想要什么，然后得到它。不仅仅是，哦，我想成为一个 6 英尺 8 英寸高的篮球运动员。那不可能发生。所以，你必须想要正确的东西，那些可以实现的东西，但处于你能力范围的边缘，并且实现它们的过程不会让你痛苦。所以你选择想要什么，然后去得到你想要的，如果你选择了正确的东西并且得到了它，那就是唯一能确定你是否聪明的外部有效信号，对吧？

turns.en: So, I think related, you choose your environment and then you adapt your environment. But don't just get stuck in your environment. Don't adapt it. Like one of the greatest gifts in modern society is that, you know, the first IQ test you have is do you stay where you were born, right? Like a lot of people end up stuck where they're born and maybe it was the optimal place for them, but usually I would guess not. Usually I would guess there was some place out there that was better for them. It might be the next town over. It might be another country. It could be anything. But people want better lives and the first step is where do you go for that better life. So that is part of choosing your environment.
turns.zh: 所以，我认为相关的是，你选择你的环境，然后你适应你的环境。但不要只是困在你的环境里。不要适应它。现代社会最大的礼物之一就是，你知道，你的第一个智商测试是你是否留在你出生的地方，对吧？很多人最终被困在他们出生的地方，也许那里对他们来说是最优的，但通常我猜不是。通常我猜外面有某个地方对他们更好。可能是隔壁镇，可能是另一个国家，可能是任何地方。但人们想要更好的生活，第一步是你去哪里寻找更好的生活。所以这是选择环境的一部分。

turns.en: But even then I feel like you have to do something. I don't think you can go through life as just an advice giver. You know it feels fake to me. It feels false to me. An ideal life is one where you write for yourself and your children and you do things that are useful and beautiful and true. You learn for yourself and to the extent that you're giving people advice, it's because they asked you and they asked you something specific and it can be helpful. You're not giving advice to become an advice giver. My least favorite VC is the type who like says, 'We're company builders. We're the team behind the team.' It's like, 'No, you're not. The founder built the company.' You know, they took all the risk. You're taking a little bit of credit. You risk somebody else's capital, healthy capital. And yes, you were a good picker. There are a few VCs out there who really do get involved and who really do roll up their sleeves and who really do take it personally. But there are few. They're very, very few and they're not the more famous ones that you would think about.
turns.zh: 但即便如此，我觉得你必须做点什么。我不认为你可以仅仅作为一个建议给予者度过一生。你知道，这对我来说感觉很假。理想的生活是为你自己和你的孩子写作，做有用、美丽和真实的事情。你为自己学习，而当你给别人建议时，是因为他们问了你，而且问的是具体的事情，这样才会有帮助。你不是为了成为建议给予者而给建议。我最不喜欢的风险投资家是那种说“我们是公司建设者。我们是团队背后的团队”的人。这就像，“不，你不是。创始人建立了公司。”你知道，他们承担了所有风险。你只是沾了一点光。你拿别人的资本冒险，健康的资本。是的，你是一个好的挑选者。外面有一些风险投资家确实参与其中，确实卷起袖子，确实把它当成自己的事。但很少。非常非常少，而且不是那些你想到的更出名的人。

turns.en: Well, that's what's unique about your Twitter is that you've exerted zero effort to actually accrue a following. You just share notes to self and yeah, people I think see the authenticity of that and the value of it.
turns.zh: 嗯，这就是你的 Twitter 独特之处，你几乎没有付出任何努力来积累粉丝。你只是分享给自己的笔记，是的，我认为人们看到了其中的真实性和价值。

turns.en: I saw a connection between some of your thoughts on envy which has been particularly I think useful and what you said about the real game of intelligence or the real test of intelligence is whether you get what you want out of life. I find if you pass that test, if you get what you want that you uniquely wanted, what is there to be jealous of? Why would you be envious of anybody else?
turns.zh: 我看到了你关于嫉妒的一些想法——我觉得特别有用——和你说的关于智力的真正游戏或真正测试是你是否得到了你生活中想要的东西之间的联系。我发现如果你通过了那个测试，如果你得到了你独特想要的东西，还有什么可嫉妒的呢？你为什么会嫉妒别人？

turns.en: That's a very good point. They do coalesce together. That said, it's very hard to pass that test. Our desires usually outstrip our capabilities and we have a way of manufacturing new ones as soon as we get what we want. But yeah, I have to remind myself to choose inspiration over envy.
turns.zh: 这是一个非常好的观点。它们确实融合在一起。话虽如此，通过这个测试非常困难。我们的欲望通常超过我们的能力，而且我们一旦得到想要的，就会制造新的欲望。但是的，我必须提醒自己选择灵感而非嫉妒。

turns.en: You know, when you see somebody doing something successfully, what I try not to be is envious. In the sense that there's no one on planet Earth that I would swap my life with—no one, not even these enlightened beings. There's nobody that I would do a full wholesale substitute with. So in that sense, envy is not real. But when you look at someone being successful, you can say, well, they don't deserve it, they got lucky. Sure, there's an element of that, and with some people there's much more of that than others, or they're being unethical about it, which is unfortunate. So those are true, but it's not good to fixate on those. Just like we discussed at the beginning, if you fixate on all the unfairness in life, you'll never get anything done.
turns.zh: 你知道，当你看到有人成功做成某件事时，我尽量不去嫉妒。因为在这个地球上，没有一个人我愿意与之交换人生——没有，连那些开悟者也不例外。没有一个人我愿意完全对调。所以从这个意义上说，嫉妒并不真实。但当你看到有人成功时，你可以说，他们不配，他们只是运气好。当然，这有一定道理，有些人身上这种成分更多，或者他们做事不道德，这很遗憾。这些说法没错，但纠结于此并不好。就像我们一开始讨论的，如果你纠结于生活中的所有不公，你永远什么都做不成。

turns.en: So just on a very practical basis, you want to find the part that's inspirational. For example, one of the reasons why I'm doing a company is I'm inspired by guys like Elon and Peter Thiel. Elon inspires me because he does these companies, he charges hard, he takes on huge projects, and he does difficult things. Peter Thiel is a little different. Thiel talks about doing important things, creating the definite future that you want to live in. But I don't see him doing it himself, right? He's an investor and philosopher. So I could just be that guy—I could just be an investor philosopher. But I choose to be inspired by the best part of what he's saying and doing, and I sort of ignore the rest. There's nothing wrong with it, but I just like the inspiration part. But if I had to look for pure inspiration, the guy that I never envied, I never swapped with him, but I was super inspired by Steve Jobs. Even when he was alive, it's easy to be inspired and not envious when people are dead.
turns.zh: 所以从非常实际的角度来说，你要找到其中鼓舞人心的部分。例如，我创办公司的一个原因就是受到埃隆和彼得·蒂尔等人的启发。埃隆激励我，因为他创办这些公司，他奋力冲刺，接手大项目，做困难的事。彼得·蒂尔则有点不同。蒂尔谈论的是做重要的事，创造你想要的确定的未来。但我没看到他亲自去做，对吧？他是投资者兼哲学家。所以我也可以成为那样的人——做个投资者兼哲学家。但我选择被他言行中最好的部分所激励，而忽略其余部分。这没什么不对，我只是喜欢那种激励。但如果要寻找纯粹的激励，那个我从未嫉妒、从未想交换人生、却深受启发的人是史蒂夫·乔布斯。即使在他活着的时候——人们死后更容易被激励而不嫉妒，真的。

turns.en: Right? But even when he was alive, I think somebody said this the other day. They said, since Steve Jobs passed, Silicon Valley is missing its cultural leader. It's like all these entrepreneurs—who do you look up to? I know Elon's pretty reviled now because of the whole thing with the administration and politics. People always pick sides on that, but I think the honest people still like Elon and are inspired by him. But there is no single cultural leader where somebody looks at that person and says, 'Yeah, I want my life to be that.' This person's building something incredible. They're not just doing it for the money. They seem relatively happy. They have a good family life. They have some ethics and morals, although yes, Steve has negative stories about him too. But you can't be an extreme character without breaking some eggs. It's just going to happen. You're going to have some negative sides to your personality, and people will want to elevate themselves by bringing those stories out. They'll want to unearth those stories and pull this person down so they can be higher up on the status ladder. But yeah, since Jobs passed, there isn't that single inspiration. But maybe we're not living in that kind of world anymore. Everyone doesn't tune in to Walter Cronkite for the evening news anymore either. So get your inspiration where you can. There's lots of mini inspiration.
turns.zh: 对吧？但即使在他活着的时候——我记得前几天有人说过，自从乔布斯去世后，硅谷就缺少了一位文化领袖。就像所有这些创业者——你仰望谁？我知道埃隆现在因为政府和政治那档子事被骂得很惨。人们总是选边站，但我认为诚实的人仍然喜欢埃隆并受他启发。但没有一个单一的文化领袖，让人看着他说：‘对，我希望我的生活就是那样。’这个人正在建造不可思议的东西。他们不只是为了钱。他们看起来相对快乐。他们有好的家庭生活。他们有道德伦理，虽然乔布斯也有负面故事。但你不能成为一个极端人物而不打破一些鸡蛋。这总会发生。你的个性会有一些负面，人们会想通过揭露这些故事来抬高自己。他们会想挖掘这些故事，把这个人拉下来，这样他们就能在地位阶梯上爬得更高。但自从乔布斯去世后，就没有那种单一的激励了。但也许我们不再生活在那种世界里了。大家也不再每晚都看沃尔特·克朗凯特的晚间新闻了。所以，从你能找到的地方获取激励吧。有很多微小的激励。

turns.en: Knowing that we are sort of inevitably mimetic creatures and we're going to be inspired by and pick up and follow people, deliberately selecting heroes or deliberately selecting traits of those heroes, rather than doing it on accident, feels like maybe as good as most of us can do.
turns.zh: 知道我们不可避免地是模仿性生物，我们会受启发、会追随他人，那么有意识地选择英雄，或者有意识地选择那些英雄的特质，而不是偶然为之，这大概是我们大多数人能做到的最好程度了。

turns.en: Yeah. And that's fine. I mean, it's again going to be resonance. It is something in that other person will remind you of something within yourself.
turns.zh: 是的。这没问题。我的意思是，这又回到了共鸣。对方身上的某些东西会提醒你，你自己内心也有某种东西。

turns.en: And then you can get some validation by saying, well okay, if that person can do it, I can too. I'll say like Steve Jobs—his having done calligraphy and art, having been a Zen Buddhist for a while, having done LSD, believing in Pixar and believing in NeXT, kind of falling out of favor and then coming back in. All of those things gave me license to just mix the spiritual and the scientific. I wasn't as afraid because there was existence proof through Jobs. Even if he wasn't running around publicly lecturing people—which I appreciate about him actually, that he wasn't out there trying to position himself as a philosopher or guru or anything like that. Rather, he was embodying it in his products. And so I think that's deeply inspirational and definitely there's resonance with parts of me. I think every human is whole. Every person is capable of everything. You're capable of being the biggest angel or the biggest demon. You're capable of having the next breakthrough or just kind of spending your whole life smoking dope and playing video games. You're capable of anything. And this is part of Deutsch's—I don't want to say philosophy, I would say it's part of Deutsch's science because he has shown that humans are Turing complete. Turing complete means you're a universal explainer. Universal explainer means you can simulate all the laws of physics in your head. Anything that can be thought can be thought by you. You can have any thought. You can have any breakthrough. You can have any amount of creativity. Everyone's capable of everything. And I think deep down we know that. And it's just we get limited in society by things like, 'Oh, you're a lawyer, you have to think this way. You went to this school, you have to behave that way. You didn't go to college, so you can only think or talk about these things. You can't piss in public with that person. You can't forge your life uniquely that way.' Or on the other side, there are people who are weirdos—San Francisco is full of weirdos—but they're told, 'You must hate money. You're not allowed to make money. You're not allowed to look normal. You have to be ironic. You got to be a hipster.' You can kind of do whatever you want. And the important thing is just figuring out what is going to get you what you want and wanting the right things in the first place. And I guess I would just argue that all of us eventually figure out that you want the long-term things, and the things that make you personally better off, and then after that you can do the things that make all of society better off. Because if you just obsess on yourself, you're not going to be happy.
turns.zh: 然后你可以得到一些验证：好吧，如果那个人能做到，我也能。比如史蒂夫·乔布斯——他学过书法和艺术，当过一段时间的禅宗佛教徒，服用过 LSD，相信皮克斯和 NeXT，一度失宠然后又回归。所有这些都让我有资格将灵性与科学融合。我不再那么害怕，因为乔布斯提供了存在的证明。即使他没有到处公开演讲——我其实很欣赏他这一点，他没有试图把自己定位成哲学家或导师之类。相反，他将这些体现在产品中。所以我认为这非常鼓舞人心，而且肯定与我内心的某些部分产生共鸣。我认为每个人都是完整的。每个人都有能力做任何事。你既能成为最大的天使，也能成为最大的恶魔。你既能取得下一个突破，也能一辈子抽大麻打游戏。你什么都能做。这是多伊奇理论的一部分——我不想说哲学，我会说这是多伊奇科学的一部分，因为他已经证明人类是图灵完备的。图灵完备意味着你是通用解释器。通用解释器意味着你可以在脑中模拟所有物理定律。任何能被思考的东西你都能思考。你可以有任何想法。你可以有任何突破。你可以有无限的创造力。每个人都有能力做任何事。而且我认为我们内心深处都知道这一点。只是我们在社会中受到限制，比如：‘哦，你是律师，你必须这样思考。你上过这所学校，你必须那样行事。你没上过大学，所以你只能思考或谈论这些事。你不能和那个人一起在公共场合小便。你不能那样独特地塑造你的人生。’或者另一方面，有些人是怪人——旧金山到处都是怪人——但他们被告知：‘你必须讨厌钱。你不能赚钱。你不能看起来正常。你必须讽刺。你必须是个嬉皮士。’你基本上可以做任何你想做的事。重要的是弄清楚什么能让你得到你想要的，并且首先想要正确的东西。我想我会说，我们最终都会明白，你想要的是长期的东西，是让你个人变得更好的东西，然后在那之后，你可以做让整个社会变得更好的事情。因为如果你只执着于自己，你不会快乐。

turns.en: Well, it's probably a grandiose association, but it feels like you're writing your Meditations.
turns.zh: 嗯，这可能是个宏大的联想，但感觉就像你在写你的《沉思录》。

turns.en: You know, they're notes to self that are public that you're iterating on. Well, meditations was more authentic because he didn't mean for his journal to be published.
turns.zh: 你知道，它们是公开的自我笔记，你在不断迭代。嗯，《沉思录》更真实，因为他没打算让日记出版。

turns.en: Yes. So, yeah, you have the observer effect.
turns.zh: 是的。所以，嗯，你有了观察者效应。

turns.en: Yes, mine is less authentic for sure. I try to keep it as authentic as I can while being public, right? Just being public has some level of inauthenticity baked into it.
turns.zh: 是的，我的肯定没那么真实。我尽量在公开时保持真实，对吧？仅仅是公开本身就带有一定程度的虚假。

turns.en: The guy I really admire, Schopenhauer, because he wrote so much harsh truth while he was alive and nobody liked him, but he knew he was telling the truth, so he didn't care.
