And now he's posthumously incredibly famous or should be anyway. I think he's the greatest of the western philosophers that I've encountered. Maybe I'll write my real book and it gets published posthumously because I do live in this world and now the world is super connected and I have kids and all that. But that's when I tell all the harsh truths.
turns.zh: 我真正钦佩的人是叔本华，因为他活着时写了那么多残酷的真相，没人喜欢他，但他知道自己说的是真话，所以不在乎。现在他死后非常有名，至少应该如此。我认为他是我遇到的最伟大的西方哲学家。也许我会写一本真正的书，死后出版，因为我确实活在这个世界里，现在世界超级互联，我有孩子等等。但那才是我说出所有残酷真相的时候。

turns.en: Yeah. One of the pieces of advice you gave somebody who was presently unhappy about finding purpose, finding meaning was God, kids, are missioned.
turns.zh: 是的。你给一个当下对寻找目标、寻找意义感到不快乐的人的一条建议是：神、孩子、使命。

turns.en: Yeah, that's right.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。

turns.en: Um, yeah, God, kids, are mission. I got to find one of the three at least. I was curious how those broke down for you personally.
turns.zh: 嗯，是的，神、孩子、使命。我至少得找到三者之一。我很好奇这些对你个人来说是如何分解的。

turns.en: I got all three. Well, my God is personal, you know. I can't even articulate it. I'm not even sure I use the word God, but it's there. I have my own relationship with whatever this is. Kids definitely have them. Love them. Broaden that. Just family. Family is great. Be close to family. Love your family. Take care of them. There's no substitutes. And expand your definition of family as you get older. And mission. Yeah. I mean, I have a product that I'm working on. I'm recording this thing with you. I'm trying to self-actualize as to what I do best.
turns.zh: 我三者都有。嗯，我的神是个人的，你知道。我甚至无法表达。我甚至不确定我是否用“神”这个词，但它就在那里。我与这一切有自己独特的关系。孩子肯定有。爱他们。扩大范围。就是家庭。家庭很棒。亲近家人。爱你的家人。照顾他们。没有替代品。随着年龄增长，扩展你对家庭的定义。还有使命。是的。我的意思是，我有一个正在做的产品。我在和你录这个节目。我在努力自我实现，做我最擅长的事。

turns.en: So, the mission is very narrow to what you're working on now. It's not a grander human level, civilization level.
turns.zh: 所以，使命非常狭窄，就是你目前在做的事情。不是更宏大的人类层面、文明层面。

turns.en: I'd like to be enlightened. I don't feel like I have a right to lecture people on these esoteric topics without myself having explored them all the way through. So on some level, part of the reason why I haven't written a book is because I feel like it might be fraudulent. You know, part of it is I'm just lazy. So it's a good excuse, but it might be fraudulent.
turns.zh: 我想开悟。我不觉得自己有权在没有彻底探索这些深奥话题的情况下对人们说教。所以在某种程度上，我没写书的部分原因是我觉得那可能是欺诈。你知道，部分原因是我只是懒。所以这是个好借口，但可能确实是欺诈。

turns.en: Can you overdose on introspection?
turns.zh: 你会过度内省吗？

turns.en: I think it would be a different thing. Introspection, if you're trying to find truth, if you're like thinking, like watching yourself and increasing your self-awareness, I don't think you can. I think you get bored of it or distracted before you overdose on it. But if you're ruminating, if you're basically like, "Oh, this shouldn't have happened and this was wrong," and you know, you're caught in that mind loop that depressed people have, then you can absolutely overdo it. So it depends what you're thinking about. If you're observing yourself, that's different than if you're obsessing over yourself or you're obsessing over some problems, some of which can be real, but most of which are imagined.
turns.zh: 我认为那是另一回事。内省，如果你在寻找真相，如果你在思考，比如观察自己并提高自我意识，我认为你不会过度。我觉得你在过度之前就会感到无聊或分心。但如果你在反刍，如果你基本上在想，“哦，这不应该发生，这是错的，”你知道，你陷入了抑郁者那种思维循环，那么你绝对会过度。所以这取决于你在想什么。如果你在观察自己，那不同于你沉迷于自己或沉迷于某些问题，其中一些可能是真实的，但大多数是想象的。

turns.en: Like one of the controversial takes I have, people don't like this, but if I'm reading a philosopher right, one tweet I just put was like: the more it matters who said it, the less it actually matters. I think that is true. Like truth is truth regardless of who it comes from. But at the same time, there are philosophies of how to live your life, and these aren't just like simple statements, these are like this person's entire philosophy, right? And I don't read books these days, I read authors. And I don't read authors, I read philosophers. Right? So I'll read Deutsch, I'll read Osho, I'll read Krishnamurti, I'll read Schopenhauer, and because I like them, I'll read everything by them. And until I'm done reading everything by them, why would I read anybody else? Right? I'll read everything by Taleb, right? But I do judge these philosophers on the outcomes they got in their lives. So if somebody has a deep philosophy but they seem like they were unhappy and they didn't get what they wanted, they were miserable about it, then you know, it's like that line from No Country for Old Men: "What good are your rules if they led you here?" The killer says that when the protagonist shows up in the wrong place, right? So it should lead you to a good place. So there are some modern writers of recent times and celebrities who I won't name, but they basically kill themselves. So I'm like, how great was that philosophy? Like I'm sorry you killed yourself, but clearly whatever you did for your philosophy did not work. Now there might be individual truths in there I can pluck out, but I'm not going to invest deeply in your philosophy or reify your life because it ended badly.
turns.zh: 比如我有一个有争议的观点，人们不喜欢这个，但如果我正确理解了一位哲学家，我发的一条推文是：谁说的越重要，它实际上越不重要。我认为这是真的。就像真相就是真相，不管它来自谁。但与此同时，有一些关于如何生活的哲学，这些不仅仅是简单的陈述，而是这个人的整个哲学，对吧？我现在不读书，我读作者。我不读作者，我读哲学家。对吧？所以我会读多伊奇，我会读奥修，我会读克里希那穆提，我会读叔本华，因为我喜欢他们，我会读他们所有的作品。在我读完他们所有的作品之前，我为什么要读别人？对吧？我会读塔勒布所有的作品，对吧？但我确实根据这些哲学家在生活中取得的结果来评判他们。所以如果某人有深刻的哲学，但他们似乎不快乐，没有得到他们想要的，他们对此痛苦，那么你知道，就像《老无所依》里的那句话：“如果你的规则把你带到这里，它们有什么用？”杀手在主角出现在错误的地方时说的，对吧？所以它应该把你带到一个好地方。所以有一些近代的现代作家和名人，我不点名，但他们基本上自杀了。所以我想，那哲学有多伟大？我很抱歉你自杀了，但显然你为哲学所做的一切都没有奏效。现在那里可能有一些我可以摘取的个别真理，但我不会深入投资你的哲学或具体化你的生活，因为它结局很糟。

turns.en: You've got a great tweet: "The real truths are heresies. They cannot be spoken, only discovered, whispered, and perhaps read."
turns.zh: 你有一条很棒的推文：“真正的真理是异端。它们无法被说出，只能被发现、低语，或许被阅读。”

turns.en: That's right. So it's my most humorous book.
turns.zh: 没错。所以那是我最幽默的书。

turns.en: Why is that though? Like why are there so many forces working against truth?
turns.zh: 但那是为什么呢？为什么有这么多力量与真相作对？

turns.en: Because society has to stay together. Groups have to have consensus. And to have consensus you have to have a shared set of beliefs that are false but make it easier to get along. And like one of those is everyone's equal, right? Everyone's equal in the Holy Ghost sense. Everyone's equal in this consciousness sense. Everyone's equal in the universal explainer sense. Everyone is not equal in almost any other sense, right? In terms of like how hard you work, how good are you doing for society or for yourself, how good is your product, how high can you jump, how hard can you hit, you know, are you qualified for this job or that job? Not equal, right? But that is a shared fiction that we have to have to get along. If we don't have that shared fiction, we don't get along. We start fighting each other. So some of these falsehoods are there for good reasons. They prevent warfare. They allow the group to coexist, but they're not true. So, I mean, that's a simple example. There are many like you see this a lot in male-female relationships, right? Like what is the whole pickup artist thing, the pickup artist society? That's all guys sharing truths about women that they're not allowed to say in public that seem to work on some number of women that allow them to go get dates. You know, like one of the truths in that domain is that women are attracted to signs of social status in men. It's not they're attracted to necessarily wealth, although wealth can be a sign of social status. They're not necessarily attracted to physicality or even character. Although I would argue for marriage, women are attracted to character, but you know, for dating, they're attracted to signs of social status in men. It's not even social status.
turns.zh: 因为社会必须保持团结。群体必须有共识。要有共识，你必须有一套共同的信念，这些信念是虚假的，但能让人们更容易相处。比如其中之一是人人平等，对吧？在圣灵意义上人人平等。在意识意义上人人平等。在普遍解释者意义上人人平等。但在几乎任何其他意义上，人并不平等，对吧？比如你工作多努力，你为社会或自己做得有多好，你的产品有多好，你能跳多高，你能打多重，你知道，你是否胜任这份或那份工作？不平等，对吧？但那是我们必须拥有的共同虚构，才能相处。如果我们没有那个共同虚构，我们就无法相处。我们开始互相争斗。所以这些虚假有些是有充分理由的。它们防止战争。它们允许群体共存，但它们不是真的。所以，我的意思是，那是一个简单的例子。还有很多，比如你在男女关系中经常看到，对吧？比如整个搭讪艺术家的事情，搭讪艺术家社群？那都是男人们分享关于女人的真相，这些真相他们不能在公开场合说，但似乎对某些女人有效，让他们能约会。你知道，比如那个领域的一个真相是，女人被男人身上的社会地位迹象所吸引。她们不一定被财富吸引，尽管财富可以是社会地位的迹象。她们不一定被身体素质甚至性格吸引。虽然我会争辩说，对于婚姻，女人被性格吸引，但你知道，对于约会，她们被男人身上的社会地位迹象所吸引。甚至不完全是社会地位。

turns.en: It's signs of social status, right? So then men have to learn how to hack signs of social status. That creates this game phenomenon which is its own falsehood going back at women. Equivalently, women are taught to believe that men are attracted to you for your character and your personality. They might stay with you for that later, but the initial attraction is all physical, right? And so that's another truth you're not supposed to talk about too much. These are on the edge, so I can talk about them a little bit. There are truths I just cannot talk about, way further down. But I would tell you that some of the greatest philosophers of all time establish their truth-telling capabilities by telling you the harsh truth. And because of that, they'll be read long after most of the rest of them are forgotten.
turns.zh: 这是社会地位的信号，对吧？所以男人必须学会如何破解社会地位的信号。这就创造了一种游戏现象，它本身就是一种谎言，反过来影响女性。同样地，女性被教导相信男人是被你的性格和个性所吸引。他们后来可能会因为这些而留在你身边，但最初的吸引力完全是生理上的，对吧？所以这是另一个你不该多谈的真相。这些处于边缘地带，所以我可以稍微谈一谈。有些真相我完全不能谈，它们要深入得多。但我要告诉你，一些最伟大的哲学家正是通过告诉你残酷的真相来建立他们讲真话的能力。正因为如此，他们会在大多数其他哲学家被遗忘之后很久仍被阅读。

turns.en: And that's part of the value of reading those permanent perfect books from dead people who are willing to tell the truth.
turns.zh: 这也是阅读那些愿意讲真话的逝者所写的永恒完美书籍的价值所在。

turns.en: They're dead, and they either told the truth, published posthumously, or published anonymously, or they just didn't care.
turns.zh: 他们已经去世了，要么是讲了真话，要么是死后出版，要么是匿名出版，要么就是根本不在乎。

turns.en: It's interesting that some truths are self-perpetuating or self-converging. Like they are making themselves more evident, and others need to be actively obscured.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，有些真理是自我延续或自我收敛的。它们让自己变得更加明显，而另一些则需要被主动掩盖。

turns.en: Well, it actually goes back to what you said earlier: some things are spreadable and some things are not. So when you get a truth that spreads, it's cliché, it's conventional wisdom. If you get a lie that spreads, that's fake news, that's all over, that's politics, that's a lot of the combat that goes on. A lie that doesn't spread just disappears very quickly. And then there are the truths that don't spread. And those are actually the most interesting. The reason they don't spread is because spread is a function of groups. To spread, there needs to be a group to spread within. So any truth that lowers group cohesion will not spread.
turns.zh: 嗯，这其实又回到了你之前说的：有些东西可以传播，有些则不能。所以当一个真理传播开来，它就变成了陈词滥调，变成了传统智慧。如果一个谎言传播开来，那就是假新闻，到处都是，那是政治，是很多争斗的来源。一个不传播的谎言很快就会消失。然后还有那些不传播的真理。这些实际上是最有趣的。它们不传播的原因是传播是群体的功能。要传播，就需要有一个群体在其中传播。所以任何降低群体凝聚力的真理都不会传播。

turns.en: And then there's kind of like a 2x2 matrix, right?
turns.zh: 然后就像一个 2x2 矩阵，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. It's a 2x2 matrix of truth versus spreadability. And basically you have conventional wisdom, fake news, heresies, and non-existence. Those are the four, or nonsense I would say. So, the problem is everyone knows the conventional wisdom. The heresies don't spread. So most of the news is fake news.
turns.zh: 是的。这是一个关于真理与可传播性的 2x2 矩阵。基本上你有传统智慧、假新闻、异端邪说和不存在的东西。就是这四种，或者我会说是胡说八道。所以问题在于每个人都知道传统智慧。异端邪说不会传播。所以大多数新闻都是假新闻。

turns.en: By definition, conventional wisdom doesn't need to be spread because it's already out there. And heresies don't spread because they're not spreadable, and nonsense doesn't go anywhere. So the only thing that makes it through the environment is fake news.
turns.zh: 根据定义，传统智慧不需要传播，因为它已经在那里了。异端邪说不会传播，因为它们不可传播，胡说八道也走不远。所以唯一能在环境中存活下来的就是假新闻。

turns.en: Well, it's actually Peter Thiel's question: digging for heresies, right?
turns.zh: 嗯，这实际上是彼得·蒂尔的问题：挖掘异端邪说，对吧？

turns.en: That's right. If you want to be non-consensus correct, you need to bet on a heresy.
turns.zh: 没错。如果你想成为非共识的正确者，你需要押注于一个异端邪说。

turns.en: Right. Now, Peter only talks about business contexts.
turns.zh: 对。不过彼得只谈商业背景。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But the real heresies are everywhere. They're out there in business, in science, in genetics, demographics, social science, economics. Yeah, that's very interesting.
turns.zh: 但真正的异端邪说无处不在。它们存在于商业、科学、遗传学、人口学、社会科学、经济学中。是的，这非常有趣。

turns.en: I'll read a clip and see if this is updated at all, particularly via Deutsch. My philosophy falls down to this: on one pole, evolution has a binding principle because it explains so much about humans. On the other is Buddhism, which is the oldest, most time-tested spiritual philosophy regarding our internal state.
turns.zh: 我会读一段摘录，看看这是否有所更新，特别是通过多伊奇。我的哲学归结为：一端是进化论，它有一个约束原则，因为它解释了关于人类的很多方面。另一端是佛教，它是最古老、最经得起时间考验的关于我们内在状态的精神哲学。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, I still stand by that. I think those are both still true. I would just add to it that I think through Deutsch, quantum physics is our best explanation for the material world we live in. His epistemology and his replacement of good explanations that are hard to vary—I add on to that a little bit: it helps me to think of it also as they often make risky and narrow predictions. That's how you determine what's true from what's false. So that is an incredibly useful addition. And then computation, and the linkages between all of these. So quantum computation, linkage between quantum physics and epistemology and evolution by natural selection, and also with Buddhist spirituality, which I know Deutsch has zero interest in and does not factor into his worldview at all. But that's where the practicality of your philosophy comes in, of just managing the humanness of our minds. Buddhism is the best expression of that.
turns.zh: 是的，我仍然坚持这一点。我认为这两者仍然正确。我只想补充一点，通过多伊奇，量子物理学是我们对物质世界的最佳解释。他的认识论和他对难以变动的良好解释的替代——我在此基础上稍加补充：这帮助我思考它们通常做出有风险和狭窄的预测。这就是你如何区分真假。所以这是一个极其有用的补充。然后是计算，以及所有这些之间的联系。所以量子计算，量子物理学与认识论和自然选择进化论之间的联系，还有佛教灵性，我知道多伊奇对此毫无兴趣，也完全没有纳入他的世界观。但这就是你哲学的实用性所在，即管理我们心智的人性。佛教是这方面最好的表达。

turns.en: Yeah, it's practical, but I also think it's true. I don't necessarily live my life 100% this way, but intellectually I'm pretty convinced that the Buddhist interpretation is correct, that consciousness is everything and everything arises within consciousness, that the self is a fiction. Do I live my actual life that way day-to-day? But I do believe it to be true tentatively, and I think it is a better model than saying that consciousness arises within matter. I think matter arises within consciousness.
turns.zh: 是的，它很实用，但我也认为它是真的。我不一定百分之百这样生活，但在理智上我相当确信佛教的解释是正确的，即意识就是一切，一切都在意识中产生，自我是一个虚构。我每天真的那样生活吗？但我暂时相信它是真的，而且我认为这是一个比说意识产生于物质更好的模型。我认为物质产生于意识。

turns.en: Is that compatible with Deutsch? I mean his thing about human exceptionalism and we are the only known conscious beings.
turns.zh: 这与多伊奇兼容吗？我是说他关于人类例外论以及我们是唯一已知的有意识存在的观点。

turns.en: It's completely compatible. It's the one thing you can't determine: is everything taking place in my consciousness, or is my consciousness taking place in everything? There is no experiment we know of.
turns.zh: 完全兼容。这是你无法确定的一件事：是一切都在我的意识中发生，还是我的意识在一切中发生？我们不知道有什么实验可以区分。

turns.en: Will that ever be knowable?
turns.zh: 那会变得可知吗？

turns.en: I don't want to say never, because someone might get very creative, but I can't think of a way to distinguish between the two. Deutsch has an experiment for the quantum multiverse, which is actually how he came up with quantum computing. People don't realize this, but he came up with the theory of quantum computation because he was trying to create a falsifiability experiment for the quantum multiverse. And to do that, he had to create an AGI and treat that as an observer and get inside its brain and then see if wave function collapse happened inside the observer rather than from the observer. And to do that, he was like, well then I need a quantum AGI. And for a quantum AGI, I need a quantum computer. So then he worked out the equations for quantum computing. That's how quantum computing was born.
turns.zh: 我不想说永远不可能，因为有人可能会非常有创意，但我无法想出区分两者的方法。多伊奇有一个关于量子多重宇宙的实验，这实际上是他提出量子计算的方式。人们没有意识到这一点，但他提出量子计算理论是因为他试图为量子多重宇宙创造一个可证伪性实验。为了做到这一点，他必须创造一个 AGI 并将其视为观察者，进入其大脑，然后看看波函数坍缩是否发生在观察者内部而不是来自观察者。为了做到这一点，他想，那么我需要一个量子 AGI。而对于量子 AGI，我需要一台量子计算机。所以他推导出了量子计算的方程。量子计算就是这样诞生的。

turns.en: That's crazy.
turns.zh: 这太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah. From quantum physics, from the multiverse theory. So I don't want to say never, because he figured out—he literally invented the field of quantum computing while trying to figure out a falsifiability experiment for the quantum multiverse.
turns.zh: 是的。从量子物理学，从多重宇宙理论而来。所以我不想说永远不可能，因为他想出来了——他实际上是在试图为量子多重宇宙找出一个可证伪性实验时发明了量子计算领域。

turns.en: So there may be a falsifiable experiment to distinguish whether materiality exists inside consciousness or consciousness exists inside materiality.
turns.zh: 所以可能存在一个可证伪的实验，来区分物质性存在于意识之内，还是意识存在于物质性之内。

turns.en: That's a pretty all-time side quest.
turns.zh: 那真是一个永恒的支线任务。

turns.en: Exactly. But even his experiment is not runnable yet.
turns.zh: 正是。但即便是他的实验也还无法运行。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: You know, you would need an AGI first and you need quantum computers first.
turns.zh: 你知道，你首先需要一个 AGI，还需要量子计算机。

turns.en: You mentioned before that you wanted to write a blog post at some point about how you can map the tenets of Buddhism directly onto a virtual reality simulation.
turns.zh: 你之前提到过，你想在某个时候写一篇博客，关于如何将佛教的教义直接映射到虚拟现实模拟中。

turns.en: Oh, shoot. I'd forgotten that one. Yeah. I think the way it kind of goes is roughly something like: if you were living in a sim, right? Let's say you found out tomorrow you're living in the Matrix. There's a splinter in your mind like "I'm in the Matrix. I need to get out," right? So that would basically be like the quest for enlightenment. You'd be looking for truth. You didn't know what it was. This is what the Matrix is all about, right? And then you would find out, let's say you break out of it, right? And now you land in another Matrix. You land in Zion. How do you know that's not another Matrix, right? You have an inception problem. How do you know which dream you're in? Which dream level you're in? Because the moment you're out, it means you can get out, which means that you can't trust this is real either, right? This could just be a sim as well. So breaking out of the simulation does you no favors. It puts you in an infinite trap. And so then you're always trying to break out of the Matrix. No matter where you are, you never know if you're in base reality or not. So there's only one way you could be convinced you're in base reality. Right now, you could be dead, but if you're dead, there's no one to convince. You can't have an experience of being dead. So that's not valid. But there's only one way to truly break out of the Matrix, which is the white room. In the Matrix, they go into the white room. It's just blank. What is that? It's just consciousness. There's nothing there other than just that pure awareness, right? If you're in pure awareness, that means you're not simulating anything other than just you're there. That's it. You're the only thing you're simulating is "I." So the equivalent to that in Buddhism is when you realize you're just pure consciousness and everything else is just forms that come and go. Everything else is Matrix, right? It's all simulation. Now, if you're in that mode, you're enlightened and you're in the white room in the Matrix. They're functionally equivalent. You're all you are is pure consciousness. It's really boring. And so what do you do before enlightenment or after enlightenment? Well, there's a Zen saying: before enlightenment, chop wood, carry water. After enlightenment, chop wood, carry water. So now you realize you're bored. And now you realize why the Matrix or the white room generates the entire Matrix, or why God creates infinite forms, or why you as God forget yourself and then go back into the game. It's because it's boring out there. So you go be Cypher. So Buddhism and simulation theory kind of lead you the same way. So, if we managed to create simulations and we put ourselves inside the simulation, at first it would be really fun. You'd be like, "I'm in a sim. I can do anything." And you're like, "Wait, I control the sim. This is really boring," right? And so then you'd have to forget that you're in a sim. You'd have to wipe your own memory in the sim so that you can actually enjoy the sim, but then you'd also feel fear and at some point you get this nervous feeling like "I need to get out of the sim." And then when you try to get out of the sim, you're like, "How do I know I'm out of the sim?" And then eventually get to the white room, you're like, "Now I'm out of the sim. But now this is really boring" and you create a sim, wipe your memory and dive back in. That's your rebirths. That's your infinite cycle of rebirths. So that's how I map Buddhism to simulation theory.
turns.zh: 哦，糟了，我都忘了那个了。嗯，是的。我觉得大致是这样的：如果你生活在一个模拟中，对吧？假设你明天发现你活在《黑客帝国》里。你脑子里有个念头，像“我在矩阵里，我得出去”，对吧？那基本上就像追求开悟。你在寻找真相，你不知道它是什么。这就是《黑客帝国》的全部意义，对吧？然后你发现了，比如说你逃出去了，对吧？然后你落入了另一个矩阵。你到了锡安。你怎么知道那不是另一个矩阵呢？对吧？你有一个“盗梦空间”问题。你怎么知道你在哪个梦里？你在哪一层梦？因为一旦你出来了，就意味着你能出来，这也意味着你同样不能相信这是真实的，对吧？这同样可能只是一个模拟。所以逃出模拟对你没有好处。它把你置于一个无限的陷阱中。于是你总是试图逃出矩阵。无论你在哪里，你永远不知道你是否在基础现实中。所以只有一种方法能让你确信你在基础现实中。现在，你可能已经死了，但如果你死了，就没有人能说服你。你无法体验死亡。所以那是不成立的。但真正逃出矩阵的唯一方法就是白色房间。在《黑客帝国》里，他们进入白色房间。那里一片空白。那是什么？那只是意识。除了那种纯粹的觉知，什么都没有，对吧？如果你处于纯粹的觉知中，那就意味着除了你在那里，你什么也没有模拟。就是这样。你唯一模拟的东西就是“我”。所以佛教中与之对应的是，当你意识到你只是纯粹的意识，而其他一切都是来去的形式。其他一切都是矩阵，对吧？都是模拟。现在，如果你处于那种模式，你就开悟了，你就处于矩阵中的白色房间。它们在功能上是等价的。你只是纯粹的意识。这真的很无聊。那么你在开悟前或开悟后做什么呢？嗯，有一句禅语：开悟前，砍柴挑水；开悟后，砍柴挑水。所以现在你意识到你很无聊。现在你明白了为什么矩阵或白色房间生成了整个矩阵，或者为什么上帝创造了无限的形式，或者为什么你作为上帝忘记了自己然后重新回到游戏中。因为外面太无聊了。所以你变成了赛弗。所以佛教和模拟理论大致引导你走向同样的方向。所以，如果我们设法创造了模拟，并把我们自己放进模拟中，一开始会很有趣。你会想，“我在模拟里，我可以做任何事。”然后你会想，“等等，我控制着模拟，这真的很无聊，”对吧？所以你就得忘记你在模拟中。你必须在模拟中抹去自己的记忆，这样你才能真正享受模拟，但随后你也会感到恐惧，在某个时刻你会感到紧张，觉得“我需要离开这个模拟。”然后当你试图离开模拟时，你会想，“我怎么知道我离开了模拟？”最后到了白色房间，你会想，“现在我离开了模拟。但这真的很无聊，”于是你创造一个模拟，抹去记忆，再跳进去。那就是你的重生。那就是你无尽的轮回。所以这就是我如何将佛教映射到模拟理论。

turns.en: The same outcome, but simulation theory to me is a lousy theory. It's unfalsifiable. It just kicks the can up the road. It makes a probabilistic argument. It says, hey, we're likely to develop this and then do that and then do this and then do that. But there are too many ways it could go wrong. Like the sim is always going to be lower resolution than reality. The people on the outside can always control the people on the inside. So there are all kinds of problems with it. Plus I think we inherently have a craving for truth. So I don't think anybody wants to live a lie. Even I myself, like even if I were living in some other reality, I would not create a fake reality where I would forget everything. Or if I did, I'd leave myself an out, an eject button, which I'd have to look really hard to find. Maybe that's called enlightenment.
turns.zh: 同样的结果，但模拟理论对我来说是个糟糕的理论。它无法证伪。它只是把问题踢到更远的地方。它做了一个概率论证。它说，嘿，我们很可能会发展这个，然后做那个，然后做这个，然后做那个。但有很多方式可能出错。比如模拟总是比现实分辨率低。外面的人总能控制里面的人。所以它有很多问题。另外，我认为我们天生渴望真相。所以我不认为有人想活在谎言中。即使是我自己，即使我生活在另一个现实中，我也不会创造一个虚假的现实让我忘记一切。或者如果我这样做了，我会给自己留一条出路，一个弹出按钮，我必须非常努力才能找到它。也许那叫做开悟。

turns.en: Right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Well, I like that the simulation theory I find is a useful belief because you see the people, if what you need out of life is to not take yourself too seriously, to be high agency, to be active but not stress too much about what you're being active about. It's actually pretty useful frame for most of life.
turns.zh: 嗯，我喜欢模拟理论，我发现它是一个有用的信念，因为你看人们，如果你从生活中需要的是不要太把自己当回事，保持高能动性，积极但不为你所积极的事情过度紧张。它实际上对大部分生活来说是一个相当有用的框架。

turns.en: It's just religion. Religion would do the exact same thing for you.
turns.zh: 那只是宗教。宗教会为你做同样的事情。

turns.en: Exactly. With less steps, less computation involved, less programmers. Instead of calling it God, you're just calling it the great programmer.
turns.zh: 正是。步骤更少，计算量更少，程序员更少。与其称之为上帝，你只是称之为伟大的程序员。

turns.en: Yeah. And more friends probably.
turns.zh: 是啊，而且可能朋友更多。

turns.en: So there's also a tendency in society that whatever the latest scientific worldview is, we layer that onto religion, right? So when it was the sun, you lived and died by the sun, then the sun god Ra was a sun god, right? And then when the king was in charge, then it was a god king. And when Newton came along, we got the mechanical universe, then it was like, oh, the universe is like a big watch. It's a mechanistic universe. God is dead. And now because computers are ascendant, the universe is a big computer.
turns.zh: 所以社会上还有一种趋势，无论最新的科学世界观是什么，我们都会把它叠加到宗教上，对吧？所以当是太阳的时候，你靠太阳生、靠太阳死，那么太阳神拉就是太阳神，对吧？然后当国王掌权时，那就是神王。当牛顿出现时，我们有了机械宇宙，然后就像，哦，宇宙像一块大手表。它是一个机械宇宙。上帝死了。现在因为计算机崛起，宇宙是一台大计算机。

turns.en: Yeah. We have a way of taking the permanent problems and making them contemporary.
turns.zh: 是啊。我们有一种方式，把永恒的问题变得当代化。

turns.en: Yeah. We always view it through the contemporary lens of our greatest knowledge at that time.
turns.zh: 是啊。我们总是通过当时最伟大知识的当代视角来看待它。

turns.en: Yeah. But I would argue that understanding things like relativity and quantum physics and studying cosmology and just sort of physics is more satisfying than saying the universe is a computer. Saying the universe is a computer is almost too reductive. It's trying too hard. As a very simple example, a computer is a state machine. A state machine has steps. Steps means everything is discrete: this happens and this happens and that happens. If this happens, then take that branch, take that branch. But everything is in a discrete sequence and that is kind of what quantum physics says.
turns.zh: 是啊。但我会说，理解像相对论和量子物理这样的东西，研究宇宙学和物理学，比说宇宙是一台计算机更令人满意。说宇宙是一台计算机几乎过于简化了。它太刻意了。举个很简单的例子，计算机是一个状态机。状态机有步骤。步骤意味着一切都是离散的：发生这个，发生那个，发生那个。如果发生这个，就走那个分支，走那个分支。但一切都处于离散序列中，而这正是量子物理所说的。

transcript.sec: Contradiction between quantum physics and relativity

turns.en: So quantum computation, quantum physics says the universe is discreet. But that is not what relativity says. Relativity says the universe is continuous. And so right there you run into a contradiction. Any sim theory that says the universe is a computer relies upon a kind of computation that does not exist. Not even quantum computation. It violates relativity. And this is why all the compute-based theories of the world, like Steven Wolfram's compute-based physics, run into this issue, which is that reality is continuous. So he then has this whole other thing, this hypograph thing, which I don't fully understand, but I just feel like it's too conveniently mapping the dominant paradigm of our times onto religion and then removing and then hiding God behind the curtain.
turns.zh: 量子计算、量子物理学说宇宙是离散的。但相对论不是这么说的。相对论说宇宙是连续的。所以这里就出现了矛盾。任何认为宇宙是一台计算机的模拟理论，都依赖于一种并不存在的计算方式。连量子计算也不行。它违反了相对论。这就是为什么所有基于算力的世界理论，比如史蒂芬·沃尔弗拉姆的基于算力的物理学，都会遇到这个问题——现实是连续的。所以他还有另一套东西，那个 hypograph 什么的，我不太懂，但我觉得它太方便地把我们时代的主导范式映射到宗教上，然后把上帝藏到幕后。

turns.en: Yeah. Which we should be suspicious of.
turns.zh: 是的。我们应该对此保持怀疑。

turns.en: Yeah. I think it's easier just to have your own viewpoint on this, because no one else will satisfy you. Nothing else will satisfy you. And I think if you can put a name to it, it's kind of problematic, or if you can put a structure to it, it's problematic.
turns.zh: 是的。我觉得这件事最好有自己的看法，因为别人无法让你满意，其他任何东西都无法让你满意。而且我认为，如果你能给它命名，那就有点问题；如果你能给它一个结构，那也有问题。

transcript.sec: Live for something larger than yourself, but on your own terms

turns.en: I really love this tweet as a capstone for a lot of what you've shared, because I feel like it ties together a lot of threads that might seem to have an apparent contradiction, which is: live for something larger than yourself, but only on your own terms.
turns.zh: 我非常喜欢这条推文，它像一块基石，总结了你分享的很多内容，因为它把很多看似矛盾的东西串在了一起：为比自己更大的东西而活，但只按自己的方式。

turns.en: Oh, I like that one. I'd forgotten about that one. I like that one. Live for something larger than yourself, but only on your own terms. You know what? I'm going to go do exactly that.
turns.zh: 哦，我喜欢这条。我都忘了。我喜欢这条。为比自己更大的东西而活，但只按自己的方式。你知道吗？我就要这么做。

turns.en: I like that one. It's a really good way to disentangle everything.
turns.zh: 我喜欢这条。它真的能把所有事情理清楚。

turns.en: Yeah, because society doesn't want to tell you to live on your own terms because they're afraid you're going to screw over society, that you're just going to be a criminal and a thug and a hedonist. But that's a path for unintelligent people. The person who doesn't know what they're doing is the one doing too many drugs or breaking too many laws or doing horrible things. You don't need to do that. It's not going to make you happy. It's just a trap. It's a trap that you're caught in. When we look at a drug addict, that's a trap they're caught in. The same way you see someone committing crimes, there are better ways to get what you want. You will actually have a better life if you don't do that. And that's where a lot of progressivism comes from. You still have to have punishments to dissuade people, but at the same time, I feel like you do have to live life on your own terms or you're not going to do it, but you have to live for something larger than yourself or it's going to lead to a very bad outcome and you're not going to be happy. So, yeah, I'm going to follow that advice. Thank you for that.
turns.zh: 是的，因为社会不想告诉你要按自己的方式生活，他们担心你会搞砸社会，变成罪犯、暴徒和享乐主义者。但那是愚蠢之人的路。不知道自己在做什么的人才会嗑太多药、犯太多法、做可怕的事。你不需要那样做。那不会让你快乐。那只是一个陷阱。一个你陷进去的陷阱。我们看一个吸毒者，那就是他们陷进去的陷阱。就像你看到有人犯罪一样，有更好的方法得到你想要的东西。如果你不那样做，你的生活实际上会更好。很多进步主义就来源于此。你仍然需要惩罚来劝阻人们，但与此同时，我觉得你必须按自己的方式生活，否则你根本不会去活；但你也必须为比自己更大的东西而活，否则会导致非常糟糕的结果，你也不会快乐。所以，是的，我会遵循这个建议。谢谢你。

turns.en: I mean, if you weren't getting high on your own supply, it wouldn't be good substance.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，如果你不吸自己的货，那它就不是好货。

turns.en: I love finding my own tweet and being like, 'Wow, that's really good.'
turns.zh: 我喜欢找到自己的推文，然后说：‘哇，这条真棒。’

turns.en: That's how I know a book is almost ready, when I pick it up again and it's better than I remembered it.
turns.zh: 这就是我判断一本书快完成的方法：当我再次拿起它，发现它比我记忆中的更好。

turns.en: Yeah. The great books are fractal, too. You learn something new every time.
turns.zh: 是的。伟大的书也是分形的。你每次都能学到新东西。

turns.en: One of the things I like doing now is I found some Schopenhauer and Seneca, for example, they have good audio books and recordings of their stuff, but they're very high density speakers and authors. So if you listen to their stuff, you miss a lot because it was written, so it's meant to be read. Which is why I had this tweet about listening to books instead of reading them is like drinking your vegetables instead of eating them, right? It's more efficient, but you're getting kind of a sugar high. You're not taking it the way it was meant to be. You're not getting all the benefits, but it's better than nothing, right? It's better to do that than to not consume it. And so I've been listening to a bunch of authors who are normally written down. And one of the things I had to come to peace with was just like, you know what, I'm only going to get like 20% of the throughput. Especially Schopenhauer, every sentence is crafted. Every thought is crafted. That guy is a genius.
turns.zh: 我现在喜欢做的一件事是，比如我找到了一些叔本华和塞内加的作品，他们有很好的有声书和录音，但他们是密度很高的演讲者和作者。所以如果你听他们的东西，你会错过很多，因为那是写出来的，所以是用来读的。这就是为什么我发过那条推文：听书而不是读书，就像喝蔬菜而不是吃蔬菜，对吧？更高效，但你得到的是某种糖分快感。你没有按它本来的方式摄取。你没有得到所有好处，但总比没有好，对吧？比完全不消费要好。所以我一直在听很多通常以文字形式存在的作者的作品。我不得不接受的一件事是：你知道吗，我只能得到大约 20% 的吞吐量。尤其是叔本华，每个句子都是精心雕琢的。每个想法都是精心雕琢的。那家伙是个天才。

turns.en: And so when you listen to him at spoken speed, I'm like, wait, stop. Oh, I missed that. Oh, what was that? What was he referring to? Oh, I got lost in a daydream. So I'm constantly pausing it. But then I finally had to give up. I was just like, you know, I'm just going to listen to what I'm going to listen to. I'm going to absorb what I'm going to absorb and I'll just go back and listen to it again later, because it was that good. And what I've realized is even when reading you have the same issue. You read a paragraph, you'll be lucky if you pick up a sentence and it might be a different sentence every time, but the good books have the property that there's not much wasted space. All the sentences are useful at some point to somebody. And so I really value that in an author, and Schopenhauer has it in spades. I can go back and read the same essay 10 times over five years and I'll get something different out of it every time.
turns.zh: 所以当你以口语速度听他时，我会想：等等，停。哦，我错过了。哦，那是什么？他指的是什么？哦，我走神了。所以我一直在暂停。但后来我最终放弃了。我就想：你知道吗，我就听我能听到的，吸收我能吸收的，以后我再回来重听，因为它太好了。我意识到，即使是阅读也有同样的问题。你读一段，能抓住一个句子就算幸运了，而且每次可能是不同的句子。但好书有一个特性：没有太多浪费的空间。所有句子在某个时候对某个人都是有用的。所以我非常看重作者这一点，叔本华在这方面尤其突出。我可以在五年内把同一篇文章读十遍，每次都能得到不同的东西。

turns.en: Yeah. You've got that: read the best hundred books over and over and over. There's no point in reading the rest.
turns.zh: 是的。你有那句话：把最好的 100 本书读了一遍又一遍。读其他的没有意义。

turns.en: Yeah. The only books I would want to read are the timeless ones about human nature, with people who are incredibly intelligent, or very modern recent stuff where there's a fast-moving field where I want to know what's on the cutting edge and I know that knowledge will be obsolete shortly. But the thing I can't do is read contemporary history or sociology. There are these very smart people who seem to get a lot of value out of reading a lot of history, and I've read some history, but I don't need to read the details of the Roman Empire.
turns.zh: 是的。我只想读那些关于人性的永恒之作，作者极其聪明；或者非常现代、快速发展的领域里的前沿知识，我知道这些知识很快就会过时。但我做不到的是读当代历史或社会学。有些非常聪明的人似乎从大量阅读历史中获得了很大价值，我也读过一些历史，但我不需要读罗马帝国的细节。

turns.en: I love Will Durant.
turns.zh: 我喜欢威尔·杜兰特。

turns.en: I love Lessons of History. I'm not going to read the whole Story of Civilization.
turns.zh: 我喜欢《历史的教训》。我不会去读《文明的故事》全套。

turns.en: Yeah. Story of Civilization, all 12 volumes. It's like too much detail.
turns.zh: 是的。《文明的故事》全套 12 卷。细节太多了。

turns.en: His other books, like Fallen Leaves and Heroes of History, the really condensed stuff.
turns.zh: 他的其他书，比如《落叶》和《历史英雄》，那些真正浓缩的东西。

turns.en: Yeah, Fallen Leaves is kind of sad. I couldn't make it through it. I started it and it was just like he was like, 'Oh, I'm getting old and this sucks and what was the point of all this?' That's what the book is basically saying. They cast him in a light that I didn't want to see him in. Maybe it gets better later, but I couldn't finish it. And by the way, even the Lessons of History, if all you read was Lessons of History, that would not work. You need to have read enough specific history that you can see, oh yeah, I've seen this pattern before.
turns.zh: 是的，《落叶》有点悲伤。我读不下去。我开始读，感觉他就像在说：‘哦，我老了，这很糟糕，这一切有什么意义？’这本书基本上就是这个意思。它把我放在一个我不想看到他的角度。也许后面会好起来，但我没读完。顺便说一句，即使是《历史的教训》，如果你只读这一本，那也不行。你需要读过足够多的具体历史，才能看出：哦，是的，我以前见过这个模式。

turns.en: So I've read plenty of history in my youth. So when I read the lessons of history, I be like, I know what he's referring to and I have a rough sense, but I'm not going to, you know, I don't have time to read 18 volumes on Genghis Khan now. Like it's too late for that. I'll read one volume on Elon because it's highly relevant to my current world as well. But the lessons of Genghis Khan have to be distilled down much more at this point. We live in the TikTok generation.
turns.zh: 我年轻时读过很多历史。所以当我读历史教训时，我就想，我知道他在说什么，我有个大概的了解，但我不会，你知道，我现在没时间读 18 卷关于成吉思汗的书。已经太晚了。我会读一本关于埃隆的书，因为它与我的当前世界高度相关。但成吉思汗的教训现在必须被大大浓缩。我们生活在 TikTok 一代。

turns.en: Yeah, I imagine your opportunity cost of book selection, like just paying very careful attention to what you're feeding yourself.
turns.zh: 是的，我想你选书的机会成本很高，就像非常注意你给自己输入什么。

turns.en: Yeah. And I think it's actually pretty easy. I think you just pick up a book and you just glance through it and you'll know. There's no obligation to finish a book or turn a page or even read in sequence or in order or any of the sort. Buffett has a good line. It's like you can read the book, but you can't tell anyone about it. You can't tell anyone you read it.
turns.zh: 是的。而且我认为这其实很容易。我觉得你只要拿起一本书，随便翻翻，你就会知道。没有义务要读完一本书，或者翻一页，甚至按顺序读。巴菲特有句名言：你可以读这本书，但不能告诉任何人。你不能告诉任何人你读过。

turns.en: Yeah, that's a sign of a good book.
turns.zh: 是的，那是一本好书的标志。

turns.en: Something I found myself wondering a couple times as I refresh on all these ideas. Is everything best pursued indirectly?
turns.zh: 当我重新审视所有这些想法时，我几次发现自己想知道：是否一切都最好间接追求？

turns.en: I've seen, you know, wealth is best pursued by exploring your genuine curiosity. Learning is best done by reading whatever you're interested in. Even status is best pursued by serving others. Happiness is elusive if it's pursued too directly. Yeah, it may not be that everything is best pursued indirectly, but the things that seem very elusive are that way because they're best pursued indirectly, right? The stuff we have figured out, we don't talk about because we can pursue it directly. But the stuff that seems elusive is because it's best pursued indirectly. And there are different reasons for different things. In the case of wealth, it's because it's a competitive game. So someone who's really into what they're doing will always out compete someone who's not into it. So that's where part of the indirectness comes from.
turns.zh: 我见过，你知道，财富最好通过探索你真正的好奇心来追求。学习最好通过阅读你感兴趣的东西来进行。甚至地位最好通过服务他人来获得。幸福如果太直接追求就会难以捉摸。是的，可能并非一切都最好间接追求，但那些看似难以捉摸的东西之所以如此，是因为它们最好间接追求，对吧？我们已经弄清楚的东西，我们不会谈论，因为我们可以直接追求。但那些看似难以捉摸的东西，是因为它们最好间接追求。而且不同的事情有不同的原因。就财富而言，因为它是一个竞争游戏。所以真正热爱自己工作的人总会胜过不热爱的人。这就是间接性的一部分来源。

turns.en: And then if you're doing something purely selfish, you won't get the best people on your side. Like for example, if you look at like porn or gambling or crypto, like especially the casino is part of crypto, you don't get the best teams. You don't get good teams because the best people don't want to do that stuff. They don't want to work with you. So now you're in an industry where you just have, sorry if I call them out, but low-quality people working alongside you. And then you're just gonna have a lower quality of life and you're gonna have people who are less ethical and kind of like you know more hedonistic. In the case of happiness, you can't pursue it directly because the direct pursuit of pleasure causes addiction and that leads to unhappiness that causes dopamine burnout. So the direct pursuit of that simply doesn't work. It burns out the receptor, you know, that you're trying to modulate in a good way over the others.
turns.zh: 而且如果你做纯粹自私的事情，你就不会得到最好的人站在你这边。比如，如果你看看色情、赌博或加密货币，尤其是加密货币中的赌场部分，你不会得到最好的团队。你不会得到好的团队，因为最好的人不想做那些事。他们不想和你合作。所以你现在处于一个行业，你身边只有，抱歉我这么说，低质量的人。然后你的生活质量就会更低，你会遇到更不道德、更享乐主义的人。就幸福而言，你不能直接追求它，因为直接追求快乐会导致成瘾，进而导致不快乐，引起多巴胺耗竭。所以直接追求根本行不通。它会烧坏你试图以良好方式调节的受体。

turns.en: Status is best pursued by curving and supporting others.
turns.zh: 地位最好通过曲线和支持他人来追求。

turns.en: Yeah. Status can't be pursued directly because status is hierarchy and it's a hidden hierarchy. Like it's very hard to tell who what status someone is. They have to glee it out. And saying that you're high status is low status because if you're high status, you don't have to say it. Everyone knows it. And so directly pursuing status is a low status is a status lowering activity. So that's why people try to be ironic or clever when they put people down. You know, they try to bring people down by saying, "Oh, he oh, Elon's a Nazi. He's evil. You know, he he did the salute." I'm like, "No, you're a midwit. You're an idiot. You should watch the video and see how many so-called salutes people do all day long when they're waving their arms around. So all you've proven is you're an idiot." But what they're trying to do is they're trying to bring him down to status. And so they're trying to elevate their own status. They're trying to pursue indirectly. So a lot of what we see as bad behavior from journalists or on Twitter or modern side with fake news is just the pursuit of status in a covert way.
turns.zh: 是的。地位不能直接追求，因为地位是等级制度，而且是一种隐藏的等级制度。很难判断一个人的地位如何。他们必须自己推断出来。而说自己地位高是低地位的表现，因为如果你地位高，你不需要说出来。每个人都知道。所以直接追求地位是一种降低地位的活动。这就是为什么人们试图在贬低别人时表现得讽刺或聪明。你知道，他们试图通过说“哦，埃隆是纳粹。他是邪恶的。你知道，他做了那个敬礼”来贬低别人。我说，“不，你是个中等智商的人。你是个白痴。你应该看看视频，看看人们整天挥舞手臂时做了多少次所谓的敬礼。所以你只证明了你是个白痴。”但他们试图做的是降低他的地位。所以他们试图提升自己的地位。他们试图间接追求。所以我们看到的很多来自记者或 Twitter 或现代假新闻的坏行为，只是以一种隐蔽的方式追求地位。

turns.en: And love, you don't want to pursue directly receiving love because love is needy and it's actually not the thing you want. It's giving love is the thing that you want. You can manufacture love anytime you want. I actually envy, you know, quotes not really envy but I there's a class of person who's just very loving, you know, and can give genuine love without talking about it. The people who are saying "oh love love love" they use the word a lot, they're pursuing status, right? It's just another way to pursue status. But the people who are genuinely capable of feeling and giving love to large quantities of people, like that's a form of an enlightened person. And they themselves always seem pretty happy. When's the last time you saw a person who's extremely loving but miserable?
turns.zh: 而爱，你不想直接追求接受爱，因为爱是匮乏的，实际上不是你想要的。给予爱才是你想要的。你可以随时制造爱。我实际上羡慕，你知道，不是真的羡慕，但有一类人非常有爱心，能够给予真诚的爱而不谈论它。那些说“哦，爱爱爱”的人，他们经常用这个词，他们在追求地位，对吧？这只是另一种追求地位的方式。但那些真正能够感受并给予大量人爱的人，那是一种开悟的人。他们自己似乎总是很幸福。你最后一次看到一个非常有爱心但痛苦的人是什么时候？

turns.en: Is this related to Deutsch's fun criterion? It's not a good brand, but it's...
turns.zh: 这与多伊奇的乐趣标准有关吗？这不是一个好品牌，但……

turns.en: Oh, yeah. No, Deutsch has an independent version of the same principle. He calls it the fun criterion, which is like if you're not having fun, don't do it.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。不，多伊奇有一个独立版本的相同原则。他称之为乐趣标准，就像如果你不开心，就不要做。

turns.en: Now, his is a little more extreme. Like his like no, don't teach, you know, this is part of the taking children seriously thing. Like don't teach your kids math if they don't enjoy it. I'm like, "God damn it, my kids are going to be mathematically innumerate and then too old to pick it up later." So, I'm not sure I follow it the whole way, but I get what he's saying and I believe there's huge merit to it.
turns.zh: 现在，他的观点更极端一些。比如，不，不要教，你知道，这是认真对待孩子的一部分。比如，如果你的孩子不喜欢数学，就不要教他们。我想，“天哪，我的孩子会变成数学文盲，然后太老而学不会。”所以，我不确定我完全同意，但我理解他的意思，我相信它有巨大的价值。

turns.en: Yeah. He calls it the fun criterion. If you're not having fun, don't do it. And I think he's right. Like, the older I get, the more I do things that are more fun, the more genuine it is. I'm not learning any less. If anything, I'm learning more. It's just I don't beat myself up anymore about not knowing about things that I should know about. And instead what happens is I end up learning the things that I'm genuinely interested in and then I learn more. I learn faster. Actually this is related to you know I've started doing startups again and I'm doing a very difficult company right now. The name of the company is The Impossible Computer Company.
turns.zh: 是的。他称之为乐趣标准。如果你不开心，就不要做。我认为他是对的。随着年龄增长，我越做有趣的事情，就越真实。我并没有学得更少。相反，我学得更多。我只是不再因为不知道应该知道的事情而自责。相反，我最终学到了我真正感兴趣的东西，然后学得更多、更快。实际上，这与我再次开始创业有关，我现在正在做一家非常困难的公司。公司名字是“不可能计算机公司”。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: It's really hard. What we're trying to do is really hard. It might be impossible. And literally the name of the company is Impossible Inc. But you know we have ImpossibleComputer.com. So what we're doing is basically impossible. I talked to four people last week, technical experts, each in their domain, a little bit about what we're doing. All four of them told me it was impossible or four told me it was impossible for four completely different reasons, right? So, it's not an easy task.
turns.zh: 这真的很难。我们试图做的事情非常困难。可能是不可能的。而且公司的名字就是 Impossible Inc.。但我们有 ImpossibleComputer.com。所以我们做的事情基本上是不可能的。上周我和四个人谈过，他们都是各自领域的技术专家，稍微谈了一下我们在做什么。四个人都告诉我这是不可能的，或者四个人告诉我这是不可能的，原因完全不同，对吧？所以，这不是一个容易的任务。

turns.en: But I'll tell you, I've never learned faster in my life because I'm so curious about it. Like, every time something comes up, I'm like, "All right, let me pull up the paper. All right, ChatGPT, fire up the O3 Pro engines, you know, fire up Gemini 2.5 Pro. Let's go. Teach me this. Teach me this." No, no, explain like I'm five. No, no, back up. What about this? I call my co-founder. I'm like, "We're doing a whiteboard session. I feel like an idiot. What is this? What do you mean? You didn't tell me this. Oh, you take it for granted. No, this is the hardest part, you know?" So, I'm learning so much, but because I'm motivated. I'm motivated to go out and learn. I'm a little motivated. I want the answer to be a good answer, but I'm not truly motivated by the answer. I want to navigate my way through the idea maze, you know, the bism of the idea maze and figure out the right answer. But the concrete act of trying to do something and trying to build something is driving the learning curve. If I didn't have that very concrete activity, then I would not be learning at anywhere near the same rate. And that motivation has been really important. It's really accelerated my learning curve. Actually, I'm not drinking caffeine partially and I'm kind of back on more of a health kick because I want to be alert and awake all the time so I can just stay up with the company. I can keep up with it.
turns.zh: 但我要告诉你，我这辈子从没学得这么快过，因为我对此充满好奇。每次遇到新东西，我就会说：“好，让我翻出论文。好，ChatGPT，启动 O3 Pro 引擎，再启动 Gemini 2.5 Pro。来吧，教我这个。教我这个。”不，不，用五岁小孩能懂的方式解释。不，不，退一步。那这个呢？我打电话给我的联合创始人，我说：“我们来做一次白板会议。我感觉自己像个白痴。这是什么？你什么意思？你没告诉我这个。哦，你觉得这是理所当然的。不，这是最难的部分，你知道吗？”所以我学到了很多，但这是因为我有动力。我有动力去学习。我有点动力。我想要一个好的答案，但我并不是真正被答案本身驱动。我想在想法迷宫中找到自己的路，弄清楚正确的答案。但真正尝试去做某事、去构建某物的具体行动，推动了我的学习曲线。如果没有那个非常具体的活动，我的学习速度会慢得多。那种动力非常重要，它极大地加速了我的学习曲线。实际上，我不喝咖啡因，部分原因是我又开始了健康生活，因为我希望时刻保持警觉和清醒，这样我就能跟上公司的节奏。

turns.en: Well, and you're additionally motivated, I'm sure, by building something that has never been built before.
turns.zh: 嗯，而且我相信，你还有额外的动力，那就是在建造从未有人建造过的东西。

turns.en: Exactly. And I'm pouring—right now we're burning a million and a half dollars a month of my money.
turns.zh: 完全正确。而且我正在投入——现在我们每个月烧掉我一百五十万美元。

turns.en: So, that's an additional layer of motivation.
turns.zh: 所以，这又多了一层动力。

turns.en: Use motivation, too. Yeah. Exactly.
turns.zh: 也是利用动力。是的，完全正确。

turns.en: Yeah. You're learning very fast when you've got—
turns.zh: 是啊。当你有了——你学得很快。

turns.en: What are we spending money on? What is that half million dollar check you just wrote? Oh, okay. Damn it.
turns.zh: 我们在花钱买什么？你刚签的那张五十万美元的支票是什么？哦，好吧。该死。

turns.en: So, you got a big carrot and a big stick and that's a good recipe for learning.
turns.zh: 所以，你既有大胡萝卜又有大棒，这是学习的好配方。

turns.en: Highly motivating. Yeah. Yeah. Well, I was very inspired by Elon on this. I think I—you know the story where he said he made $200 million from the sale of PayPal and he put $100 million into SpaceX and $80 million into Tesla and $20 million into SolarCity, to borrow money for rent.
turns.zh: 非常激励人心。是的。是的。嗯，我在这方面深受埃隆启发。我想我——你知道那个故事，他说他从出售 PayPal 中赚了 2 亿美元，然后把 1 亿美元投入 SpaceX，8000 万投入特斯拉，2000 万投入 SolarCity，还要借钱付房租。

turns.en: I don't know if that's true but it's a great lie. Very inspirational. So I'm like, well if you can do that I can make a bet too. Well, and that story even oversimplifies it a little because it happened iteratively, which for anybody who's on a burning platform knows like that's way more painful. Yes. Um, than a one time.
turns.zh: 我不知道这是不是真的，但这是个很棒的谎言。非常鼓舞人心。所以我想，好吧，如果你能做到，我也可以赌一把。嗯，而且那个故事甚至有点过于简化了，因为它是迭代发生的，对于任何身处燃烧平台的人来说，都知道那要痛苦得多。是的。嗯，比一次性更痛苦。

turns.en: So, he's sitting there with $200 million and he puts a hundred down and says that should be plenty to start these companies and get them there. And then they're going to die. They both look like they're on the verge of death. And he has to write checks for all of his remaining money, including his house.
turns.zh: 所以，他手里有 2 亿美元，他投了 1 亿，说这应该足够创办这些公司并让它们成功。然后它们就要死了。两家公司看起来都濒临死亡。他不得不把他剩下的所有钱都写进支票，包括他的房子。

turns.en: Yeah. Like at the same time functionally to keep them both alive by a shoestring, by a thread, and they both lived.
turns.zh: 是的。就像同时用一根细绳、一根线勉强让它们都活着，而它们都活了下来。

turns.en: Yeah. I don't know how real these stories are but they're good man. Fred Smith has one, you know, the founder of FedEx, of how like he had to go gamble the last money in Vegas to make payroll and he did.
turns.zh: 是的。我不知道这些故事有多真实，但它们很棒。弗雷德·史密斯也有一个，你知道，联邦快递的创始人，他不得不去拉斯维加斯赌掉最后的钱来发工资，他做到了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Or Steve Jobs, you know, he poured a lot of his wealth post-Apple. He didn't have that much because the original Apple wasn't worth that much, but he sank it into Pixar, into NeXT.
turns.zh: 或者史蒂夫·乔布斯，你知道，他在离开苹果后投入了大量财富。他并没有那么多钱，因为最初的苹果并不值那么多，但他把钱投进了皮克斯、NeXT。

turns.en: Yeah. Um, because those were all self-financed. Ross Perot, interestingly, was a big backer of NeXT after Jobs. But, you know, he almost lost it there. So, it's not that you have to risk the money to make it. It's more just that you have to have the courage of your convictions when nobody else does. You have to make it from zero to one. You know, the space between zero and one is infinite. To make that leap requires everything.
turns.zh: 是的。嗯，因为那些都是自筹资金的。有趣的是，罗斯·佩罗是乔布斯之后 NeXT 的重要支持者。但是，你知道，他差点在那里输光。所以，并不是说你必须冒险才能成功。更多的是，当别人都没有勇气时，你必须坚持自己的信念。你必须从零到一。你知道，零到一之间的空间是无限的。要实现那个飞跃，需要付出一切。

turns.en: Well, it's a good use of capital. You know what? What else are you going to do with the earnings than put them into a mission that you believe in that's doing something that's unprecedented?
turns.zh: 嗯，这是对资本的良好运用。你知道吗？除了把收益投入你相信的、在做前所未有之事的使命中，你还能做什么呢？

turns.en: That could buy a really big house and look down on everybody.
turns.zh: 那可以买一栋大房子，俯视所有人。

turns.en: Yeah. I think this is—I mean that's part of why Elon I think gets so much respect is he put everything on the line.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为这就是——我的意思是，这就是为什么我认为埃隆受到如此多尊重的原因之一，他把一切都押上了。

turns.en: I mean the guy will sleep in a tent on a log cabin, you know, for the Starship launch. And whatever motives people can ascribe to Elon, it's—you can ascribe, if you want to be negative, you can ascribe an ego motive, but you can't ascribe a selfishness motive. Like he's not trying to hoard it.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，这家伙会睡在帐篷里的小木屋里，你知道，为了星舰发射。无论人们能给埃隆赋予什么动机，如果你想要负面，你可以赋予自我动机，但你不能赋予自私动机。他并不是想囤积财富。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Um, he took too much risk.
turns.zh: 嗯，他承担了太多风险。

turns.en: Yeah. World's richest man was a byproduct, not a goal for him.
turns.zh: 是的。世界首富对他来说是副产品，而不是目标。

turns.en: But because in similar to what you're doing, he is easily overlooked that he did the pre-seed and the seed and the A and the B for both Tesla and SpaceX basically himself, right?
turns.zh: 但类似于你正在做的事情，人们很容易忽略，他基本上自己为特斯拉和 SpaceX 做了种子轮、A 轮和 B 轮融资，对吧？

turns.en: So not only founder but biggest funder. I got two capstone tweets here. Either create wealth or passive income or become a monk or do what you love more than money. What remains is taming the mind and the body, seeking truth, creating love and art. The world has nothing to offer you and you are free.
turns.zh: 所以不仅是创始人，还是最大的出资人。我这里有两条总结性推文。要么创造财富或被动收入，要么成为僧侣，要么做你比金钱更热爱的事。剩下的就是驯服身心，寻求真理，创造爱和艺术。世界无法给你任何东西，你是自由的。

turns.en: That's a big checklist goals.
turns.zh: 那是一个很大的目标清单。

turns.en: The next one's even longer.
turns.zh: 下一条更长。

turns.en: Uh we'll go through that one again. Either create wealth or a passive income or become a monk or do something you love more than money.
turns.zh: 呃，我们再过一遍那条。要么创造财富或被动收入，要么成为僧侣，要么做你比金钱更热爱的事。

turns.en: Okay, so that first line is my definition of retirement. Like if you're doing something you love or if you're a monk or if you have a passive income or if you have wealth now, you can retire—like you're basically done.
turns.zh: 好的，所以第一行是我对退休的定义。比如，如果你在做你热爱的事，或者你是僧侣，或者你有被动收入，或者你现在有财富，你就可以退休了——基本上你就完成了。

turns.en: So retire and then what remains is taming the mind and the body, seeking truth, creating love and art.
turns.zh: 所以退休后，剩下的就是驯服身心，寻求真理，创造爱和艺术。

turns.en: Yep. So that goes back to truth, love, and beauty, right? Or my favorite tweet of mine is, you know, a fit body, a calm mind, a house full of love. Like these things cannot be bought. They must be earned. And that's again puts the same themes back in wealth, happiness, health, and love in a calm mind.
turns.zh: 是的。所以这又回到了真、爱、美，对吧？或者我最喜欢的一条推文是，你知道，健康的身体、平静的心灵、充满爱的家。这些东西无法用钱买到，必须靠努力赢得。这又把同样的主题带回了财富、幸福、健康，以及平静心灵中的爱。

turns.en: Yeah. So knowing that, you know, anything that you do to achieve any of those has to leave you in position to get those things that can't be bought.
turns.zh: 是的。所以知道这一点，你为达成其中任何一项所做的任何事情，都必须让你处于能够获得那些无法用钱买到的东西的位置。

turns.en: Right. Right. And then yeah. Then there's the long one.
turns.zh: 对。对。然后是的。还有那条长的。

turns.en: Well, the world has nothing to offer you.
turns.zh: 嗯，世界无法给你任何东西。

turns.en: The world has nothing to offer.
turns.zh: 世界无法提供任何东西。

turns.en: Yeah. Exactly. Now, you know, an enlightened person would say, well, I just found out I don't exist, so the world has nothing to offer me because the world doesn't exist. Like it's all just one big consensual conscious hallucination.
turns.zh: 是的。完全正确。现在，你知道，一个开悟的人会说，好吧，我刚发现我不存在，所以世界无法给我任何东西，因为世界不存在。就像它只是一个巨大的共识性意识幻觉。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Right. So that's an easier path.
turns.zh: 对。所以那是一条更容易的路。

transcript.sec: Desiring Everything: Wealth, Enlightenment, and Balance

turns.en: I guess I want to find happiness and spiritual enlightenment and all those things, but I'm not willing to give up my world for it. I'm a greedy one, right? I might be the greediest person alive because I want to be Elon Musk. I want to be a successful entrepreneur. I want to be Teal. I want to be a successful investor. I want a great household life. I want to be healthy as long as I can. I don't mind being famous. But I also want enlightenment. I'm not willing to give that up. So to me, the people who are aspiring to just be wealthy and successful but not happy or enlightened, they're not greedy enough. Maybe they need to be a little more selfish, a little more greedy. An amazing life is 70-80% in each of those categories without going 150 on one and zero in any other.
turns.zh: 我想找到幸福和精神启迪之类的东西，但我不愿意为此放弃我的世俗生活。我是个贪婪的人，对吧？我可能是世上最贪婪的人，因为我想成为埃隆·马斯克，我想成为成功的创业者，我想成为蒂尔，我想成为成功的投资者，我想要美好的家庭生活，我想尽可能保持健康，我不介意出名。但我也想要启迪，我不愿意放弃它。所以对我来说，那些只追求财富和成功却不追求幸福或启迪的人，他们还不够贪婪。也许他们需要更自私一点，更贪婪一点。一个精彩的人生是在每个类别都达到 70-80%，而不是在一个上做到 150 而在其他上为零。

turns.en: Yeah, you get above the threshold on each of them. I don't think it's a coincidence that in the Buddha story, Buddha was a prince, or if you read all the old enlightenment stories and the Ashtavakra Gita, it's always the king or the prince who becomes enlightened. It's rarely the peasant, because they were smart enough to realize the poor man has to dig the fields, figure out how to feed himself. He doesn't have the luxury of sitting around and contemplating and saying 'Who am I?' So in the tradition, either you give up everything right at the beginning and become an ascetic, or you start as a prince and realize it's all worthless. The hard one would be having the energy and drive to become a prince and then be willing to give it all up. That's a big hero's journey, as you listed all those things you want to be: Elon Musk, Teal, a great founder, a great investor.
turns.zh: 是的，你在每个方面都超过了门槛。我不认为这是巧合：在佛陀的故事里，佛陀是个王子；或者如果你读所有古老的启迪故事和《阿斯塔瓦克拉之歌》，总是国王或王子获得启迪，很少是农民。因为他们足够聪明，意识到穷人必须耕地，想办法养活自己，没有闲情逸致坐下来沉思并问“我是谁？”所以在传统中，要么你一开始就放弃一切成为苦行者，要么你从王子开始然后意识到一切皆空。困难的是有精力和动力成为王子，然后愿意放弃一切。那是一个伟大的英雄之旅，就像你列出的那些你想成为的角色：埃隆·马斯克、蒂尔、伟大的创始人、伟大的投资者。

turns.en: Yeah, I have to temper my expectations here because I'm old, so I don't have the energy level of a 20-year-old. I do have to bound my desires accordingly.
turns.zh: 是的，我在这里必须降低期望，因为我老了，没有 20 岁年轻人的精力。我必须相应地限制我的欲望。

turns.en: Well, you've achieved a lot of them already. I'm curious, can you hold those desires in a very gentle way where they don't harm you? Have you found a balance with still striving, still hoping but not?
turns.zh: 嗯，你已经实现了其中很多。我很好奇，你能以一种非常温和的方式持有这些欲望而不伤害自己吗？你是否找到了平衡，仍然在努力、仍然在希望，但又不过度？

turns.en: Yeah, I think you can. It can definitely be easier than all the people who are in therapy or doing drugs or mental wrecks or feel attacked. But I was that person too. I think some of it is just aging. But a lot of it is introspection and self-reflection, realizing that this all goes to zero anyway. The line in Christianity: shrouds have no pockets. You don't take anything with you. It is all just a grand adventure. A friend of mine has a good phrase: 'endgame content.' Like in Mario games, you play through all the levels, chase Peach and Bowser, and at the end you're done. Then they give you a level with all gold and toads and mushrooms, you just run around and jump, the stakes are very low. It's entertaining. It's endgame content. So I feel like I'm in endgame content where I have nothing to lose. I might lose some money, a little reputation. I don't care about that. I'm fine falling on my face in public. I get to play without fear, and that means I can go for the highest goals. The downside is a VC might say, 'What do you mean you're playing without fear? I want you up all night, fearful, working hard.' But I'm not sure that's the only way to build something. I don't think Steve Jobs was coming from a place of fear. I don't even think Elon was coming from a place of fear. So I think it's better to operate out of inspiration. This means I can try to do something that's really difficult and worthwhile.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为可以。这肯定比那些接受治疗、吸毒、精神崩溃或感到被攻击的人容易。但我曾经也是那样的人。我认为部分原因是年龄增长，但很多是内省和自我反思，意识到这一切最终都会归零。基督教里有句话：寿衣没有口袋。你什么也带不走。这只是一场伟大的冒险。我有个朋友用了一个好词：“终局内容”。就像马里奥游戏里，你玩过所有关卡，追逐桃花公主和库巴，最后通关了。然后他们给你一个全是金币、蘑菇和蟾蜍的关卡，你只是跑来跑去、跳跃，风险很低。这很有趣。这是终局内容。所以我觉得自己处于终局内容中，没什么可失去的。我可能会损失一些钱、一点名声，但我不在乎。我可以在公众面前丢脸。我可以毫无恐惧地玩耍，这意味着我可以追求最高的目标。坏处是，风投可能会说：“你说你毫无恐惧地玩耍？我要你彻夜不眠、充满恐惧、努力工作。”但我不确定这是唯一的建设方式。我不认为史蒂夫·乔布斯是出于恐惧。我甚至不认为埃隆是出于恐惧。所以我认为最好出于灵感行事。这意味着我可以尝试做真正困难且值得的事情。

turns.en: Yeah, there may be that some of those really aspirational projects can only be done from a place like that.
turns.zh: 是的，也许一些真正有抱负的项目只能从那样的状态出发才能完成。

turns.en: I think so. I don't think it's a coincidence that Apple, Tesla, and SpaceX are self-financed companies. Most of the great companies are either self-financed or they break out very early before they really need venture capital. Google broke out; they raised VC just to get John Doerr and Mike Moritz on the board, but they were already at escape velocity. Microsoft as well; they took a tiny check from one investor, but they were already off to the races. Amazon was profitable very quickly. They took a small investment, but none of these giant companies did round after round after round grinding it out through the standard path. The truly largest companies did not do that. They either got profitable very quickly and almost didn't need venture capital, or they were self-financed by a visionary entrepreneur. It's very rare that one of the huge iconic great companies was built by round after round of venture capital.
turns.zh: 我也这么认为。我不认为苹果、特斯拉和 SpaceX 是自筹资金的公司是巧合。大多数伟大的公司要么是自筹资金，要么在真正需要风险投资之前就早早爆发了。谷歌爆发了；他们融资只是为了请约翰·杜尔和迈克·莫里茨加入董事会，但他们已经达到了逃逸速度。微软也一样；他们从一位投资者那里拿了一小笔钱，但已经起步了。亚马逊很快盈利。他们拿了小额投资，但这些巨头公司没有一家是通过一轮又一轮的标准路径磨出来的。真正最大的公司没有这样做。它们要么很快盈利，几乎不需要风险投资，要么由有远见的企业家自筹资金。很少有巨大的标志性伟大公司是通过一轮又一轮的风险投资建立起来的。

turns.en: That's a heresy.
turns.zh: 这真是异端邪说。

turns.en: There you go. I don't have a lot of respect for investors. I don't disrespect them; I think they're an important part of the ecosystem and I'm a capitalist, but I don't think they need to pat themselves on the back.
turns.zh: 就是这样。我对投资者没有太多敬意。我不是不尊重他们；我认为他们是生态系统的重要组成部分，我是资本家，但我不认为他们需要自我表扬。

turns.en: Do you think wanting some things are mutually incompatible?
turns.zh: 你认为想要某些东西是相互排斥的吗？

turns.en: I mean, they have to be, right? The biblical phrase: easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to get into heaven. What I interpret that as is if you're pursuing material things, you can't pursue spiritual things. You can't both be successful in this material life and striving for it, living the life of a merchant, and also be a Buddha. So they might be mutually contradictory. I was up late one night stretching and I thought, 'I should just make sure stretching is always part of my routine.'
turns.zh: 我的意思是，它们必须是相互排斥的，对吧？圣经里说：骆驼穿过针眼比富人进天堂还容易。我的理解是，如果你追求物质，就不能追求精神。你不可能既在物质生活中成功并为之奋斗，过着商人的生活，同时又成为佛陀。所以它们可能是相互矛盾的。有一天晚上我熬夜拉伸，心想：“我应该确保拉伸总是我日常的一部分。”

turns.en: I was like what is my routine? What am I trying to do here? So then I just typed that out and then I tweeted that out and I got one of the highest compliments I got a tweet which is this guy I think M. Mayor he said oh you wrote you wrote the book.
turns.zh: 我当时想，我的日常是什么？我在这里想做什么？于是我就把它打出来，然后发了一条推文，我得到了最高的赞美之一，一个叫 M. Mayor 的人说，哦，你写了一本书。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: You put a book in a tweet with this one. And I've seen so many people replicate this, so it's clearly kind of resonant.
turns.zh: 你用这条推文写了一本书。我看到很多人复制它，所以它显然引起了共鸣。

turns.en: Yeah, there was a mistake in there, though, which kind of sucks. There was one thing that I'll swap out at the end.
turns.zh: 是的，不过里面有一个错误，有点糟糕。最后有一件事我会换掉。

turns.en: All right, correct me when I get here. Fast, lift, sprint, stretch, and meditate build.
turns.zh: 好的，等我到这里时纠正我。禁食、举重、冲刺、拉伸和冥想构建。

turns.en: So, hold on. Fast, lift, sprint, stretch, meditate. So, that's all the physical stuff.
turns.zh: 等等。禁食、举重、冲刺、拉伸、冥想。所以，这都是身体方面的事情。

turns.en: Yeah. Kind of the morning routines, you know, daily workout thing. And I like that it's first because you're starting with health as your first priority.
turns.zh: 是的。有点像晨间例行公事，你知道，日常锻炼之类的东西。我喜欢它排在第一位，因为你把健康作为首要任务。

turns.en: That's right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Build, sell, write, create, invest, and own.
turns.zh: 构建、销售、写作、创造、投资和拥有。

turns.en: Yeah. So, this is the making money the wealth part. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。所以，这是赚钱、财富的部分。是的。

turns.en: Productize yourself. Build or buy equity in a business. Read, reflect, love, seek truth, and ignore society.
turns.zh: 产品化你自己。建立或购买企业的股权。阅读、反思、爱、寻求真理，并忽略社会。

turns.en: Truth and love along with a healthy dose of selfishness.
turns.zh: 真理和爱，再加上一点健康的自私。

turns.en: Make these habits. Say no to everything else.
turns.zh: 养成这些习惯。对其他一切说不。

turns.en: Yeah. Habits. Because that's the only way you stick with them.
turns.zh: 是的。习惯。因为这是你坚持它们的唯一方式。

turns.en: Avoid debt, jail, addiction, disgrace, shortcuts, and media.
turns.zh: 避免债务、监狱、成瘾、耻辱、捷径和媒体。

turns.en: Right? So, I replace one part in here, which is I think I replaced shortcuts with sugar or something else. It's actually in the tweet. I have a followup on that tweet saying, "Oh, I wish I replaced." I have to look it up, but I think that's what I wanted to replace. And my favorite part is actually the code here, which is relax. Victory is assured.
turns.zh: 对吧？所以我替换了这里的一部分，我想我把“捷径”换成了“糖”或其他东西。实际上在推文里。我有一条后续推文说：“哦，我希望我替换了。”我得查一下，但我想那就是我想替换的。我最喜欢的部分实际上是这里的代码，就是放松。胜利是必然的。

turns.en: That's right. Relax. That's the gently down the stream part.
turns.zh: 没错。放松。那就是顺流而下的部分。

turns.en: I'd say exactly what I was thinking. Do it merrily and go gently.
turns.zh: 这正是我的想法。快乐地去做，温柔地前行。

turns.en: Exactly. Yeah.
turns.zh: 正是。是的。

turns.en: Thank you.
turns.zh: 谢谢。

turns.en: Thank you. Scrape.
turns.zh: 谢谢。刮掉。
